title: Dolester Miles’s Lemon Meringue Tart Recipe
author: Kim Severson

comments.commentBody: How is it possible to have so many ratings and no notes?  I'm highly suspicious.  I gave the recipe 5 stars because it is nearly identical to one I've baked previously, except for the white chocolate, which could only add more yum.  Way to go Ms. Miles!!
If you would have had the privilege of tasting this in person, you would truly know.  It’s a thing of beauty.

Pricking the crust to allow air to escape keeps the crust from sliding down the sides of the pan when blind baking. I always blind bake this way. Works better than pie weights. Don’t forget to prick the sides, too. 

Also, Italian merengue is made by beating hot simple syrup into the whites as you whip them up. Both Swiss and Italian make a creamier merengue. More like a marshmallow, not airy like American merengue.

The "airy" meringue you mention is actually called French meringue, in which the egg whites are not cooked, and which therefore requires baking after being spread onto the pie. I agree it is not a delicious meringue, and I would probably make this tart without any meringue (because I love lemon by itself). 

Swiss Meringue is called "7-minute frosting" here in the US and it is pretty billowy and marshmallowy.

Turned out great for a first go at lemon meringue.
The white chocolate made it too sweet for my taste (prefer more tang), so will leave out this ingredient next time.

The crust for this tart is very good; however, the whole thing is far too sweet, tooth-achingly sweet. If I ever make this recipe again, I’ll reduce the sugar, say, by half or more so it isn’t so candy-like.

As I thought it would, baking the pastry blind without weighing down  WILL make the dough shrink in the pre-baking even if you freeze the pie shell as instructed before baking.
Luckily I had some pastry scraps leftover and attached them to the half shrunken shell.  Then covered the pastry case with parchment and filled to the brim with sugar to weigh it down.  I keep a tin of sugar on hand to use for this purpose and you can use it over and over again.

If you prick it all over with a fork before you freeze it, say 30 or so stabs, it won’t shrink or bubble up. Weighing it down reduces the flakiness and you get a different texture. Plus less browning.

I've never seen the suggestion of using sugar for the weight in a blind bake! Think this is brilliant.  We need a chapter on the blind bake.  Pricking the crust all over never worked for me, mine always shrank.
I could not get the crust ingredients to stick together. So, I add a tablespoon of cider vinegar and a tablespoon of water.  Worked out fine.

I find it a little off putting that the meringue ingredients are listed with the filling as opposed to having them listed under a separate "meringue" list of ingredients. I mistakenly added all the sugar to the filling ingredients.  After all the tart is comprised of three components: crust, filling and meringue.
I guess that's what happens when you try to cook and travel at the same time!

Wow, yum. However, the dough was a nightmare to work with, and I'd recommend running the machine to get it to come together or else you'll be pulsing to kingdom come. Also let it sit out at room temperature for a few minutes before rolling. We loved the addition of white chocolate in the lemon filling (genius), but that flavor got lost with the ocean of meringue on top, so maybe only apply a thin layer of it instead of the whole amount. Overall, though, fantastic!

I would agree with others that this is far too sweet as written. I halved the sugar in the crust (to 1/3 cup) -- and really pies are fine with unsweetened crusts too.  I also halved the sugar in the curd (to 1/4 cup) and still found it plenty  sweet with the white chocolate. Finally, I reduced the sugar in the meringue to just over 1 tbsp per egg white (I beat all the egg whites, as you end up with extras from the crust).  I also added a couple of tbsp of vodka to the crust dough.
A note in the ingredients list that the sugar for the meringue and filling is to be divided would be helpful for those of us baking with little kids and other life distractions

2 questions for bakers more professional than I am.
What are the dolids that need to be strained out of the lemon mixture?  The lemon zest?
Also, I’ve never heard of heating the whites and sugar before mixing to make the meringue. What does that do for you?

Sometimes when you make the lemon custard you get little thick bits of cooked egg, so straining will make sure it's perfectly smooth.  Plus, the zest.

Heating the whites and sugar makes a more stable, smoother and denser meringue than simply beating the whites and adding the sugar when nearly stiff.  Since the meringue in this tart isn't baked, it's also probably safer to have heated the whites before beating them.
Heating the whites and sugar is a Swiss meringue - or Italian, I never remember the difference.  But I always use it as it holds up better and has a shine to it.

To Joanna: Swiss Meringue is when you cook the egg whites and sugar together to 170 deg, beating all the time, and then whipping the mixture until cool. Italian Meringue is when you cook the sugar syrup and add it to partially-whipped egg whites and beating until cool. Both produce a fluffy, stable and cooked meringue (that you can also use to make buttercream, by adding room temperature butter to the cooled and whipped meringue).
Can you freeze this?

The only part of this that you could freeze without sacrifice is the crust. Even then you should bake the crust straight fro the freezer into the preheated oven.  You could freeze & thaw the baked crust and let it come to room temp or rewarm it slightly before filling it.  The filling will not hold up to freeze/thaw.
Meyer lemons are ripe so time to make lemon tarts
This was delicious!!! Don’t need a fancy stand mixer or a torch. I made this no problem with the hand blender and the beater attachments and broiling it at the end. So tasty!

Way too much work. This is a lot of steps and bother for what you get.  there are way easier recipes for lemon tart on line.  I think the white chocolate is too sweet in it.  I made it for my husbands birthday because he loves lemon meringue pie and we were disappointed.
Put the pie under the broiler for a full minute before it turned golden brown and reduced the sugar in the meringue to 1/2 cup. Otherwise the pie would have been too sweet

Great recipe, what caught my eye was the genius of using white chocolate to achieve a smooth and silky lemon custard which allows to reduce the amount of egg yolks and avoid using gelatin, cream or condensed milk

Some comments talk about the sugar quantities on the crust, custard and meringue.  Those can easily be adjusted up or down depending every person taste

Be aware that the crust thickens when baked
What I'm curious about is pre-baking the crust without weighing it down.  Will it not shrink because it's a sweet cookie dough like crust or because it was frozen for a period of time.
Because it was frozen.

The recipe says to prick the crust with a fork.  These small holes will vent steam.  As a former professional pie maker, it is my experience that if you prick your empty pie shell with a fork before baking, you do not need pie weights.
What is the approx volume of lemon curd this recipe makes? Want to use up jarred lemon curd and adjust recipe accordingly. Thanks for all comments.
The meringue was sublime and the lemon tart filling was truly tasty. But the crust was a disappointment.
This was awful. overly sweet and never set properly

This tart is so lemony and wonderful.  I made it for a dinner party and everyone loved it.  I will definitely make it again.  I didn't brown the meringue because I didn't have a kitchen torch, but otherwise made it exactly per the recipe.  Not super time intensive + no hard to find ingredients = very likely that I will make it with some frequency.

This was a tough sell. I used jarred curd instead of fresh, but the flavor was fine- although I put in the curd cold, it still went liquid in the oven, so I guess the extra-long setting time would be worth it; or a kitchen torch instead of broiling. The crust needed to be laid out thinner than I rolled it; but my only real issue with the recipe itself was the meringue. We followed the directions exactly and got incredibly flat meringue, no ridging at all. The pie was wicked sweet. What the heck?

Make the lemon curd yourself!  You’ll get fresher flavor and you can modify the sweetness to your taste.  But this is a sweet dessert, no question about it. 

I like to add some of the lemon juice and zest at the last minute to preserve the fresh taste.

Made this for a friend’s dinner party and everyone loved it! I couldn’t get the dough to come together, so I ended up needing to add water to get it to stick. Everything else turned out great and will definitely make again!

I made this using a different crust recipe. Essentially the same dry ingredients but a whole egg instead of egg yolks. The filling is delicious and the meringue makes it impressive. I halved the sugar content of the curd upon recommendation from the notes. I took this to a Friendsgiving party. Everyone ate so much of the “real food” that they barely had room for dessert, leaving me with half a tart in leftover in my fridge. Not a bad problem to have!
Much too sweet!
To prevent getting thick clumps of egg, you can whisk the custard on a double boiler like the meringue. Keep the heat low-medium and don’t stop whisking until the mixture is thick. About 10 minutes.
And strain it when cooked.
Absolutely amazing recipe. My dad raves about it every time he has it. My go-to forever now!

I liked this a lot. Next time I will roll the dough thinner, I watched guests trying to cut the crust with a fork and it wasn’t easy.  For the crust I added a little water and about 1/2tsp vodka - I read somewhere that it makes it easier to roll out. I used only about half of the meringue and broiled it about 3 hours before we ate and it was fine, no weeping. Very lemony, nice contrast.  Will make again.

If I made this again I would make a regular butter pastry. Based on the pan size suggested I would also double the lemon curd. It was a very thin layer of curd compared to the amount of meringue. The curd and meringue were tasty. I just thought the amounts were off.

I just baked this tart, and it is delicious! I found that I had extra Dough and was able to make two small tartlets in addition to the main tart. I doubled the filling, and had plenty to fill both the main end extra tarts. I do think the filling requires about 1 tablespoon extra lemon juice, but I do like a really tart curd especially with the very sweet meringue topping.  To make it extra fancy pull out a gallon Ziploc bag, or piping bag, and pipe the meringue in pretty dots along the top.

Made this as mini tarts in a muffin tin and they turned out amazing! If you decide to do that as well, adjusting the tart bake and meringue broil times is a good idea (a little less, but eyeball it).
- used less sugar in the lemon filling and meringue, still turned out great and even a little too sweet still (will use a little less in the meringue next time) 
- probs would make more lemon filling in the future, but thats because im a fan of the tart!

But WOW, it takes time, but this is AMAZING
Absolutely delicious! Have tried many lemon meringue tart recipes and this is by far the best one. The best sweet crust in terms of flavor and texture and easy to work with.

made this with a gluten free crust and dairy free butter (miyoko unsalted) and it was still lovely! recommend more of a pastry crust rather than shortcrust if you're doing gluten free, as gf can be crumblier and more challenging to work with

Delicious.  The addition of white chocolate to the lemon filling is really wonderful.  The Swiss meringue is perfect, although I recommend weighing the egg whites (100 grams).  The Swiss meringue did not weep even after two days of refrigeration.  Made this recipe twice (once as one large tart and once as 6 small tartlets) and both times to rave reviews.  Making it again next week for a lemon lovers birthday.  This is my new go to Lemon Meringue Tart recipe.
News Year's Eve dessert, huge success!  I would increase the amount of meringue next time, but otherwise perfect!  I agree that the white chocolate is genius.  I did up the zest, figured why not?
The lemon mixture for this is one of the best things I have ever cooked, but the meringue tastes like a marshmallow, far too sweet for me. Next time I will do it with a drier, less sweet meringue.

I made this with a shortbread crust in a rectangular tart pan and lightly browned the meringue in the broiler. I used callebaut white chocolate callets and heated the egg whites to 160 Fahrenheit in the double boiler before whipping in the mixer. Looks impressive!

Delicious result! The crust dough was a little difficult to work with. It took longer to bake than 20 minutes and cracked, but the final product is crisp and buttery so it must be very forgiving. The filling is perfect and the cooked meringue is worth doing as it is stable, doesn’t weep, and survives travel and refrigeration.

I know this is a stupid question but would you rather eat a lemon meringue tart or lemon meringue pie? I am familiar with my mother's lemon meringue pie. But it may be easier to make with a tart crust instead of a pie crust. Do you have a preference?

That isn’t a stupid question at all! In principle, a tart shell should be a bit stronger, since it’s served out of the pan, while a pie shell, cut in the pan, can be crumblier, that is, more tender. In practice, I usually use Dorie Greenspan’s Rich Sweet Tart Dough for any sweet pie/tart, and ATK’s All Butter Pie Dough for savory pies/tarts. Or whatever the recipe writer recommends. Or whatever I feel like making-Parmesan-mustard crust for appetizer tartlets, semolina dough for rustic tarts/pie!

Like others I had some tearing with the dough but I just patched it up. I baked the crust for 15 minutes with dried beans for pie weights and 5 minutes without to brown it up. The end results were delicious and the tart was easy to slice without crumbling.

The cooking times on this website…. 
45 minutes plus chilling? Took me the whole afternoon. Be realistic. Making a pie crust, a filling and a topping will never take only 45 minutes (not counting the chilling times).
Made this tonight, it was sheer perfection! I lowered the amount of sugar in the curd by about 1/4, it balanced out nicely with the sweetness of the white chocolate. I cannot wait to make it again!

A fussy recipe that was just ok. The addition of white chocolate broke in the custard, adding it off heat. Custard was too thick to push through fine mesh sieve so I poured the mess into the shell and hoped for the best. The amount of custard was not enough in my opinion.  Finished tart was good but not worth the effort. Will go back to an old  lemon meringue pie recipe next time.

This got rave reviews at the party I brought it to. However, a few notes: the dough (for me) did not form a cohesive mass in the food processor. However, I was able to press it together into a disc on the countertop. It cracked readily during the rolling out process but was easily patched with excess scraps. I didn't find the filling overly sweet -- I'm curious if others might have accidentally used white candy melts rather than bar white chocolate.

I agree with others who thought this quite sweet. After making the filling, I added a tablespoon or so to make it a bit more tart. Also, better explanation on the desired thickness of the filling would have been helpful. Often other recipe sites, like King Arthur, will describe thickness in terms of creating a ribbon or thickly coating the back of a spoon. Finally, I made the crust as written with the addition of a tablespoon of vodka. I had no trouble rolling it out and the was crust to spare.

I just don’t feel like the recipe makes enough curd! Have made it 2x now & each time disappointed with the amount of curd. 

Otherwise - delicious & beautiful!
Made it as directed and everyone LOVED it.. The white chocolate adds a richer flavor.

title: Green Tomato Salad Recipe
author: Kim Severson

comments.commentBody: Are these unripe tomatoes or tomatoes that are green?
Unripe, which are firmer and more acidic than any kind/color of ripened tomatoes.
Same questions; are they unripe or tomatoes that are green when ripe?
I took it to mean actual green tomatoes, not unripe ones.

Great fall salad. No cucumbers at home, so added daikon radish and the crispness goes very well. Accompanied by a creamy Greek feta cheese and sardines from a tin. Together with sautéed mushrooms and a bit of egg (recipe by Martha Clark) with a toasty warm pita and dry white wine, a lovely dinner!

This seems odd ) I’ve not prepared it yet) because contrary to all ‘normal’ prep it’s the cucumber that gets ‘sweated’ - not the tomatoes...is this on purpose? Or some mix/up .. I’m no expert in Green Tomatoes ( live NCarolina so no shortage down here where they’re mostly BATTERED ‘n’ FRIED-eek) but why is this being called for ?

As CJ said, the initial text strongly indicates unripe (and it's a pretty pointless recipe if it's just ripe tomatoes). Also, the photo shows some of the tomatoes barely turning pink, and most green-when-ripe tomatoes never turn pink at all. (I recommend against pink--tomatoes just turning pink are very different from fully unripe, which are much more tart.)

Edna Lewis said all there was to say about fried green tomatoes and she would have loved this salad. The key is to make sure that the tomatoes are as hard as you can find- ignore color. Drain the chopped cukes with the salted tomats.  Serve in a black Chamba bowl from Columbia if you are lucky to have one.
Made as directed and added feta as one person recommended and the zest as well as the juice of the lime.  It was delicious and refreshing and yes I am using my fall green tomatoes!
Green, unripe tomatoes. Make a similar salad in the late fall just before frost to use up the last of the unripe tomatoes on the vine.

Can substitute about 1 tbsp rice wine vinegar in place of lime juice, and about half the amount of olive oil.
It also can be made the day before, and the peppers are a bit less spicy.

This recipe calls for too much olive oil and too many onions so lighten up on those. I would use half of the amount called for. I actually made this with red tomatoes because I didn’t have green. It still was really good!
Good way to use up green tomatoes. Would consider as a salad topping for greens. Could halve the oil. Not too spicy with 2 small jalapeños. Subbed a shallot for onion and cilantro for mint.

We had a ton of green tomatoes in our garden as we were pulling the plants out for fall so I made this to accompany Sheet Pan Chicken with Sweet Potatoes and Peppers. Both the fresh, bright flavors and the pretty green color of the salad complimented that dish perfectly. A great thing to do if you have a plethora of green tomatoes and don't want to fry them!
Nicely balanced with great flavor and crunch. I used two serranos, everything else exactly as written besides halving olive oil, and my husband and I loved it.

I had 2 green tomatoes. Skipped Step 1. Combined tomatoes with: a handful of tomatillos, entire cuke, 1/2 onion, salt, fresh parsley, dried mint, lime juice and EVOO. Made it like this twice and cannot get enough of it!
Has anyone made this a head of time?  If so, what were the results?
I wanted a recipe for using green tomatoes that wasn’t fried. This was easy and delicious.
Delicious! A well balanced and tasty salad. An excellent use for green tomatoes from the garden .
this was an esp delicious salad.  I used mostly green but also some that were sort of half ripe.  Very nice.
Whoops, the yummy mushroom recipe is Seared Wild Mushrooms by Martha Rose Shulman. This salad goes well after the mushrooms.

This was good, but I liked it with more salt and more lime juice. I added a small green pepper from the garden, which added more crunch. I cut the tomatoes and the cucumber on the mandoline, which made a very pretty presentation.

I disagree. I believe she’s referring to green heirloom varieties such as green zebra or Cherokee green. (Not unripe red tomatoes.) Which is why she gives a thumbs up to subbing with red tomatoes. And also because she mentions they are hard to find. No one sells unripe tomatoes. My local farmers market has the green tomatoes she’s referring to, and they are tangy and bright. Yellow tomatoes would be good too. Unripe tomatoes tend to be mealy because the texture hasn’t matured.
Use only 1/2 a small only and cut to the thinest possible with mandolin and some fresh cut dill

I was glad to find a good recipe for the unripened tomatoes left in my garden. I did have to add more lime than the recipe called for and I also added a pinch of sugar and some ground cumin for a bit more flavor. Served this with some homemade beet falafel and flatbreads. Had a great texture. I will definitely make it again.

A nice way to use green tomatoes from the garden at the end of the season. I followed the recipe as printed, but after a few bites, I added some herbs of Provence, which made it all the better. Any good sweet aromatic herb mix will do. Next time I plan to try cilantro. Thinly sliced green pepper or celery could also be added, all of which I also plan to try with my remaining tomato orphans.
A,n, yes, feta! Definitely feta!

title: Creamy Ramp Pesto Pasta Recipe
author: Kim Severson

comments.commentBody: Did anyone else have to find out what ramps are?
Yes.  Probably many of us.  In fact I am about to Google it right now!
Ramps are wild garlic.  They look like chives.
Ramps are a variety of wild onion which are among the first green plants to come up in spring. They grow throughout Appalachia at the edge of wooded areas.
I live in s. Ca and I never see ramps in the mkt.  what can I use as a substitute?
You can sometime find them at the Hollywood or Atwater Farmer's Market in Los Angeles in early Spring.

I never see ramps in markets either.  But we do forage sometimes.  I would think the tender parts of garlic scapes would be a good substitute.  Or for something quite a bit milder, garlic chives.  I think even scallions would work in a pinch.  None of these will be a perfect equivalent, but cooks like me aren't afraid to wing it a bit.  If it's too mild, add a little fresh, chopped garlic and process a little more.
To CJ McD. Ramps are anything but mild. Smell stays with you for days.

Shortcut: don't separate the stem from the leaves until AFTER you blanch the leaves. Just hold the bulbs and drunk the leaves in the boiling water, hold, and then pull them out after 15 second. The recipe says to only use a quarter of the bulbs but I used all of them. To compensate for the increased flavor of the ramps, I used about one and a half lemons/lemon zest, and a full cup of ricotta. This dish is amazing! Lick the bowl delicious!
Couldn’t find ramps so substituted 3 leeks and 2 garlic cloves instead. Turned out great! Surely a different flavor than with ramps but an easy way to make this a year round recipe.

You can make a batch of the ramp pesto and freeze it in an ice tray, then transfer cubes to a plastic freezer bag. Each cube is about a tablespoon. This is a delicious and lighter pesto, and since ramps are hard to come by, it’s nice to have a supply.
This looks delicious, but why do you cook the pasta one minute less than recommended?  Usually recipes that cook the pasta for a shorter time then cook it more in the sauce, but this one does not.

We have ramps now in Seattle, but really, the season is just a few weeks long. Ramps (wild leeks) are foraged in wooded areas in the mid-west and eastern states. I've heard of people trying to cultivate them, but with little success.

Adding ricotta or mascarpone to this otherwise delicious pesto dilutes the wonderful tangy taste of the ramps too much.  Ramps are rare and hard to find for most folks.  Don’t waste them by adding creamy cheese.  The pistachios and the pasta temper the pungency of the ramps.  Blanching the ramps is also completely unnecessary.
I made this in a mortar and pestle and added some cooked shrimp to the pasta at the end. Worked great!

Yikes!  Ramps are a slow-growing, native plant that are uncommon in nature.  They are often illegally harvested from public lands.  If you pull up the bulb, the plant is finished.  Try using just the leaves.  Always make sure the source of the ramps is legitimate.

There is no real substitute for Ramps.  They are a wild onion that grows in northern climes.  SOOOOo yummy!  First thing that appears in late April in our Famer's Market here in Madison, WI.  Thanks for the great recipe!

Ramps are a Northeastern wild onion.  We don't see them here in the Midwest.  Spring onions are close, not sure if you can find them in the Missouri woods, our spring has advanced too far for that at this point.  Wild garlic, if you can find it, or chives maybe a month ago could have been found Ozark meadows.  Oak woods raise up a lot of interesting onion like wild plants.

Ramps grow in the Appalachian Mountains also. There are at least 3 separate Ramp Festivals in Western NC and eastern TN at this time of year. The festivals serve sauteed ramps in scrambled eggs and/or fry them with bacon in breakfast potatoes. I make them also with angel hair pasta, savory bread pudding and spread on pizza with morels - which are harvested in the same season.
Can I just substitute green onions? I can find them at my store... thanks.

This pesto has become an annual favorite. It pairs wonderfully with grilled sausages or pork chops and/or balsamic-marinated cherry tomatoes. 
I find the quantity here quite heavy for one pound of bucatini (actually it’s more than enough for two pounds!).  Thankfully, the pesto freezes beautifully, so we can extend our ramp season into the summer!

This is my favorite recipe for ramps, which grow abundantly in the woods near our house. The creaminess of the ricotta balances the sharp flavor of the ramps, the lemon brightens it up. I used less lemon and more bulbs.

Using this recipe as a roadmap, I bought 7 bunches of ramps. Blanched the leaves leaving the bulbs attached, dried them with dish towels, then chopped off the bulbs and sautéed them in olive oil. Zested 6 lemons and squeezed out the juice. Toasted about 2 cups of pine nuts. Then used the Cuisinart to grate about half a pound of Parmesan cheese, added the bulbs, leaves, lemon zest and the lemon juice. Added all the leaves in batches, added salt, pepper & olive oil to get the right consistency.

Ramps are very common in Austria so I was happy to have more recipes on how to use them. I do think though that the taste of the Ricotta kind of overpowered all the other ingredients and I liked the pesto better without it (or at least with less Ricotta). I also blanched all the Ramps and didn't separate them in stems etc. which worked fine.
Apart from that, really liked it!

If you forage ramps, as I do, good advice to take them sparingly each year to ensure there are enough to divide and come back. You don't need a lot for a good pesto. Make a batch, divide among small jars and freeze. When you thaw, add a little olive oil to top and store in the 'fridge. We do ramp pesto pasta without the ricotta, tossed with angel hair pasta and fried thin chicken cutlets plus a green salad. Delicious every time.
Use guanicale, add chopped pistachios to pesto and on top with parmesan, rigatoni

I thought that this was very good. I didn't feel the need to blanch the ramps, so I just skipped that part. To everyone saying "Why mask the flavor of the ramps with the ricotta?"--I say to you that this will be pretty rampy regardless, and my breath this morning goes to show it. I did go ahead and toss in all the bulbs, though.

I've never eaten ramps (or heard of them until now). My google research described them as a mixture of onion and garlic in flavor, so perhaps leeks and garlic are a substitute if you don't live in a ramp growing region.
Luckily I live where ramps are found growing wild or sold at local farmer's markets.  They're only seen for a few weeks of the year.  This recipe turned out great, what a wonderful way to use ramps.
This will be a future classic. Made it as described and it was totally perfect. Ended up using half the bulbs and stems. Served with scallops on the side. Heavenly!
This is a winner! This is my favorite of the various ramp recipes I’ve tried. The ricotta and lemon mellow out the sharpness of the ramps.

This was so good, I might make it again tonight!  Made more-or-less as written, subbing pine nuts for pistachios and ricotta salata for plain ricotta.  Probably used a lot fewer ramps, too, but I find a few ramps go a long way.
Run don’t walk. Make this whenever you get Ramos. Easy, crazy delicious and fresh!
If you find yourself with extra stems/bulbs, pickle them!
A little perplexed with the minute less than al dente too: while it was quite good, pasta a bit tough.
This was absolutely amazing! Made the sauce to go w homemade pasta and use homemade ricotta and it it a literal dream. Will absolutely be coming back to this whenever I get my hands on some ramps!
I didn’t think I had enough ramps so I added three green scallions with the leaves and the bulb and it came out great.

This is delicious!!! But... double the sauce recipe. Like others, I too found that I needed to use most of the bulbs and stems. Taste the pesto as you go and adjust... you’ll find a balance of flavor that is nirvana... then ecstasy when you combine it with the ricotta and pasta water. The ground PINK pepper is a key to the dish.  Pair with a Pinot Gris... and this become a 3 Michelin star meal in 30 minutes.

Ramps are called “wild garlic” in the UK. I harvested some leave and stems in a forest in Somerset his spring, so no bulbs. Children and adults loved this pesto. Made some without cheese too for a vegan, and it was still yummy; I just added a bit of olive oil, pasta water, and some nutritional yeast and extra salt. Both versions were quite lemony though, and maybe over shadowed the wild garlic a bit. Will make again next spring!

I made this pesto without the ricotta. Used some extra spinach I needed to use up and with some pasta water added it was saucy and rich enough as is. We topped with shrimp and red pepper flakes instead of peppercorns. Fabulous.

For a ramp education, check out the 84th annual Feast of the Rampson in Richwood, West Virginia, April 22, 2023 https://www.richwoodchamberofcommerce.org/feast-of-the-ramson#:~:text=FEAST%20OF%20THE%20RAMSON%20Saturday%2C%20April%2022%2C%202023,Community%20Center%20%28formerly%20Armory%20at%20the%20City%20Park%29

Ramps are in season in the Maryland and West Virginia area, probably Pennsylvania too right now. I was taught by my grandmother (who grew up here) that they’re wild leeks but they’re probably really wild garlic. I make pesto, or broil them or pickle them or roast them or sauté them. Wish I could send a picture. They’re delicious.  I don’t use them as leeks or garlic substitutes.
Holy moley! The sauce is delicious!
Made last night with marscapone and fresh ramps. It was so good and my family loved it! Going back to the farmers market next week to make more to freeze!
I'm allergic to pistachios, so I made this with walnuts instead. It was incredibly delicious.

It's ramp time in VT.  I use a steamer to blanch the ramps.  If I'm going to freeze, I wing them with oil only, then pull to add to recipes all winter.  I will try this full recipe tonight for dinner with a side of grilled sausages.  Off to Rampsylvania!   Best, Alice
This pesto was so much better the next day that I would make it ahead next time. I added shrimp for a bit of protein and served with asparagus (which, like the ramps, came in our CSA box).

I couldn't find the pink peppercorns, so I skipped that part. It would also be helpful to write in the exact amount of lemon juice. I had a BIG lemon and it was too much. I went back and added more parmesan to help balance it. I also substituted pine nuts for the pistachios. I mistakenly bought bufala mozzarella instead of the ricotta, but it still turned out delicious!
Just made the pesto...elite. Very punchy
Very nice and quick.Used basil pesto instead.

I have never liked ramp pesto because the taste is so strong one breathes garlic for weeks! By tempering with cheese and especially lemon it’s a lighter and yummier sauce. The water with cheese scared me by was fully absorbed and finished cooking the pasta.  This is great recipe!
Absolutely fantastic. I wanted to find a great pasta recipe with ramps (fav season of the year) and this delivered! It was fun to make, really quite easy, and tasted amazing. 10 stars.

@TLG: I hear you, but if you use ALL of the bulbs and stems, you won't have any left over for pickling! Can I be bold and say that a pickled ramp in a martini, with a drop or two of pickling brine, is cocktail heaven? Ramps take seven years to grow, and ramp season only lasts for three weeks. Man the Mason jars! To the pickling stations!
Tried with and without ricotta. Better with the ricotta.

title: Lamb Chops Scottadito With Crispy Kale Recipe
author: Kim Severson

stats.Total Time: About 40 minutes, plus at least 4 hours' marinating

comments.commentBody: If the grill is very hot, I suggest cooking just 11/2 minutes per side, or the lamb, 1/4 inch thick,  will be very well done
I agree, a minute and a half is plenty for thin chops

Why grapeseed oil?  It has a similar smoke point as canola oil which also has minimal to no taste.  And grapeseed oil costs almost twice as much as canola oil.  I see the recipe calls for "or a similar" oil but I still question what is a clear recommendation for an unnecessarily expensive product that adds nothing to the recipe.
It’s about using a natural product versus a gmo.
Everyone has their favorites and grapeseed is just one option. I often see canola oil described as flavorless or “neutral”, but to me it has a distinctly fishy taste.
I'd use Coconut oil instead. It's great for high heat cooking, it's good for you and if you're near a Trader Joe's, you can get a jar of it for $5.
A total of six or seven minutes for 1/4-inch-thick chops? They'll be pretty well done, no?

I made these almost as instructed, with the exception that I cooked them under a gas broiler. 3 to 4 minutes on each side. I also only had three hours to marinate them. But they were still delishimo!

Do be sure to grill the lemon slices.  I did this in a cast-iron skillet on the stove top while the chops were broiling. They add a terrific acidity and brightness to the lamb.
Any substitute for Kale? It is bitter, difficult to chew, and downright disgusting!
No, no, no. Kale, when cooked as suggested, becomes crisp. My grandkids love it. I’m not kidding. Try it
Are you using the right kale? She specifically calls for Lacinato aka Tuscian aka dinosaur kale, which isn't as tough as regular curly kale.
I totally agree....

Skipped the kale, but followed the directions for broiling and the dinner was tasty. Served with flat pan potatoes and figs from a separate NYT recipe and side order of broccoli to make it easy. 
Great fall dinner.
The lamb was Devine.  I wasn't crazy about the crispy kale under it but if I do it again, I would remove the entire stem, too hard to chew.
Has anyone cooked these?  Are the comments made on experience or just commenting on the recipe!

I eat them in Italy and they don’t add:
Grapeseed/anchovies/capers/lemon/garlic or sage. 
They grill them with salt and pepper. Nice and simple, Italians don’t like cover up taste flavors.
I wonder if you could grill the kale. That way you wouldn’t have to heat up the oven in the summer, when you’re likely to be preparing this dish.
I made this as directed and my boyfriend threw up. I liked it though. Would make again, just maybe not with him.

Loved! Best lamb chops we've made in a while. Pounding does the trick, as does high heat on the grill. My husband was unclear re: if the meat stays on the bone during the pounding - it does. I only marinated for a couple of hours (still yummy), and I forwent the kale b/c didn't want to deal. Delish regardless.
Double the kale for sure

title: Green Peach Salad With Simple Lime Dressing Recipe
author: Kim Severson

comments.commentBody: This is an excellent little recipe. I added a pinch of chile powder and half a habanero. You can use whatever vegetable oil you like, but it works very well with avocado. Important to let it sit before serving!

A bright little summer salad that gets better as it sits. Don’t skip the serranos; the sweet, spicy and sour flavors play great with cream cheese or on top of plain Greek yogurt. Would probably be perfect on a bagel too, now that I think about it. Yeah, this one’s a winner.
Used jalapeño and about half the oil. Excellent

Great recipe especially when peaches aren’t quite ripe. Made in the morning for dinner and the lime really brought out the peach flavor.  Chile haters in the house - fresh mint added a nice freshness.   Served as a side to pork and was great.  Will definitely make again.
Also good with nectarines.

This was so tasty. I made with white unripe, which are not as strong flavored. I used jalapeño v Serrano to be more crowd friendly. I also added shredded goat cheese and sliced almonds for make it more of a filling side dish.
The too much oil compliants are poor process, the entire point is to create an emulsion. If you can't whisk to emulsion you may need a stick blender or Vitamix style to create it properly

Used jalapeño and about half the oil. Excellent

Add the pinch of chili pepper to taste

added shredded goat cheese and sliced almonds for make it more of a filling side dish

A little more zippiness is warranted. A little extra lime, a fresh chili, chili powder are all good suggestions.
Made it by mistake without the oil- it was delicious!
@KMM thank you for the mint suggestion. The mint completes the recipe!
Too much oil. Add the pinch of chili pepper to taste. I did mine overnight.
So tasty and refreshing.  I used nectarines and a good olive oil.
Bring salad
This was skippable in my opinion... and way too much oil if you’re going to make it.
My diners were surprised and delighted in the unexpected flavors of this dish. A lovely summer salad.

This is delicious. The full quantity of oil works well here. A little more zippiness is warranted. A little extra lime, a fresh chili, chili powder are all good suggestions. Make with an additional peach or two and definitely a day ahead.
this is very, very good! I liked it better when first made - later it was weirdly slimy/still tasty though.
I followed the recipe and used two seeded serranos. Wonderful on a hot summer evening; like a seviche of peaches.
Make sure to use very unripe peaches, and let them sit. I liked it, Sage thought it was decent, and Mom said that she doesn't like "slimy/spicy/sour peaches".
Kind of disappointing. The dressing overwhelms the great natural flavor of the peaches--even when I increased the proportion of peach.
I added grilled corn into this for a bbq and it was fantastic and super easy!
Could you make it without the oil?

title: Stuffed Ham, Southern Maryland Style Recipe
author: Kim Severson

comments.commentBody:
Doing it yourself is not nearly as daunting as it seems in the recipe. If you live in MD or NC you may be able to find corned hams in grocery stores around the holidays, which makes this dish even easier. NC-based Food Lion carries them here in MD.

Hopefully, this recipe and the associated article will help to spread this wonderful dish. Everyone for whom I've prepared it has instantly fallen in love. Once you taste it, you just know these ingredients were meant to be together.

Thank you !! I will have to order from Food Lion again !! They get only CORNED ham !! I always ask for a 20 pounder and cook it on the stove in a big crab boiler that my parents made theirs in during my growing years !! I miss home But will drive there for the corned ham if need be. Well worth the work and the 4 hour journey !! Thank you for reminding me about Food Lion. Have a wonderful Holiday season !! And Merry Christmas !!
Wow! this is a recipe worth its monumental efforts. In Maine our pork farmers offer plenty of fresh ham legs ready to corn.
In Kentucky the Luckett version (which came from Maryland in the early 1800's) uses salt and air cured country ham.

For the past several years, a group of friends have gathered in Clements, MD on a November weekend and, in loving memory of the mother of one of the groups' members who showed us how it's done, we collectively prepare 30 or so hams. One is prepared the night before which we enjoy after all the work is done. We share the fruits of our labor with friends and keep one (or two!) in the freezer for later use.

The process is a great group task and the rewards are delicious.

Great article but there is no mention of neighboring Charles county.  We also have been making stuffed ham for generations.  It’s funny how many variations of this ham there are.  In Our version, we use much more kale, less cabbage and we also add watercress.  We also add quite a bit more of the listed spices as our family and friends like the ham spicy!  We make a 25 lb ham for Easter every year.  Once wrapped with the greens, it is so large we have to boil it in a big crab steamer!

This was a slave recipe here in Maryland, not an English one that some locals want to say about it, and don't let anyone tell you otherwise. The fact that heat is in this recipe says it all, like most Afro-Caribbean cuisine, English food does not. The slave owners gave the slaves at the holidays the worst part of the pig & African and Caribbean influence from enslaved cooks brought a new twist to European ham, to create something uniquely American.

This fascinating story makes me wish I had lived in Maryland! How wonderful to see an example of a beloved local (and complicated!) dish that has survived the homogenization of American tastes.
Unfortunately it's beyond my abilities (and audience). I'm gearing up to try posole for the first time, and deciphering dried hominy, pork butt vs. pork shoulder, etc. has been challenging enough for me!

Some of my family has been in st. Mary's county since the dove landed.... All I can tell you is kale, and a little mustard greens. Some guy in the comment who said go heavy handed on the spices is absolutely correct. Plenty of spiced kale mostly kale and spices on the ham. You can order one from Hills if you really want to but they're expensive, our Google the country store in Clements Maryland. They sell the original and have the oldest recipe that I know of. 🍻

The appearance of this ham strongly suggests the presence of pink salt in the cure although it is not mentioned in the recipe.
 Comments welcome.

This recipe does not adequately address the "corning" or the ham.  Just soaking the ham in salt water will not produce a lovely pink ham as depicted in the photo.  You need to add some kind of corning agent like saltpeter, which is no long available for purchase on the internet.  It would be useful if the recipe would include some instructions as to how you end up with a pink ham

I use pink curing salt when corning a brisket to get that nice color (1 Tbsp for a 4-5 lb. brisket). I bet that could readily apply here.
I use Anthony's Pink Curing Salt #1.

I made this for the first time for Easter lunch. I was intrigued by the whole process, though while doing it I was often unsure if I was doing it right. My family and I liked the end results, though I wish I had had the authentic southern Maryland version so I could know how close mine was. I imagine those who continue the tradition have more counter space and brute strength than I do, two things the recipe requires.

I attended college in Southern Maryland back in the 80's and found several markets that sell the stuffed ham. You can find them online. It's about $12.99/# but if you're ever in St. Mary's county be sure to visit old St. Mary's City, the site of the original settlement from the 1600's. I still make this for Christmas. My friends here in Michigan love it.
http://www.wjdent.com/stuffed-ham.html These folks do a great job. I had theirs years ago. Very expensive to ship.

Mix the stuffing outside. It creates quite the mess. We also cooked this on a burner outside (one of the ones for brewing beer or frying a turkey). Use a heavier hand on the spices. 

Use sodium nitrite (curing salt #1) if you want to turn the ham pink.

I was taught in St.Mary's County how to stuff a ham years ago and I still do it whenever able. I am going to make one this Christmas time. I enjoy it tho it is a task for me at times due to shoulder surgery last year. But I think I will be up for the task again this year. I live in NC now but would love to just purchace half a corned ham to do the job again next month. My parents and Grandparents are long gone but may have to have my brother grab me a corned ham so I can just drive 3 hrs away.

I lived in Maryland for years and this is what I make every Christmas and in between. But I got tired of always looking for a corned ham, so I started using a regular old shank portion ham and I don't corn it. My family likes it better less salt also ! Just a tip if you use just a regular ham just cut the bone out and freeze it for later use. put a rack in the bottom of pot or a basket "which makes it so much easier to pull your ham out" But add two cartons of vegetable broth and the rest water.
Wouldn't this need sodium nitrate to get the ham pink (as in the picture)? Otherwise, it would be a rather unappetizing gray, I think.

Just alittle idea make a honey pineapple glaze it makes the ham melt in your mouth, save some of the hot glaze to pour over you meat when served  use a gravy boat this is terrific holiday treat ,idea from london ontario Canada 
From kat MacDonald 

Enjoy everyone

Kat, to each their own but curious if you are speaking from experience.  Traditional ham with a pineapple glaze is good but if you have never tried the SMD stuffed version u would know that sweet wouldn't compliment or even go with it.  It would be like topping a bowl of chocolate ice cream with fries

I made this with a corned ham years ago and it turned out well - I wonder though , would this work with a plain fresh ham?  For Thanksgiving we got about 5 pounds from Chapitico market in in St Mary's county but its a heck of a hike down there.  Would like to see how it turns out with an alternative?

title: B.J. Dennis’s Okra Stir-Fry With Rice Recipe
author: Kim Severson

comments.commentBody:
The ingredients list says 1/4 c oil, and the recipe instructions say Pour 1/2 c oil.  

Can frozen okra work in this dish?

It could. Fresh cooks up with a nicer, firmer texture; we steam it a bit and saute it whole in olio to crisp tender alot, or sometimes just saute/steam it. But... if I didn't have fresh and wanted to make this I would not hesitate to use frozen... unthawed (thawed it will get mushier); I have a bag in my freezer as I type this. 

Might be a bit more of a challenge to get it to brown a bit but that is not a deal breaker.

Seems to me the 'operative' term is "to make sure it coats the entire bottom of the pan" so I'd bet 1/4 cup is more than enough - but, as 'both' measurement comments say 'adding/plus' "more if needed".  Don't know if frozen will work but might be worth a try - if it doesn't work, then never do it again ;>) (that's my motto - but sometimes i forget and repeat errors - oh well)
Good Luck and best regards,
Bob Galli
Edison, NJ

In my experience with other recipes, substituting frozen for fresh veggies is hit or miss. The primary issue is the extra amount of water in the frozen veggies -- they tend to cook soggier than when from fresh. I've had the best luck if I thaw and drain the frozen veggies before adding them into the recipe. 

Good luck!

Really enjoyed  and appreciated a vegetarian recipe with an unusual ingredient like okra. Used frozen okra from last summer's garden, which browned up no problem over medium-high heat in a 10" pan. Adding the rice to the other ingredients in an 8" pan would have been tight, but 10" was just right. A generous 1/4 cup oil was adequate. Seeded the peppers. Boiled the rice in veggie stock. for extra flavor. Thank you for sharing this!
Appears the recipe oil measurement discrepancy has been fixed, so we can move ON.... all good.

This is a quick and easy recipe. I used two orange habaneros and the end result was hotter than hell. Definitely recommend going easy on the heat and drizzling the end product with Tabasco instead, though.
the ingredients list says 1/4 cup oil and Step 1 says "Pour 1/2 cup oil" - how much oil?
A variation:  my mother "stretched" fried okra by adding diced red potatoes red potatoes.  In 1950 she used bacon drippings.  Delicious.
I think a larger pan is in order here. An 8" pan would be really crowded. I'll try this recipe in a wok using cold cooked rice (as for fried rice).

We LOVE okra and often eat it whole, sauteed w/garlic and sometimes tomatoes (or in a peanut based stew, or Egyptian style..etc etc.). I love this recipe. Used rice cooked in chicken stock. We had some Carolina rice and although recipe says non-aromatic, aromatic would not kill it, not at all. Added shrimp. Used some Louisiana hot sauce during eating. So darn easy and so darn good. Love it!!
Easy recipe. Added crushed roasted peantuts to each serving.
The list of ingredients calls for 1/4 cup of oil.  The cooking instructions call for 1/2 cup.  Which is correct?

This was really good. Very easy. Would be summer perfect w fresh tomatoes on side. Used brown rice cooked w a veg stock. I say double the okra and add a little more peppers. Used our wok, perfect fit!
Delicious! A great dish for okra. Used a poblano and some shishitos in my cast iron skillet. Next time I'll add some egg, too for a little extra protein.
In Step 1 the recipe only says "the oil" so I imagine that it means the 1/4 cup of oil listed in the ingredients.   I do not see "1/2 cup of oil" that everyone has made a reference to!
Do you remove seeds and chop/dice the hot peppers?
My kind of vegetarian.

I thought this was terrific!  I used about 4 c cooked brown rice, 1 lb of fresh okra, approx 1 cup of diced onion, 1 T minced ginger,  q large clove of minced garlic, 1 large unseeded Serrano pepper and salt and pepper (to taste).  Let it cook a bit longer to get a crust on the rice.  Was a hit - even with my husband who is not an okra fan.
Wow, just when I thought I knew something about okra.  Delicious, the coconut oil really adds to the flavor.  I added leftover roast chicken, so good.
This recipe is superb! Threw in some black eyed peas to add a bit of protein and it came out beautifully.
Made as directed with frozen okra and frozen onions and an aromatic rice (basmati) because that was what was on hand. Cooked extra long to brown the rice. Amazingly delicious!

Delicious! I erred on the safer side with Serranos instead of hotter peppers. Still plenty of heat. I agree with others that an 8” skillet isn’t big enough to nicely cook through and toast the rice (I also ended up with close to a pound of okra), so I used a slightly larger pan. Next time I’ll go even bigger. I love crispy rice! Lastly, I leaned into the fried rice aspect and tossed some scrambled egg at the end. Definitely adding this to my rotation.
Being a Southerner, I made this with bacon grease. We found it to be delicious! Will make again.

I’ve now made this about 10 times. It is addictive! I use less oil and more ginger and pepper. We are an onion allergic household, so that gets skipped. This last time, I added 3/4 c chicken stock after the rice and steamed it 5 minutes, then stir fried it more after. We ate every bite!

I enjoyed this though I'd make a few changes next time. I used a cast iron skillet because it was closest to the 8" recommended, but would definitely choose a larger, non-stick pan next time to help make the rice a bit more crispy. 

I used two bird's eye chilis and personally would have liked more heat, but I guess you never can tell how hot they'll be. Added some extra hot sauce to compensate. 

I'd like to try cooking the aromatics in the oil at the first step to get a bit more flavour in.

I've made this seven or eight times now--in a wok. It's great with shrimp or some quickly blackened fish. It's one of those simple recipes that's somehow greater than the sum of its parts--the ginger, I think, is the transcendent touch.

Makes my mouth water just reading the ingredients. I lived in Lawton Oklahoma as a child, where our neighbors introduced to us okra and all the ways to eat it. We had it just like this minus the peppers, with grits and chicken. Yum!!!
A dish that is greater than the sum of its parts. I threw in some shrimp, as mentioned in the description and let the rice cook until it developed a browned crust. I will make this again.

Hi! Is there a recipe for the chutney mentioned in the article? It sounds good but not sure how to make. Could tahini be substituted for the ground benne seeds? Would this work - a recipe of tahini, cilantro, small chili peppers, garlic and oil...? Also, what kind of oil? Coconut oil? And what is texture of the chutney supposed to be? Any tips or suggestions much appreciated! Thank you!
It says 1/4 cup cooking oil, but more if needed to cover the entire bottom of the pan.
Definitely add some shrimp!
added 2 eggs at the end... not bad

We used frozen okra in this dish and it worked just fine. We cooked it (a little less time than the package directed) before adding the partially-cooked okra to the recipe. We also skipped the habaneros and doused the finished dish with tabasco sauce. Next time we will add halved cherry tomatoes.
This was a hit. I added andallouis sausages for protein
This is the first okra recipe I have truly enjoyed outside of a gumbo. Browning the okra before adding the other ingredients takes the slime away. Now one of my go to rice recipes.
Could you add an egg to this?

We love this recipe.  I have made it several times, once with 2 raw eggs that I scrambled into rice mixture at the last minute, also used leftover fried chicken thighs that I de-boned and diced up.  This recipe is generous to all additions, vegetable or protein.
Use shrimp stock to cook the rice. Sprinkle shrimp with Old Bay before adding them at end of onion cooking time.
Wow! Garden okra and Carolina Gold rice.

Delicious. Worked well with Hatch green chiles (what I had on hand), although I did use a smaller amount. Also subbed about half the rice for frozen riced cauliflower, which I added after the onions but before the actual rice. Tabasco over the top is a nice addition.
I just made this with red quinoa instead of rice.  Very tasty.

This was very good, although we reduced the amount of oil and omitted the hot peppers.
I liked how quickly the okra was cooked frying it in the pan. Very taste.
Delicious.  Used leftover basmati rice.
Delicious as written!!
Add more veggies and maybe honey

This tastes good, is easy, healthy and adaptable. Used green onions, milder spicy peppers, and tossed in a handful of leftover chicken. Everyone was happy. Will be doing this again, especially when people are hungry and no one  wants wants to wait.
Pairs beautifully with pan-friend tofu. Slice, pat dry, sprinkle with salt and garlic powder. Fry and remove from pan and proceed with the recipe

I didn’t grow up eating okra and now living in the south we get it in our CSA all summer. The slime when you get them freaked me out at first and finding recipes with okra that I love have been rare but this one is a WINNER. Added the shrimp as suggested and it was fantastic.
Made this per the recipe, using frozen / thawed okra and crushed red pepper instead of fresh green, as it was all I had. Result was delicious!
I liked this and will make again. I added some shredded chicken breast I happened to have- which was good.
I added a couple of chopped up tomatillos a a Tbsp of “Soul Food” seasoning.  Two habaneros made it way too hot!

title: Vegan Mushroom Étouffée Recipe
author: Kim Severson

comments.commentBody: This was delicious and incredibly simple to make. My carnivorous husband absolutely loved it as well. The only change I made was soaking some kombu in hot veggie stock as a substitute for dulse since I didn't have any. Worked just fine.
This was very good. I added a can of chickpeas to make it a bit more filling.

This recipe is amazingly delicious - it just needs a tweak on the cooking time.  Simmer the sauce for 30 minutes before adding the mushrooms- then simmer 20 minutes more.  It’s perfect! Rich, flavorful, thickened without adding any extra anything.

I used just 2 cups of chicken broth and added a Tbsp of cornstarch at the end, and still found it a bit thin. Full flavored, but slightly soupy. You may want to go even lighter on the liquids if you like a nice thick gravy-like étouffée, as I do.
Mine turned out like a thick gravy, but I think I used plenty of flour in the roux at the beginning ... nice thick, full-fat coconut milk is also a must!  :-)

Delicious and wholesome. I would suggest making a roux in step 1 rather than just tossing oil and flour together (Over med-low heat, gradually combine equal parts oil and flour. Stir as the flour toasts and darkens. Should take 10-20 min depending on how dark you want it-- my mother says it's ready when it looks like a melted Hershey bar).
Added some enoki mushrooms at the end, yum!

I was surprised by how tasty this was. I used a mixture of cremini and shiitake mushrooms and left out the seaweed flakes. I also used a little less liquid than called for, to make for a thicker 'gravy' texture. I would start with 1 1/2 cups, you can always add more if it looks too thick for your taste. The mushrooms ended up, weirdly, with a similar texture to shrimp or crawfish - kind of chewy and meaty, in a delicious way. It's savory, hearty, easy, great.

Great recipe - very flexible, and I would do it again! 

Notes: I subbed green bell peppers for red bell (I don’t love the flavor of green bell peppers).  I also left out the celery, which I didn’t miss.  I subbed parsley with cilantro for the garnish, and forgot to buy Old Bay Seasoning (but I had the Creole Seasoning, which was great!)
Yup, definitely read this as "oysters" comma "mushrooms". After living in Nola for two years, I think it's kosher to throw in oysters any way. Sorry vegetarians - you're missing out.
Not missing out at all. Gaining a lot in terms of health benefits. Look at the studies of plant-based diets.
Some vegans and vegetarians eat bivalves, because they don't have a central nervous system, and they're good for ocean life. So your pity is unnecessary.

This has been a go-to recipe for us for the past four years. My partner recently went on a low-salt diet, so I have been grinding/mixing my own salt-free Old Bay and Creole seasoning. I also added lime juice instead of lemon as my partner thinks it helps with the lack of added salt. I’ve used good quality fresh tomatoes in the past, but like it better with fire-roasted (low-sodium) canned tomatoes. As mentioned by others, use the full pound of mushrooms which is much more than 2 cups!

Love this recipe. I’ve made it a few times now. I’ve found oyster mushrooms hard to come by or quite expensive, white button mushrooms worked just fine to add to them or substitute. I used dried dulse flakes the first time and fresh dulse after that. I like fresh dulse to be better, threw some pieces in at the same time as the bay leaf and it broke up after about 15 min and added some nice umami flavor. I bet kombu (kelp) would work too.
if you, like me, vastly underestimated the amount of mushrooms you had, you can add dried lentils (I added half a cup) to make it more substantial.

I suggest you make this recipe as is, at least the first time. That's pretty much what I did and it was delicious. 

Heeding some of the advice in comments, I simmered the stew for about 20 minutes before adding the mushrooms, then for another 20 minutes. It thickened up nicely.

I did not add any salt. I used a good quality box of vegetable broth and that was enough salt. 

Next time I will add more mushrooms. (I really like mushrooms.) That's all I would change.

This is one my of my favorite vegan/vegetarian dinners! Sometimes I sub butter instead of oil for the roux and instead of a cup of canned diced tomatoes, get a 28 oz. can of San Marzano’s whole and cut them up in the can and sub that instead for better tomato flavor. Doesn’t change the consistency either but makes it more creole

Great recipe! Make sure to look up “how to make a roux” to actually perfect this recipe. Instead of 3 TBSP oil to 3 flour, I used a wooden spoon for measurement and did 1:1 ratio. If you don’t already know, Tony Chachere’s Creole seasoning is the best. If you need more heat to the spices, add chili powder- NOT more Cajun seasoning/Old Bay. It will make it too salty if you do, in my opinion. I also add shrimp to top it off too! Thank you!

SO GOOD!! I will definitely keep this in my winter meal routarion. It’s so easy to make and is so warming during the NY winter. The one thing I may adjust is not adding any additional salt until after it simmers for 20. It was just the tiniest bit too salty for my taste. My partner didn’t think it was, but the man would pour salt on his cereal if he could. Other than that, this is a knock out!
Loved! A pound of oyster mushrooms is definitely more than 2 cups though. Go with the full pound - you won't be sorry. Yum.
Outstanding and low-effort. Increased the oil and flour a bit and cut the broth to 2 cups. Use your best veggie stock, it's worth it. I

I’ve made this several times- delicious. I would say though to use the thyme, paprika, pepper blend instead of a salted creole seasoning in addition to the old bay. The old bay has enough salt to flavor this beautifully. I also add shrimp when I’m not trying to go totally meatless…
Great recipe. Add a tablespoon (or two) of (vegan) butter for added depth.
I used fire-roasted tomatoes and added some corn, which was good!

This was horrid. We had to throw the whole dish away because it was unsalvageable due to the amount of salt. I HIGHLY recommend doing the 1 teaspoon each black pepper, paprika, and dried thyme OR be sure your Creole seasoning has NO salt in it (we used 
Tony Chachere's.) That combined with the Old Bay was far too much.

Really delicious! I couldn’t get Old Bay where I am, so I used Cajun seasoning—ordered Old Bay for next time. Didn’t have enough oyster mushrooms, so used about 1/3 shiitakes. Butter instead of oil and a little extra flour. Added a whole can of San Marzano tomatoes and a little less water than called for (just over two cups).

Used white rice because I was worried my husband wouldn’t be keen on brown, but pearled spelt/farro might be great: extra substance/protein.

Will def make again!
So good. Also added some okra coins at the celery step and it was a nice complement.
This was delicious. To be mindful of possible saltiness, I omitted dulse flakes and went light on using salt. Made creole mix as suggested.
The kids say it’s a little spicy(but ate it all!), though the adults loved it as written.

Wow!  This was amazing. A bounty of oyster mushrooms fell into my market bag and it was great to find this recipe.  I made sure to drain the tomatoes thanks to some notes here - the sauce turned out perfect (you can always thin it a bit if needed).

I made it as directed, and it was a big hit at my dinner party.  If your diners are adventurous, consider adding a small quantity of hot peppers along with the green peppers.  I made 1 1/2 times the quantity in the recipe, and that went well.

Followed recipe closely with the exception of the Old Bay , substituted in Cajun spice which was excellent. Let the sauce simmer for a little longer than called for which helped it get thicker and creamier. Wow, healthy and easy to make. Perfect for a fall dinner and for the vegetarian dietary plans.

This was amazing. We couldn't find dulse powder, but the grocery store sold Kelp powder, which worked just as well (as far as I can tell) and helped impart a little bit of a seafood flavoring to the dish. I added about 1/4 teaspoon of cayenne along with the other spices as well, because we like things a little spicier. This was really delicious.

I love all the vegan recipes the times have been posting, as I’m attempting to switch from vegetarian to fully plant-based.  I’ll be trying this one. I do wish more of the vegan dishes had a bit more protein in them. I’m starting to feel concerned that I’m not getting anywhere near the 50ish grams recommended for adult women.
Try adding some seitan or tempeh to pretty much any dish that would have meat.
I add a cup or cup and a half of beans (whatever I have in the fridge or freezer. This time was Rancho Gordo Vaquero beans which are the best!
Absolutely delicious! Added two cans of no salt red beans, a splash of wine and some sliced Okra. This recipe is now on repeat in our home. Fabulous!

This dish is absolutely perfect. The dulse imparts a wonderful seafood flavor, and the oyster mushrooms have a perfect consistency to match. You’d be forgiven for thinking you’re eating seafood. I sprinkle a little kelp on as I cook instead of salt (you can buy this at most grocery stores; it comes in a shaker like a salt substitute). I add some cayenne. The recipe is very forgiving, a little extra of this, a little less of that — it’s no problem. It stores well and makes great leftovers!
Very tasty but if you want it more gravy like like a real étouffée I added 2 tbs of arrowroot powder (or you could use cornstarch) near the end

This recipe is *heaven*. I’ve made it a bunch of times and it never fails to disappoint. I use fresh tomatoes, usually Campari or grape tomatoes. Buying the dulse flakes is totally worth it. The flavor is outstanding - I bought Cenalga brand on Amazon and have loved them. This last time I made it I made it with cremini/baby bellas because the grocery store only had very sad looking oyster mushrooms and it’s still delicious. Healthy and so flavorful.

This is wonderful! So easy to sub in things you might have around - adding more veggies or sausage (not vegan, obviously, but still great). I increased the amount of Creole and Old Bay seasoning, but it's totally a personal choice (and finish with hot sauce at the table) Even better a day or two later as leftovers. Will definitely make this again!

I am from New Orleans, visiting with family living in the Northeast.  Since two of the families are strict vegetarian, I wanted to represent my home town.  This recipe got rave reviews!  I used the entire liquid amount when I TRIPLED the recipe (hungry men and teenagers) so I will heed the comments of other cooks to cut back on the liquid.  Otherwise, it was a winner.  I think everyone had seconds and maybe thirds.
This is delicious, we're not vegan so subbed chicken stock since that's what we had on hand. This dish has defintely gone into our rotation.
Saute the mushrooms with the onions, peppers, garlic

Fantastic. I used portabellos instead of oyster, smoked paprika instead of regular (I was out), and smoked salt instead of regular (just for fun). Did not add any chicken broth and the mushrooms released plenty of liquid, definitely didn't need more. Maybe oysters release less? Anyway, this will go into our rotation. Oh, and I didn't have dulse, but I didn't miss it.

Used a handful of dried mushrooms, and mixed a lb of fresh king, oyster and seafood mushrooms.

After simmering, i wilted in a large package of fresh baby spinach. 

I think it turned out great
Absolutely wonderful!  Wouldn't change a thing!  Even the one radically committed non-vegan at the table ate seconds!

Every time I make this I forget to read the comments that it comes out very runny and that I have to add corn starch or potato starch to thicken it.  Fantastic recipe - I just need to either use less liquid.  Probably less liquid.
Cut the cups of broth for saucier sauce, don't need 1 tbs of old bay and 1 tbs of Cajun and salt since there's less water in it. Better roux is made w butter but to each their own.
Agreed, but this recipe was specifically made vegan, which means zero butter.

This recipe is fantastic, I’ve already made it twice this week! Yes, there’s probably a lot of salt so next time I might adjust the amount of seasoning but also live a little every now and then. Tony’s   old bay? ::chef’s kiss:: Things I edited: baby bella mushroom cut into chunks were nice and “meaty”, veggie better than boullion, olive oil instead of coconut (either worked great and couldn’t taste much difference), made shrimp to add on top with a sprinkling of green onion. Delightful!

Made this tonight, first time, and it is an immediate "keeper". I can't overstate how satisfying this dish is. I lacked diced tomatoes. Oyster mushrooms are also unavailable where I live. Instead I used a can of fire roasted diced tomatoes, which added flavor, and I replaced the shrooms with about 4 oz of soy curls. Perfect! Next batch I'll keep the hacks mentioned, adding okra as well. Love this dish! As one who lived in Louisiana almost 20 years this étouffée tastes as good as I remember it!

I made this for a dinner party to accommodate a vegan guest. Everyone—including the carnivores—loved it! I made the sauce ahead and added the mushrooms a bit before I was ready to serve. The sauce had thickened considerably in the meantime! I added water. I also replaced 1 cup of the broth with vermouth. Scrumptious!

We found the finished product to be a bit more soupy than the thick texture we associate with an etouffee, even after simmering longer, but by leftovers the next morning it was a great consistency! So we also suggest starting with a less water than the recipe says and adjusting up if needed. Loved the flavors. Tearing the mushrooms by hand, instead of chopping with a knife, gives the long strands and craggly edges of a crab or other crustacean meat, if you are seeking a closer imitation.
The dish turned out very salty for me because of the variance of sodium in creole/cajun seasonings.

I didn't have the seafood dulse, but made it anyway, so missing that "smoky" flavor they mentioned. but I still liked it anyway and made my old bay from scratch(the ingredients of the product are onlime and easy to simulate). it's perfect cause it's easy meal with low time-consuming preparation... and I was looking for some good winter meals as I try to reduce my meat intake but still have good amounts of protein.

Made this again recently after learning the hard way that my husband doesn’t like oyster mushrooms, so I blackened some canned jackfruit (after squeezing out the brine) and it’s still a hit! I also always blackened the mushrooms before putting them in the etoufee as well, delicious
Awesome dish.  No lemon needed
2024-09-13: Two cups of oyster mushrooms is about 8 ounces at the farmer's market

Great recipe for meatless Monday or any day. It’s healthy and delicious. I used whatever mushrooms on hand. Tonight we have beech and crimini mushrooms from Trader Joe. The other night we used shiitake mushrooms. Always use red bell pepper which seems sweeter and helps brighten the dish. Use Kombu as suggested to substitute for dulse flakes.

I really enjoyed this! I made it with a mixed pack of oyster, enoki, and royal trumpet mushrooms (from Costco). We made it with fish sauce because we were out of dulse and also out of kombu, which made it not-vegan but also was good! As others have commented, it did turn out a bit thin. Next time we will do more and a more proper roux and probably also cornstarch.
Delicious! This recipe is a keeper, for sure.

This was awesome! I'm not vegan, but I can't get crawfish where I live and was missing some good etouffee (and I like to minimize meat consumption when I can). I doubled the amount of oil and flour to start the roux and added eggplant in addition to the mushrooms. I think this really helped make it thicker (it's an etouffee not a creole). Replace old bay with a whole lot of cayenne pepper and Tony's if you want this to really taste spicy and authentic.
Is this gluten free?
Check label of Creole seasoning to see if gluten free. No gluten in Old Bay seasoning. No gluten in rest of ingredients.
I used gf flour for the roux and substituted the paprika, thyme, and pepper for the Cajun seasoning to make it gf.

ThIs dish was absolutely amazingly delicious! I see a few comments about it being soupy.  Mine initially was as well, but I cooked it down without a lid until I achieved the desired consistency.  It made all the difference.  I Did not use the Old Bay or bay leaf and it was so yummy.  I will definitely make it again.  Highly recommended.

I made this as directed, even searching in several stores for the dulse, which I doubt I’ll ever use again. The resulting dish tasted unpleasant, murky and with off-notes, although all the ingredients were fresh.

 My husband and I ate less than a cupful each, including the rice. I’m not sure what went wrong, considering the great reviews it got, but I have a huge container of leftovers that I know I’ll just end up tossing, despite the cost (oyster mushrooms were a small fortune).

We loved this dish and I grew up in south Louisiana. The recipe is spot on. We were able to buy some beautiful oyster mushrooms (don't use any other kind). I didn't have dulse, but Amazon sent it overnight. I do think it added something to the background. We will make this again.

Never used 'creole seasoning' or Old Bay before.  Came home from store with Tony Cachere's creole seasoning.  Subbed wakame for dulse and peanut oil for coconut.  Left out the salt.  Otherwise made as written.  Incredibly salty.

Absolutely delicious, easy and super healthy. The only comment I'd make is that it's strongly spiced as is, so perhaps start with a little less of the creole and old bay seasoning and work you way up to the perfect amount for your taste. Also, I didn't have quite a pound of oyster mushrooms so threw in s few cremini which worked fine. The lemon squeeze for serving is a must.

Because of the pandemic and social distancing, I was able to buy oyster mushrooms at my local farmer's market.  I improvised by using King Trumpet mushrooms and white button mushrooms, about a 50-50 spilt.  Also, I found that a pound of fresh mushrooms produced more that the two cups called for in the recipe.  It might be helpful to focus on two cups rather than one pound.  Most fresh mushrooms sold in a bag comes in ounces.  The King Trumpet mushrooms I brought came in a 9.5 ounce bag.

Next time I'll skip the flour and just saute the onion mix. Used 1 small can tomatoes with juice. Skipped the dulse and broth and used a couple of tomato cans' worth of water instead. Extremely flavorful. 1 lb oyster mushrooms way more than 2 cups. Forgot the lemon; would have been nice.
I forgot. Also added a blob of tomato paste.

I adjusted from 3 Tb of flour to app 5 Tb, to get the roux thick enough to where it was clumpy and golden. Followed the recipe exactly up to adding only 2C of broth and subbing crushed wakame for the dulse.  Turned out perf!

This is SO delicious! We followed the method verbatim, except no celery, and a little more onion. I also subbed Canun seasoning blend and paprika for old bay seasoning, and small few mini mushrooms for oysters. We served it with polenta and I am so impressed. Perfect, savory, filling, satisfying vegan dinner.

Wonderful recipe! 1 pound of oyster mushrooms is definitely more than 2 cups. I had about 3/4 lb and it definitely could have taken a full pound. Also added 1/2 tsp of cayenne for some heat, which was perfect.
Loved this recipe! I served it with and wild and brown rice mix, which was lovely. Honestly wouldn't change a thing.
I followed recipe almost exactly. I did add one can of chickpeas as the end and used shitake mushrooms because I had them on hand. I found them nicely meaty. Overall excellent recipe.
Excellent recipe. Followed the recipe exactly, with terrific results. A real keeper! Thank you!!!

This was delicious. I skipped the dulse and it was still great. I used elm oyster mushrooms for how they break down well for soups, reduced the water by 1/2 cup, added 2tbsp cornstarch in water and added a little more than 1tsp lemon juice to the pot. I also stuck with the more traditional basmati rice.
I used trumpet mushrooms and a can of kidney beans. I used Tony Chachere's seasoning and made my own Old Bay type mix. Very tasty with brown rice and cornbread.
Brilliant as is, and adaptable in so many ways.  A favorite in our household.
I made this last week.  It was Delicious!

Delicious & easy to make. I substituted coconut flour (careful it browns quite quickly) and added okra - both worked great & I think the okra helped thicken the sauce a bit. For my meat loving husband, I cooked a spicy sausage on the side, cut it up, & spooned the etouffee on top.
Added a cup of short grain brown rice, and used 4 cups of vegetable broth - added over time as liquid was absorbed. Risotto style. Made a really nice rice in the dish!

Delicious! I made a few changes which I’d like to note. I used 2 containers of cremini mushrooms instead of oyster mushrooms ($), and a whole can of diced tomatoes (did not drain). Next time I’d use 4-5 tablespoons of the flour. I also swapped paprika for smoked paprika which was delicious. My last swap was for a brown and wild rice medley which I think gave it more flavor. 

A lovely flavorful, healthy meal I will definitely be making again!

Delish! I skipped the dulse, didn't have any. Used creminis and portobellas since that is what I had on hand and the different sizes were actually quite nice. A touch extra of smoke paprika for that meat flavor. Served over cauliflower rice cooked with a hardy pinch of turmeric and some garlic powder. Totally grain free and tasty. Oh an gluten free flour cause I have to, but more like 1.5 tblspn since GF flour tends to be more thirsty.

What went wrong?  I followed the recipe as written and ended up with a very watery soup - nothing at all like the photo.  I sooooo want to make something that tastes like this picture looks.  Sigh.  It was tasty enough, but not what I had hoped.  Any suggestions?  Longer cooking did not help.
Very good!  I went light on Creole seasoning for my own preferences.   I will make this again!  Meat eating husband liked it!
Amazing. We don’t have dulce at our grocery store. This was fine without. Added asparagus and spring onions. Substituted cilantro for parsley.
Really really scrumptious. And easy! We’re not specifically vegan, but we had a load of mushrooms and this fit the recipe bill. Highly recommended!
Love this recipe! Since we are not vegetarian I use chicken broth if I don't have vegetable broth on hand. I also serve over Farro rather than rice. Adding jalapeño peppers adds a nice kick!
can i use almond flour or any other type of no gluten flour?
this was delicious! made it pretty much exactly as written except with the addition of celery leaves. used the written amount of water and found the consistency was perfect, not too soupy at all.
