Delicious, but used a different mix of seasonings to sub the ones in the recipe. I used what I had.

I routinely make a dark roux for gumbo or etouffee with Bob's all purpose or Pamela's (like Bob's better), but brown it more slowly, and carefully, than the 90 second flash roux with wheat flour. Use a bit higher ratio than the 1:1 with wheat to make the paste. Add the vegetables to the hot roux just as usual.
How far in advance can you prepare this?
easy to make and hearty taste

This was really good and I don’t know what the meat seafood  version is like.  Blended seaweed sheet with the tomatoes and added garbanzo beans. Served over Arborio rice.   What should the size and consistency of the mushroom be like.

title: Mall-Style Vegetable Stir-Fry Recipe
author: Kim Severson

comments.commentBody: I skipped the put-sauce-into-blender step. Didn't see the point and don't think it made any difference to the result, which was easy and tasty.
Thank you. The blender step almost derailed my trying. I am a very beginning cook and feel I should follow the recipe exactly, at least the first time!
Absolutely true
I didn't realize that was a step until reading your comment. Oops!! Probably just as good--and less dishes!

Coconut aminos are the way to go if you are looking for a lower salt alternative to soy sauce. I use coconut aminos exclusively in my Asian cooking and I cannot tell any difference in the flavor and don't miss the salt content.

Loved this recipe, but as written it's way too salty. I'd already poured in the soy sauce, so I added white wine and cooked it down. I ended up adding about a cup of water to get rid of the excess salt taste. Next time, I'll start with 2 tablespoons of soy sauce and make up the difference with salt-free broth and wine. I also used black beans instead of chickpeas--I think they go better. I served with corn fritters.

I liked the sauce. With all the comments on how salty the sauce is, I used 1/3 of low sodium Tamari sauce (gluten-free soy sauce) and added 1 tsp of peanut butter and didn't need anything else. This can be done with all kinds of veggies good for stir fry. I used cabbage, bell peppers, zucchini and carrots. At the end I threw some cashews bc I like the crunch on a stir fry.
Any thoughts on how to reduce the sodium level?  This one dish is an entire day's worth of sodium.

You can always go with the low sodium soy sauce, and make sure the brine your chickpeas are in is "lower sodium" (or buy dried and soak on your own). That should reduce the sodium by 40%ish, I would think.

I haven't made this yet, but I'm going to try Coconut Aminos. One half cup of one brand of coconut aminos is 2160 mg sodium and one half cup reduced sodium soy sauce is about 4600 mg sodium.  That should knock it down enough to be o.k. without it being a salt-lick.  If I get a chance to try it soon, I'll try to remember to report back.
I am also careful with sodium, and would recommend the following: definitely use lower sodium soy sauce, but cut it back to a scant 1/4 C.  Supplement it with chicken (or vegetable) broth.

Regarding Step 4, an Oxford comma (after chickpeas)  could clarify that the sauce is not part of the chickpeas, but the sauce that was made in the first three steps.  In other words, it’s easy to miss the sauce in this step
I substituted all that stuff with MEAT....  it was delicious.
I suppose you could add practically any vegetable that strikes your fancy to this recipe, but snow-peas strikes me as essential.  Also, throw in a handful of peanuts for each serving at the very end.

I skipped the step that called for putting the sauce in the blender. It didn't seem to need it. I did not see all the notes about reducing the soy sauce till I had already added it, so I tried to cut down the salty flavor by adding dry white wine and a bit of broth. I added jalapeño peppers with the vegetables because we like our meals spicy. This recipe was a hit!

Dates, cilantro and lime strike me as being outside the Chinese stir-fry ethos, even if it is a "mall" version. And the dates? Why not use date syrup? Much easier and faster clean up,  too. Also halved the soy sauce.
What do you do with the sauce? At which point do you add it to the stir-fried veggies?
Stir in broccoli, chickpeas and sauce,
This was good despite an accompanying epic rice fail. Added cabbage, snow peas and extra ginger. Cheated a little by using sauce from jar. No leftovers, so a pretty good review.

This was delicious. I added some shredded cabbage because it was in the fridge. My complaint though is that the time estimate for preparing the dish in woefully understated. 35 minutes? No way, and I'm an experienced home cook. Also, when clean-up time comes, you have the skillet (or wok); the pan you used to simmer the sauce; the blender: and, if you served the dish with rice -- which I did; brown rice -- that pan too.

This came out great. Took advice of others and used 1/4 cup of tamari, and found I had enough sauce to cover the veggies. Also used dried apricot to replace the dates (none on hand) and lightly pan-fried extra firm tofu instead of the chickpeas. Added some mushrooms I had laying around. Rather than serving over rice, I soaked some rice noodles and added them to the pan after the sauce. I think this recipe is very adaptable to whatever veggies/protein you have on hand.
This was good, but way too salty for my taste. Next time I will try low sodium soy sauce and substituting with some broth as suggested by other cooks.
Just fabulous. I used bok choy instead of broccoli - the family loved it so it'll be in a regular rotation in the house. It is really easy too, so I highly recommend this recipe.  5 stars from me.

Used low-sodium soy sauce and low-sodium canned chickpeas as others suggested. My wife really loved the recipe and gave it a 10 out of 10; I opted to add some additional soy sauce to the finished product to cut the sweetness. It is very much as promised, a "mall style" (think Panda Express) style dish, but with elevated ingredients.
Not worth the effort

Low sodium soy sauce, added chicken (sorry vegan/vegetarians) instead of chickpeas, baby bokchoy instead of broccoli, added fennel, sunflower seeds instead of sesame seeds. Otherwise, I followed the recipe exactly!

This is really yummy - not salty at all. Maybe because we had low sodium soy sauce? The whole family ate it right up.  I'm going to make up a big batch of the sauce and freeze a few week's worth to make weeknight dinner prep even easier. Fab easy vegan dinner. Hooray!

I use 1/4 cup soy sauce and 1/4 cup water to cut back on the saltiness. I'm also lazy, so I grate the ginger and garlic, and use 1.5 tablespoons of plum sauce instead of the dates. No blending needed!
Used 3 TBSP brown sugar instead of the dates. Also skipped the blender step. Sauce was delicious!
I didn't have any dates and didn't feel like going to the store just for them, so I added a few tablespoons of brown sugar and I thought it was fine. Skipping them also precluded using the blender.
Made this last night and it was a hit. I added green onions and celery (because I had some in the fridge and didn’t want them going bad) and peanuts. I’ll definitely make again!

made it with chickpeas.
I prefer the one I made with rice noodles and tofu.
Also, dates were on the large side, a bit too sweet. 
Would be helpful to specify dates in terms of cups/tbsps
Popular with teenagers! Note: This gets salty fast. Use low-salt soy sauce & low-salt chickpeas.
I didn’t find the sauce too salty. I left out the chickpeas but added cashews. Mixed with Maifun brown rice noodles. Sriracha with serving.
So salty! Blerg. I was disappointed. Way too salty to even enjoy it. If you haven't made it yet, cut back on the soy sauce by half and use water to make up for it. Wow. So salty.
Great but should be a bit more spicy. Next time I will add more garlic and ginger and maybe a chili pepper. We added shrimp served it with glass noodles. Hats off to Kim Severson.
What do you do with the sauce? Do you add it to the stir fried veggies, and if so, when?

Hi!

Sauce is to be added in Step 4:

“Stir in broccoli, chickpeas and sauce, and cook, covered, stirring occasionally, until vegetables are cooked to your liking, about 10 minutes.”
carefully, reread step 4
This recipe is easy and delicious! We had no carrots so used green beans.  Substituted low sodium tamari as well to reduce the sodium.

Ok I had an aging can of garbanzos, leftover roast chicken and a lot of veggies from one of those dipping trays leftover from a potluck.
The sauce was very salty, but since I had at east twice the amount of veggies, plus a couple cups of chicken, I figured rightly it would be ok. I added a splash of white wine, but don't think I really needed it. And a jalapeño, just 'cause.
Spouse was quite pleased. He loves mall food. I will do it again.
I know this was meant to be a vegetarian dish, but adding flank steak (searing it in the pan before everything else) worked!

I followed the stir-fry directions explicitly but exchanged the sauce recipe for a low-sodium bottled teriyaki sauce (3/4 cup) and received nothing but high praise.  The cilantro and lime finish it off wonderfully.
I followed the recipe exactly, and when I stirred in the sauce, I added broth to make the stir fry more moist and a bit runny so it mixed better with the rice after serving.

This wasn't as salty as I had thought because of other comments, but it somehow turned out really sour and the lime wedges gave it a really intense aroma. Maybe I shouldn't have taken two quite large limes and a bit less rice vinegar... Apart from that, it tasted fine.
Have everything except the dates. s. What would be the equivalent in granulated sugar?
I don’t like dates. I’m thinking of substituting pomegranate syrup

This recipe is very tasty, and I will make it again. I cut the soy sauce in half down to 1/4 cup. I used olive oil instead of grapeseed or coconut, as that is what I had on hand. I also added one chicken breast, cut into small pieces, as I was trying to serve 5 people. The chicken worked well in this recipe. I used basmati rice instead of brown rice with the stir fry, and it was good!

I found this to be delicious and very easy to prepare.  Great use for dates.  I get a 5 lb. bag every year from my sister in law who lives in Yuma, and am always looking for creative ways to use them.

This was good, even though I screwed up and didn't add the sauce until the end. I used 1/4 cup of soy sauce and 1/4 cup of water and and found it plenty salty. Kale was a good substitute for broccoli (which I dislike). Question about the dates: how many fresh medjool dates equal six dried ones? I used four but maybe the sauce could have been a bit sweeter.

Just five minutes before completion of cooking I added firm tofu cut into small cubes, in place of chickpeas, and the dish was delicious! I also added some green scallions, cauliflower and a bit of sliced red cabbage.
Added crumbled tofu in place of chickpeas! Super good- needed a bit of sriracha for some added kick.

This recipe was a big hit--though blending the sauce (we used our mini food processor) seems important since the blobs of mushy dates would seem sort of gross I think. Also, we sauteed a little tofu for the protein, to give more textural contrast than the garbanzos.
Three tablespoons of vinegar was too much. Would cut by 2/3 and try one T.

This was fantastic. The dates in the sauce is a genius idea. Do blend it, however, since you will want it to coat your veggies evenly. Makes great leftovers, too. The chickpeas sounded weird to me, but were great.

You could use almost any combination of veggies, just add them to the pan based on the time it will take to cook them. The sauce was easy and delicious but I didn't use all of it. I added it to the pan a little at a time until I liked the consistency. I had about 1/4 cup left in the blender. I would not skip the blender part. The sauce tasted so much better once I blended it. Blending incorporates the date flavor completely—big difference in taste once that happened.

This was a terrific recipe to play with. I added snow peas and mushrooms, didn’t have carrots and while they would have been welcome, I didn’t miss them. Instead of chickpeas, I used velveted chicken. The sauce was salty so I diluted it with water and a little extra cornstarch. It still was more plentiful and still plentiful.

this was really good, made with chicken thighs diced up, tossed in salt, pepper and some arrowroot powder, then cooked in olive oil in a skillet. left to keep warm in the oven until the step when the sauce is added. skipped the chickpeas. followed the rest, except for the blending sauce step. it did not need it. used 50/50 low sodium soy sauce and coco aminos.
Yummy, make again.

Thank you to everyone who commented re: the saltiness. I added 1/4 cup of reduced sodium soy sauce and it was right at the brink of being too salty! Other than that, it was a delicious and easy dinner. I cut down on the number of dates (4 instead of 6), added a tablespoon of chili paste, and didn’t blend the sauce or add a thickening agent, because I’m about that sweet, au naturale chunkiness.
I need a covid replacement! I have no dates...should I replace it with white sugar, brown sugar, honey, maple syrup, or orange juice?

Definitely should have read the recipe all the way through! My intention was to make it as directed but had to make some changes due to poor planning. Substituted ground ginger in the sauce. Didn’t blend the sauce. Didn’t cover the veggies while cooking. And it turned out delicious!! A little salty but luckily that’s my thing anyway

The sauce is great, used recipe as a guide for the rest, based on what I had available. The sauce is good, though salty, as mentioned. I used 1/4 c of soy sauce and 1/4 c of liquid (soy) aminos. I added ~1/4 c of mirin for sweetness/tang.  No broccoli, so used zucchini, some mushrooms, and shredded cabbage. These added extra liquid, so might have been better cooked separately and then added at the end. Threw in some chicken for the last 10 minutes, served over thin rice noodles. Will make again!
Easy recipe, but still much too salty, even using low sodium soy sauce. If I ever make this again I'll use half broth or maybe wine perhaps.
Great recipe! I have made this multiple times and it is always a hit. For those who could not find dates, I used dried apples and it tasted just as delicious as when I have made this with dates.

I made as written except I used 1 cup chickpeas and 1 cup edamame and didn't blend the sauce. This dish is so delicious. It's salty, but that's what I wanted from a "mall-style" stir fry. I happened to cook my chickpeas from dried with no added salt, so maybe that helped balance the overall saltiness. Still, really tasty and easy and full of plants. I highly recommend it!
I don't normally complain about these recipes, but the sauce was so salty (even after adding a half cup of water) that we found this difficult to eat. Not a winner.

Used 3 TBSP brown sugar instead of the dates. Also skipped the blender step. I reduced soy sauce to only 1/4 cup and skipped the arrowroot entirely (was planning to thicken with cornstarch if needed but wasn't needed - perhaps because I cut the amount of soy sauce.)

I took some liberties. 
Made sauce as written (excellent!) but without the sesame oil. Did use blender to make it smooth.
For the stir fry, I used carrots, daikon, celery, peas, and scallions, no chickpeas.
After sauce was done, I added pre-soaked rice noodles, stirred it all together, and off heat they went. 
I then warmed tofu cubes marinated in very little chili crisp.
My oh, my, Is it good!

We made this as written, except as another reviewer suggested, we skipped the step of putting the sauce in the blender and it came out delicious anyhow. We also added tofu in addition to all the other ingredients, and we skipped the cilantro. We would made this again. I was fantastic!
I love the NYT recipes but this one was a bust. The 1/2 cup soy sauce was way too salty and made the dish inedible (and I like salt!). I froze the dish and hopefully can salvage in a soup.

This really hit the spot! Usually when I try to make something to satisfy the Chinese takeout itch I’m really disappointed. I used half soy sauce, half water per others recs and it was perfect - still very salty, which I wanted.

I used tempeh instead of chickpeas and added snap peas. 

I blenderized the sauce and was glad I did - more like “real” mall Chinese. (Btw I don’t think I’ve ever actually had mall Chinese, or if I did, I was in 4th grade.)
I should have known that “mall style” does not translate into edible.

This came together easily as I had all of the ingredients on hand. I used bok choy in  place of broccoli, chicken thigh in place of chick peas and added a few shiitake mushrooms. As written it is too salty for my taste. I will definitely cut the soy. It was very tasty for a quick meal.
Soooo good and the family LOVED it!

Half and half cooking soy sauce and water. The dates really really worked. Skipper sesame oil as I didn’t have any. This was the most successful takeout-style stir fry I’ve EVER made - i think the dates for sweetness, the not-overly-stiff cornstarch slurry, and then the blending were the keys. I will absolutely make again. Veg mix: shallot, mushroom, red bell pepper, broccoli, snow peas, tempeh and scallion greens.
Use 1/2 the soy with 1/2 chic stock

I omitted the chickpeas and added medium firm tofu cubes instead. But everything else in the recipe I kept the same. It was very delicious, the end result was flavourful and didn’t taste like dates at all (I puréed the sauce until smooth). I will definitely be making this again.
The sauce was really awful.  Too much rice wine vinegar.  Much worse than Mall food. I'm sorry I wasted my time and money.
Substituted Boc Choy for the broccoli. Added 1/4 c of Sherry to the sauce. Delicious!

Sauce was good and it does need the blender to finely blend the dates into the sauce.  I could have done without the chickpeas and just used more veggies ... mushrooms, bok chow, etc.  but it's good and fairly easy.
Made this as written with the exception of thin sliced chicken breast at the very beginning and omitting the chickpeas.  It was really good!

The goal was to do something with what was left of a rotisserie chicken, plus I had a can of past best-use date chickpeas. I used less soy sauce, subbing more rice wine vinegar. Probably I overdid adding random veggies plus the chicken, because there isn't that much sauce. Skipped getting out the blender. Chickpeas are a bit mushy but that could be age.
 But not bad, over rice. It was a good way to use leftovers and the chickpeas (best used by 2021. What can I say?)
I like the idea of using dates in the sauce- they supposedly cause less of a glucose spike. One could do this in an all purpose stir fry sauce, too.
This recipe is missing a step. I made sure to let strangers into my home to eat next to me to get the full mall experience. They even stole my chopsticks.

title: Bryant Terry’s Millet Cakes With Smoky-Spicy Green Sauce Recipe
author: Kim Severson

comments.commentBody: I thought this recipe was delicious, but do take issue with the way in which it was presented.  It is most useful if a recipe proceeds in the order in which things need to be done--I found that the presentation was disorganized and there were no clear estimates of the time to be allowed.  Next time, I will prepare the cakes and the sauce ahead of time, and just cook the cakes prior to serving.

In the grand tradition of substitutions, I'm going to try this with roasted poblanos, omitting the jalapeño, rather than regular green peppers. Will let y'all know. 

I've also never seen powdered flaxseed, so I'll just pulverize some in our mini food processor or mortar.
Ground flaxseed at Trader Joe’s.

If you have access to Bob’s Red Mill Products in your grocery store, ground Flaxseed meal is available. Keep extra in the freezer. Add it other things in small amounts - cereal, salad dressing, etc. to use it up.
Ground flax and water are a substitute for eggs, so if you don’t care about it being vegan, you could also just use two eggs.
Good lord but that looks like a lot of work.

Made this delicious meal on a weeknight- it took 2-3/4h. I was cooking the whole time.
On their own, the cakes are very plain; I thought they would be flavourful.
BUT The Sauce! Oh My! I have decided to always have that on hand! Forever!  Do Not Skip The Sauce!
Recipe is a lot of work and could use every pan in the house! 
I reused saucepans and skipped the skillets.

1/2

2/2
I didn’t have 2h to set up in fridge, so I put tray in freezer while I blended up the sauce.
Next time I will make the lentils and millet at least 1/2day in advance. 
I added 2T oat flour (in addition to millet flour) because mixture was too wet.  That might be due to lentil grain not being thoroughly cooled.
I broke it into steps to have better flow to work. Still a lot to get thru.
Next time will garnish w/Smoky Spiced Pine Nuts (NYT)
As a vegan with celiac's, I'm always looking for interesting vegan GF dishes. I usually have to adapt. Here, I substituted GF panko, but that was the only change. Delicious.

Pretty tasteless overall and the cake batter was thin, had to add flour to make it easier to handle. The 1.5 hr prep time was not realistic unless you have a pair of sous-chefs to run the various preps simultaneously. Alone, plan on 2.5 - 3 hrs prep time.
How many cakes does this recipe make?
Step 8: "Using your hands, form the mixture into 12 cakes, using about 1/4 cup of mixture per cake"
Has anyone tried freezing these after step 8 and frying later?
Never again. Too much effort for too little pay off on the burgers. Sauce very nice
Can I make this in an air fryer? My family eats a whole food plant-based diet with no oil or other processed foods for health reasons.

This makes for a pleasant meal, but I have to agree with the commenter who said that the return on investment isn't enough to justify the prep time and finickiness of the recipe. I did enjoy the nuttiness of the toasted millet seeds and the lightness of this vegan and gluten-free entree. I think the sauce would be improved with acid of some sort. Maybe white balsamic vinegar?

title: Alice Waters’s Seasonal Minestrone Recipe
author: Kim Severson

stats.Total Time: 2 hours 45 minutes, plus overnight soaking of beans

comments.commentBody: Even with fresh thyme (and shallot and 1/2 cup of corn kernels added to the ingredients) this simple minestrone seemed bland.  Two tablespoons of tomato paste and 2 tablespoons red wine pulled the flavors together.

this recipe is more of a suggestion than a hard and fast game plan.  even the intro discusses changing the recipe for the season, etc.  to add some oomph i added about 1 T of tomato paste& a large pinch of red chili flakes. i used canned whole tomatoes w/ juice & went heavy on the veggies.
towards the end i also added about 1 T sherry vinegar & 3-5 dashes of worcestershire sauce. miso would’ve worked too)
yummy & easy, but definitely needs some tinkering.

Adequate seasoning is critical to this recipe (salt and pepper). It needed adjustment in seasoning at the end, and it was delicious. Made it with canned beans and canned diced tomatoes but otherwise as directed. Parmesan and a drizzle of olive oil was perfect toppingUsed two cans of drained cannellini beans, saved and used half of its liquid. No green beans but doubled the zucchini and halved The garlic. Also used half chicken stock and half water and a Parmesan rind. At the end punched it up a

Made exactly as directed with fresh ingredients from local Farmers Market. Exceptional. I used both Parmesan as well as pesto. Needed to thin out eventually as water cooked off. But plan to make this a regular.

I made this with the Spring sub-ins as suggested (leek, fennel, asparagus, snap peas, spinach) and used chicken stock instead of water, small can diced tomatoes, throwing in the parmigiano rind.  Delicious and spring-like, esp with a dollop of mint pesto.  Next time, I will blanch the asparagus and snap peas first and dunk in an ice bath - without this step they turned an unappetizing shade of brownish-green.   Nonetheless the soup was yummy and will make again with summer veggies come July.
This is a good example where it would be really handy to have the article printed with the recipe.  It's a continuing source of annoyance that you can't do this.

When I want text from the accompanying article (or others' comments), I copy and paste it to my private notes. The note will then print with the recipe. (There's a character limit; you may have to edit.)
Is three cups of water enough for eight servings?
I found it not to be nearly enough water.
We liked it a lot. I used leftover garlic broth instead of water and added tomato paste to taste. Made fresh pesto. A great early fall soup.

This was delicious! As others have said, up the spices. In my case: more salt, black pepper, dried Sicilian oregano, chili flakes. Added more tomato than called for and a touch of sherry vinegar at the end. Buonissimo!!
This is such a simple, boring recipe. I'm sure Alice makes it and it tastes great. However, this recipe is one as a very simple cook makes all the time and I find it boring!!!!

I used many of the suggestions noted by others and made a delightful soup!  Onion carrot celery fennel as the mirepoix. It’s winter so used parsnips potato and cabbage and canned diced tomato (no juice) as veg. Used 1 qt Chicken bone broth, not water. Included a Parmesan rind (essential). Added chick peas and spinach at the end. Served with pesto, Parm and drizzle of EVOO. Excellent!
Bland & disappointing, even with the freshest possible ingredients. Everything-the spinach & cannellini beans came from my garden this morning. Even generous spoonfuls of pesto didn't help much.
We actually really liked the soup. I used pesto as recommended whereas the others in the family used mozzarella cheese as a topping.
My husband love it but I felt it wasn't very special. Something missing with the flavor
I’d suggest putting a tablespoon or 2 of pesto in  at the end with a splash of white wine. Does wonders for flavor!

This is a delicious simple recipe.  Used Rancho Gordo Marcella beans soaked overnight.  They were cooked in 20 minutes.  Next time I probably won’t soak them.  Used the bean broth and water for parboiling the tomatoes to skin them, added 1 tspn of bouillion.  The addition of parmesan and pistou really elevated and integrated all the flavors.  Nothing bland about it.

Adequate seasoning is critical to this recipe (salt and pepper). It needed adjustment in seasoning at the end, and it was delicious.  Made it with canned beans and canned diced tomatoes but otherwise as directed. Parmesan and a drizzle of olive oil was perfect topping. It was great.

Good base, but I could tell it would be too bland for me:

Dried thyme instead of fresh
Double the beans- canned with juice
Double the zucchini plus 1 yellow squash
1 28oz can diced tomatoes
Double the liquid- use half chicken stock 
Add smoked Turkey leg when liquid is added
Parm rind
Orchetta pasta cooked in soup when spinach is added

Loved it!

I’ve made this twice. Too bland the first time. Second time we used chicken broth and then added Better than Bullion to add depth to the broth. Canned tomatoes, dried thyme and minced
Garlic from the jar worked great for us!
Have you ever read the ingredient list on any “Better than”?

I’m using canned beans, canned tomatoes, added celery, omitted leek, going to add Parmesan rind and chicken broth and maybe extra garlic. I’m using kale. Adding red lentil pasta - just a little. And garnishing w a touch of pesto. I like the flexible approach but agree with some readers may be a little bland. If I had wine on hand that would go in too...

Love this soup. Had to do a few modifications since it's what I had in the fridge. So I used shelled fava beans instead of green beans, eliminated the leek, used a mix of pea shoots and baby kale instead of spinach, and added more water...it was still delicious.
Absolutely delicous. I did add a tablespoon of tomato paste and used a combo of veggie stock and water.

I followed the recipe adding some celery and tomato paste and then burned-off some white wine before adding the water. I added some vegetable stock to the water and followed the suggestion of adding some sherry vinegar. The family was very happy.
I made this recipe twice - I added the 2 tablespoons of tomato paste and increased the garlic to 6 cloves... we loved it

I realized I was out of thyme (pun intended) so instead I used the sage and rosemary I had on hand. It gave it that “we’re getting close to Thanksgiving” smell, which was wonderful. Also, agreed on adding more flavor is necessary. I added some chicken stock which helped to bring it together.
How on earth is this enough for 8 people? Hardly enough for 4...
I have made this soup three times now, almost exactly as written, except with canned beans and canned tomatoes (to save time). It has plenty of flavor and is absolutely delicious.

I added the juice from the beans as well as 2 cups of water. I also split the first 3 cups between water and chicken broth. I browned up pancetta to use as the oil for the veggies. Adds some complexity to the dish. Removed the chunks of pancetta and used as topping if anyone wanted them :) 

Made with pistou (pesto without nuts) and it was amazing :)

This is a delicious and hearty soup! I took others’ advice and added a few tbsp tomato paste, and a Parmesan cheese rind. I also added 3 additional carrots, and 4 cups of vegetable broth in addition to the 3 cups of water.

I simmered a few Parmesan rinds with the soup and cooked it on a simmer for about an hour before adding beans and greens so they wouldn't become a brown mess. A blob of pesto (I keep a homemade supply frozen in ice cube-sized blocks) adds important flavor to the soup.
This was very good, even without the pesto and better after a day in the refrigerator.

If this is being published on today date, May 6th, 2018, I fail to see how it can be seasonal.
Alice Waters is renowned for sourcing local ingredients at the peak of perfection and would never serve this soup at this time of year. The tomatoes, zucchini, and green beans, for instance, are  far from being available, unless they come from very far away.
Perhaps we should file this one away for a few months and focus on ingredients that favor her philosophy in this current season

As someone else noted, in the lead-in it notes it suggests that this recipe is a base to be adjusted for the seasons. For now: “In the spring, fresh peas, asparagus and spinach would make a good vegetable trio, with some fennel standing in for the carrot in the sofrito.“

Alice Waters resides in California....and that is where most of our produce comes and delivered fresh all over the USA. Love California weather. For the rest of us, we have to wait for the seasons. Even here in Florida we have seasons for different vegetables even though it is warm most of the year. Nice recipe to make your own according to the season and what's available.

Added tomato paste and white wine as suggested below. Used what’s in the fridge: kale, spinach, baby carrots, mushrooms, canned white beans, 1 leftover Italian sausage; plus Parmesan and mozzarella. Yum.

First time through a new recipe I follow it to a tee.  This smelled wonderful cooking and tasted great with Parmesan added at table.  No pasta, but a warm baguette.  Yums and mmmms all around.  This is a keeper.

Made this soup today with all organic and fresh ingredients.  Used homemade chicken stock instead of water.  And made a flavored fresh basil olive oil to drizzle on top of soup before serving with dusting of parmesan.  Terrific!  Can't wait to make it again at the top of the season.

Used two cans of drained cannellini beans, saved and used half of its liquid. No green beans but doubled the zucchini and halved The garlic. Also used half chicken stock and half water and a Parmesan rind. At the end punched it up a tad with some freeze dried  Italian seasoning.  Absolutely delicious minestrone. Will make it again and again.
I just saw Matt's suggestion from 10 months ago about copying article or helpful notes and pasting them to private notes so they will print out with recipe. Oh my. Thank you so much, Matt. Brillian!
very good.  11-20

As suggested by Waters, I switched up some of the veg. Made with carrot, turnip, fennel, zucchini, tomato and spinach as the veg which made the broth a little sweet. So, I cut the sweetness with 1T Worcestershire and 1 tsp Sherry vinegar and 1/2 tsp hot pepper flakes. That did the trick. Also cooked soup with Parmesan rind. Others have mentioned the importance of seasoning well with salt and pepper throughout the cooking process which is much needed in this recipe. It was delicious, again!

Made the Summer version and used canned whole plum tomatoes with juice as the tomatoes are not tasty here in June. Also added a small bunch of spinach. Very fresh tasting, but definitely needed the Parm cheese and drizzle of Olive oil at serving time. Will make again.
I applied all the tips.

Just made this almost exactly to the recipe, but used a can of cannelini beans, rinsed and a little chicken broth at the end (instead of water).  Possibly the best soup I've ever made!  I especially love how the flavors of the fresh produce shine!  Yum!
Wait, just realized I also added about a cup of fresh basil accidentally (was also preparing a caprese).  Sooo good!
Added ham because I had some on hand; used some fresh and some sun-dried tomatoes to the recipe as written - rich and delicious. I will definitely try this again without the meat.
used vegetable broth for more flavor

Followed advice of other commenters and added extras. Veg stock instead of water, tomato paste in with the onion at the beginning, doubled the garlic, simmered with Parmesan rind. Added sherry vinegar and Worcestershire sauce at the end. Yummy
I made the recipe using chicken stock in lieu of water and served with macaroni noodles cooked separately, delicious.

Also made with veggie stock to oomph flavor.  Added more beans and spinach to use up some on hand. Had a turkey kielbasa around so popped that in too.  Canned tomatoes added good flavor as well.  I love how flexible this base recipe can be.

Delicious! Sauteed 2 big leeks, added fennel, carrots, celery, dash of thyme and shake of red pepper. Then 1/2 chopped savoy cabbage. 4 c chix broth, 3 c water, 1 Japanese yams and 1 turnip. Cooked 20-30 min w bay leaf and Parm rind. Added 1 can white beans and 1/2 c small pasta. Cooked another 15-20 min. Added sherry vinegar, salt and pepper. Yum!
Also 1 can drained chopped tomatoes with the broth and a chopped zucchini for some green with the pasta and beans
Made a variation with butternut squash, and just onions, garlic, tomatoes and carrots - then a sprinkle of saffron and a splash of lemon juice at the end - and it turned out great!

I made a variation on this with butternut squash, 1 can of pinto beans, and then just spinach, onions, carrots and garlic along with the spices as directed. I used 2 cups of water and about 1 cup of my own veggie broth. I added a splash of lemon juice and saffron at the end. Turned out great!

My tweaks for richer flavor were to cook a couple tablespoons of tomato paste with the garlic before adding anything else. I added a few red pepper flakes and a parmesan rind too. I used veg broth instead of water and canned diced tomatoes. I also parboiled the green beans and cooked some small shells both of which I put in at the end.

Delicious with lots of leftover soup.  I added tomato paste and red wine, as suggested, but not sure if it was needed. I had only one can of cannellini beans so threw in a half cup of ditalini as substitute only. I don’t like to substitute in a NYT recipe unless I have to, but this recipe is so versatile, it really is about the season and what is on hand.
It's a jumping off point! Mine was delicious

To my mind, this recipe doesn’t have enough water to make enough broth. I added 2 more cups of water. To give it a little more flavor, I added a bit of chicken stock and red pepper — and a tiny splash of good, thick balsamic. Turned out great!

This was excellent. Followed the recipe nearly exactly except: browned the leeks before adding them, used homemade chicken stock, and followed other suggestions and added 1 T of tomato paste with onions and carrots. This soup was the perfect way to let farmers market veggies and truly exceptional heirloom tomatoes shine. Beware the other warnings about needing to season more - had we listened this would have been oversalted.
I used significantly more salt, thyme, onion and leek and 1.5 cups of RAO’s marinara sauce. Also added black pepper. It tastes delicious to me now.
Where is the stock, the most critical ingredient of an excellent minestrone? Without it, soup has no soul. Homemade of course.
Add tomato paste
Substituted 28 oz can of chopped fire roasted tomatoes and added 1 cup of water but otherwise followed recipe exactly and it got raves from my guests. A definite keeper.

I used two quarts of organic chicken stock and added some better than bullion (low sodium chicken flavor) to punch up the flavor. I substituted dried thyme, canned cannellini beans (well rinsed) and canned diced tomatoes. I added a little bit of extra zucchini and spinach to compensate for all of the extra liquid. Turned out fantastic.  Serve with sourdough bread!

We love the subtle flavors of vegetables and didn’t need to punch up—but perhaps because we used dried thyme and canned fire roasted tomatoes we had more flavor. This is supposed to be flexible—that’s what we love about Alice Waters Simple Cooking recipes
Question: do you think this soup would freeze well, for meal prep?
Loved this recipe. I cooked the beans in a pressure cooker for 30 minutes. Also added a can of tomatoes because it’s what I had on hand.
My secret to delicious minestrone is adding one piece of Parmesan rind as it simmers.

This soup was fantastic! Slight modifications for timing and season! 
- Used two cans of great northern beans vs dried beans
- Used a light Vegetable broth instead of water 
- Butternut squash instead of Green Beans (served during Thanksgiving Week get together)
- Replaced zucchini with Yellow squash 
- Added a dash more salt and about 20 twists from the pepper-mill at the end 
Great fall flavors and very hearty!

 
-
Whenever I see a recipe is adapted, I can’t help wondering what I’m missing out on. Recipe FOMO?

Loved the simplicity and used it as a template. Swapped the spinach for cabbage. Used great northern white beans. I added some parmesan rinds while cooking the beans. Most of all, used some pork stock I had leftover from a different dish I had cooked. Based on what I read in other comments, the recipe needs some punching up and the stock worked beautifully. 

My only complaint was that the broth to veggies ratio wasn't right. I'd back off some of the veggies to allow a brothier soup.

I made this almost just like the recipe.  No leek or zucchini.  But I added a parmesan rind, which gave it superb flavor.  I also cooked the mirepoix much longer than the recipe called for.  This step adds depth of flavor.  A fine minestrone that reminded me of my childhood.
Don't overcook the beans before adding to soup! Mine were mushy because I didn't pay close enough attention. The soup was still great though. I used bok choy instead of spinach and added celery.

title: Award-Winning Maple Blueberry Pie Recipe
author: Kim Severson

comments.commentBody: So it seems you are double cooking the blueberries - once in water bath and then again in overn - doesn't this make the blueberries mushier than if you just put them in pie crust?

Hello Jan,
In my experience pre-cooking the blueberries makes them perfect, if you do not pre-cook blueberries they make for a wet, soppy pie. I cook mine on the stove-top with a grated apple for the pectin and spices add to my pre-baked shell for a simple pie or add to my pie dough and bake per usual. In each case I have had no problems. The stove-top method allows you to control the consistency of the blueberry mixture, if the apple is not enough, just add a little cornstarch. Happy Baking!

Cooking only half the berries in a sauce with cornstarch and lemon to pour over the other half raw in the crust, plus glazing the crust with a little heated jam and letting that cool before assembling the pie, solves all these problems and makes a delightfully fresh tasting pie. Bound to be more nutrition left in the berries if they are not cooked to death.

Jan that's my question too. Cannot see the point of pre-cooking the filling. Particularly if using wild blue berries. Otherwise seems lovely. Our fav blueberry pie is in a cooked shell: half cooked blue berries folded with the rest uncooked berries. Whipped cream.

The sous vide technique lets you cook them at a precise temperature and leaves them whole.  It's more difficult to cook them in a pot and preserve their shape and not have them lose their juices.  An interesting use of the technique.

But please do not get put off if you do not have fancy sous vide equipment - slow cooking, gentle stirring on the stove-top does the trick!  Alice Waters never serves a dehydrated strawberry rehydrated and infused with beet juice for color - sometimes the simplest approach is the best and easiest!

p.s.

Whipped cream w maple syrup, or w powdered sugar & rose extract (indian markets or try the internet), or w vanilla & powdered sugar.

The Joy of Cooking "cockaigne" pie crust is both tender and flaky due to the method of cutting the fat into the flour.

No recipe keeps the berries fresher, as only half the berries cook 2 minutes and the other half are only cooked by having the hot sauce poured over them. You may need to add more cornstarch depending on the berries.

Pointless to pre-cook the blueberries.    Despite what one poster indicated, it's not scientifically possible for double cooking blueberries to result in less mushy blueberries  than those that are only cooked once.     Also, having made hundreds of pie crusts in my years, a tsp of salt is quite a bit.    I use a pinch (1/4 tsp or less) for 1 1/2 cups flour.    Lastly, with 1/3 cup of maple syrup in the filling, the maple extract seems like overkill.   I certainly would not buy it for this.

I'm not a blueberry pie fan (my sister and I are sour cherry all the way) but I baked this and loved it, and so did my family. I love blueberries but not in pies or muffins, but the cooking of the blueberries really makes a big difference. It doesn't taste like a traditional blueberry pie. It had a faintly exotic flavor, like it was huckleberries or some other rare Pacific Northwest berry that doesn't get baked into pies here in the NE.
Why are maple and blueberry's a good combination?

You haven't grown up enjoying local maple syrup over blueberry pancakes. Fresh blueberries dropped onto pancakes baking on the griddle, then spread with butter and maple syrup.
Once that experience is parked in your brain, you'll know.
Blueberries and maple syrup are divine. Try cooking some (1 cup berries with a tablespoon or two of good syrup) for your pancakes or waffles sometime. Heaven. Leftovers keep in a jar in the fridge.

Because they're both native to Maine, and in Maine, they know what they're doing.  it's the perfect natural pairing.

Before you bake/eat the pie, eat a lobster!

I did not have maple extract so I substituted 1/4 tsp vanilla extract.I also decided it needed a little more acid and added a bit more lemon juice but this will depend on the sweetness of the blueberries.Finally, yes you ab-solutely have to pre-cook this or the filling will be very runny. In addition, pre-cooking will allow you to taste and adjust the flavoring before committing it to the pie.I even added about 1/8 tsp of pectin to help thicken it.It's delicious but filling requires pre-cooking.
Maple is great with anything!! A wonderful sweetener!!
Unbelievable blueberry pie! I used to hate blueberry pie until this recipe. Highly recommend!

I followed the recipe pretty exactly (minus maple sugar and maple extract, neither of which I could find—used raw sugar and vanilla extract). Double-cooking the blueberries went fine, in case you’re worried about the comments—mine were still mostly whole.
I really enjoyed this recipe! As a skilled pie maker, it’s wonderful to use a recipe that goes above and beyond most fruit pies. I also took the design idea and it came out beautiful.

To avoid too much wetness for blueberry filling AND the double cook, just toss the raw berries with tapioca granules - about 2 Tablespoons for this many berries.  You will get a perfectly set blueberry filling, with the berries neither mushy nor dry.

Looking at the picture I see a Galette or slap pie.
I wonder if by lowering the initial baking temperature to 350-375, the berries could cook in the pie in the oven at one time without the pre cook in the sous-vide or on top of the stove? If doing this would it be smart to cornstarch flour or clear gel to the berries before baking?

Don't skimp on the tapioca! I did two even tablespoons because I didn't want it the filling to be gummy, and it didn't set at all. I may have also overlooked the filling on the stovetop, so be gentle if you aren't doing sous vide. But despite the fact that the fruit was runny everyone raved about the taste. I'll probably try it again next summer.
Delicious but needs more tapioca to set fully. I used a heaping 2 T but that's not enough. Use more!

Can’t find instant tapioca anywhere — everyone is out of stock. 

I did find tapioca flour. Can this substitute, and if so, how much?

This pie is absolutely delicious. The apple and maple really go well with the blueberry, making a unique but amazing pie. The dough ended up being a little bit dry for me, I’d recommend potentially adding a little bit extra water if you’re concerned about the dough. The filling was also just a little sweet, so I don’t think the pie would suffer from taking some of the sugar out. Definitely worth making, and a really interesting take on a blueberry pie

This is a PAIN to make but, good gravy, it's incredible. Agree with others that the dough was dry--I also had a hard time with it, but it did puff up nicely. Used the maple sugar and extract, which are worth ordering because the flavor does come though. Two things: 1) Put a sheet pan underneath the pie or on the rack below!! Ours bubbled over on the oven floor and smoked up the kitchen. 2) We would have used an immersion blender to blend half of the cooked berries before baking for less texture.
Our family did not care for the maple sweetening.

I just would like to say that Steps 2-4 are confusing if you are NOT using a Sous-Vide because it sounds like you should skip Step 3; however, I read the recipe at least 5 times before realizing there's a bunch of other ingredients listed in Step 3 that need to be included (I know they are listed in the ingredients list, but if you're just moving through the recipe and skip Step 3, it will be too late before you realize there's a whole host of ingredients you never got to).

Fabulous recipe! I added a tablespoon of lemon rind and omitted the maple extract. I used a full bodied maple syrup ( B grade ) from Quebec. As well I prebaked the crust bottom, then poured in the filling and topped the pie with pie pastry pieces cut decoratively using cookie cutters. I brushed the pastry with egg and sprinkled some granulated raw sugar on top. Definitely a repeat! Love serving this pie with a superior quality vanilla ice cream!

Divide the blueberries into two equal portions, cooking half with sugar, lemon, vanilla  and spices  until they burst and get to a jammy consistency with a cornstarch slurry.  Cool those, then combine with the other (uncooked) half of the berries, pile into a cooked crust and  chill,  This makes a delicious pie that preserves the freshness of the fruit.  Don’t forget to add cardamom with the cinnamon  for a “what is that wonderful flavor?”  surprise.
Tapioca wasn't enough to set filling....but tasted great. Will try again.
Felt like too much salt in the dough, usually use 1/8 tsp kosher salt. Couldn't taste the other spices.
Can I use cornstarch instead of tapioca? If so, how much?
@Babs Also interested in this sub! Have you tried?

@Babs, you can, of course. I start out making pies with flour as a thicker move on to cornstarch and now I prefer tapioca and I don’t grind it up, either.  A few little tapiocas floating in the pie are kind of cute. I feel tapioca adds the least flavor to the pie.

There are recommended amounts on the box for both cornstarch and tapioca. Usually, that’s the place to start.

Great thing about fruit pies is you make the pie paste, mix the fruit, spices and the sugar and whatever else you want, put it all together and bake it. 14 steps seems like self imposed torture for extremely little reward.

I have never had anything left of a fruit pie. made the simple old-fashioned way. I believe my blueberry pie was one of the factors that my daughter-in-law considered when my son was courting her.

title: Kerala Roadside Chicken Recipe
author: Kim Severson

comments.commentBody:
I always smear turmeric and a tablespoon of water on the chicken and let it sit for a five minutes.Cleanses the chicken and removes any odor of the skin.

Three and a half cups of oil may be a little too much .So go a bit easy. Garnishing does not require so much of oil. I would add a dab of yogurt when the chicken is marinating.
Sprinkle a few fresh green coriander leaves on top when serving
I didn't use any oil.  Great recipe without it and heart healthy!
Unlike in the North, Keralites do not use yoghurt for marinating meat or seafood (when you are on the roadside, there is no place to store the yogurt!).
this is deep fried so why would you use less oil?

Roasting the chicken in the oven ( add a tablespoon of coconut or safflower oil to the marinade) rather than frying also works well and is much healthier. Lots of fresh coriander garnish makes it delicious
What Temp did you use for roasting in the oven andd approx. tim
Is there a best substitute for coconut oil?
Any oil that has a high smoke point will work, such as safflower oil, peanut oil, corn oil, etc... you just need it to be hot.
Having eaten something similar in Kerala, my guess is that the recipe would work without coconut oil, but not without curry leaves.
Not for flavor, coconut oil imparts a coconut flavor that works very well in curries. It also keeps for a long time, so don't hesitate to buy some.
3 cups of coconut oil plus 1/4 cup sounds like an awful lot of oil for such a small amount of chicken, no?
You can strain and reuse the cooked oil.
It is enough to deep fry (1-2 inches deep) in a 12 inch pan.
It looks like this recipe is deep frying the chicken, so you will need at least that much to submerge the chick pieces in a skillet.

I plan to cook this dish shortly, however I would make the following alterations: I would use dark meat instead of white meat. I would garnish using the shallots, lime and maybe some some mint. Instead of frying I would barbecue.
Sounds good, but it's a completely different dish now, and nothing like the original!  Barbecue?!

@L not really - the recipe already calls for dark meat and a shallot garnish. i personally wouldn't add lime to indian food but ymmv. 'barbecue' in this sense just means 'grill.' it would change the dish, sure, but choosing to grill/saute/bake instead of frying is a common sub people make.
How did it work out?

Minor comment on all the "cautious" reactions to coconut oil...the science on CO has caught up over the last decade. The knee-jerk reaction that it's an unhealthy oil has been reversed.
If you are up on all the science and still have good reasons for not using it, then great. If you are working off the old "saturated fat is automatically bad" paradigm, it may be worth reevaluating.
CO has a strong following in the health community.
But each to his own. Peace.

I read this too but the latest on Dr Oz disagrees--coconut oil only in very limited quantities (1 tbsp a day max)--having said that, the amount absorbed in a serving of chicken is probably within this limit

This dish is vastly improved if you use boneless, skinless chicken thighs instead of chicken breast. The dark meat really works wonders with this spice mixture. The spice stuck to the meat a lot better with the dark meat, and formed a crispy shell around it, while also preventing it from being too dry. 3 cups is way too much oil. You can get away with making this with a half cup of coconut oil or even less to cut down on the greasiness, and still have the yummy comfort of fried chicken!
My thought exactly to use thigh instead. Thanks for the post. I’ll make it this weekend.
Grapeseed oil has a high smoke point and has far less saturated fat (1.3g vs 13.6g) than coconut oil.

Grapeseed oil is about 14% saturated fat, just like olive oil. Coconut oil is about 90% saturated fat. All cause arterial and endothelial damage. I made this recipe using NO oil and a small amount of coconut flavoring so there was no fat on board. I also used tofu to avoid animal protein, which raises IGF-1 levels. It was a cinch to plant-base this recipe - no oils, no animals, no added sodium - and it's delicious!
Refined coconut oil has no coconut flavor, so don't add coconut flavor if you replace coconut oil with another oil.

This is a lot of coconut oil! Reduce by a third and sauté, brown and finish in oven for 15-20 minutes for perfect cooking. Also I would marinate the chicken in 3 tbsp yogurt and a squeeze of lime as well to tenderize the meat and cook it faster. Try adding coriander powder to the spices to add another layer of flavor, and the best companion to curry leaves and red chilies for the garnish is to use a teaspoon of mustard seeds. Put them in first, let them splutter, then add the other two.
It sounds like you made a completely different recipe. I followed the recipe almost exactly, and it was very good. I did not worry about using three cups of oil.

I was low on coconut oil, so I ended up using 3 tablespoons to cook the chicken and 3 tablespoons to prepare the garnish. Worked just fine--more than fine actually. The two of us ate almost the entire platter. Luckily there is just enough for lunch tomorrow. Served with lime wedges and Melissa Clark's one-pot mujadara with leeks and greens.
This is super-easy, and delicious.  Even my anti-chicken teenager loved it.  I used a little sesame oil in the ginger-garlic mixture to make it "paste" better.
Not sure why 3 cups is called for. I just scooped 3 spoonfuls in and it all worked out. Unless you want a deep-fried Southern dish, cut waaay back on the coconut oil

great recipe.  Good article that accompanied this.  Indian food is not about goopy curries.  I used smoked paprika but will check all the Indian groceries here to see if I can find the Kasmiri chile powder.   more of these recipes please...
Or just add in some cayenne with your paprika, that should tkae it up a notch. :)

i bought mine on amazon. a nice sized box and has lasted me a good while. (inside the spice is in a foil bag so it stays fresh.) it is not spicy, i'm not sure why people keep mentioning using cayenne as a sub. just because it's a chile does not mean it is spicy.
If you cannot find the Kashmiri Chile powder locally they sell it on Amazon. That’s where I found mine and I made this recipe. It was amazing. Good luck!

Delicious recipe. I didn't fry the chicken, but baked it. Preheated 3 tablespoons of coconut oil in a Pyrex dish before adding the chicken pieces, shallots and chili. I used a full bag of shallots, quartered. All turned out super well and the meat was succulent. Served with natural yoghurt, brown rice and homemade parathas.
I used a nonstick skillet and a tablespoon of coconut oil at a time which worked great. The chicken was still carmelized. I topped with yogurt and fresh cilantro. It's awesome!

I made it plant based, no animals, no oil, and it's delicious. I used tofu in place of the chicken and substituted coconut flavoring to add some flavor without the saturated fat of the oil.  You also don't need any salt in this recipe, it is so flavorful on its own!  The Indian grocer near me sells Kashimiri chili and masala. What a great heart-healthy recipe!

Indian food without salt? Blasphemy. Without at least a little added salt, the spice blend is muddy and muted. The salt brings brightness and clarity to the seasonings. Trying to enjoy Indian food without salt is like visiting the Louvre and never taking off your sunglasses. You can see the art - and it is beautiful - but you'll never experience it fully.

If you're used to adding sodium to your food, the real flavors of the dish probably will change. I eat a plant based diet so I don't add salt to anything. I love the way my food tastes, I don't need salt to make anything taste good.  And I'm not about to start adding it. You have a nice day!
How do you cook the chicken without any oil?

Made it as written, except I have been to Kerala a few times and I know I’m not a huge fan of coconut oil, so I used peanut (and much less).  As others have mentioned it needs some salt, but other than that it’s pretty darn good.  The flavors definitely remind me of nighttime roadside fry pushcarts, standing around with the other locals (yes, often drunk) enjoying spicy finger food.

Love this recipe! Easy to include a batch of cubed extra-firm tofu to accommodate vegetarians -- it takes beautifully to the spice mixture.  Used only enough coconut oil to bring level to about 1/4" in pan, i.e., did not deep fry, just turned the meat frequently.  Rave reviews!

This tasted authentically Indian. I thought the three cups of coconut oil sound like too much too, but that's the right amount. Don't leave out the garnish, it makes a good dish great. You can transfer oil from the chicken when it's done to use for the garnish. The chicken by itself was the right amount of spicy for our 8 year old but too much for the 4 yr old.
Great recipe! I had run out of coconut oil so used just a little ghee to sauté the chicken and also the garnish. Delicious!
I used 3 tablespoons of grape seed oil for the chicken and olive oil for cooking the topping and was quite pleased with the result. Would definitely cook again.

I made this with 1.8lb  chicken breast and used just 2T of coconut oil, which was plenty. I turned the pieces at about 5 min. Did the curry leaves, etc.,  in very little oil. Delicious dish! Made for dinner but totally plan on making for bites with drinks when we have friends over. Might try with thighs but still plenty moist with breasts.
Go online to Logees where you can buy a curry plant to grow in your home and use for Indian recipes....
This is honestly my new favorite dish of all time.
Better than freezing curry leaves-I prefer drying them on a cookie sheet.  They retain their green color and doesn't splatter when you put them in the hot oil

I made this dish and it was totally delicious! I couldn't find Kashmiri chili powder, so I used cayenne and paprika (1:1) and I think it came out great. A lot of people seem to be concerned about the amount of oil needed to fry the chicken... I too was skeptical and used probably closer to 2 cups, but found that using coconut oil didn't make the chicken feel or taste particularly greasy. I served the chicken over rice, with a side of roasted garlic broccoli.

I couldn't bring myself to deep fry in coconut oil.   I added about a tablespoon of canola oil just before cooking to the chicken and coated it, then broiled the chicken for around 15 minutes turning once.  It came out just fine.  I did to the cook the shallots et. al. in coconut oil.  It's a keeper.

Made modifications.  Used 1 lb. boneless thighs but kept the same amt of spices.  Only used 2-3 tbl of coconut oil so sauteed not deep-fried.  Wanted a veggie so used a green bell pepper.  Next time would consider using anise because of the fennel in the seasoning.  Passed on the curry leaves because didn't have any.  Served with rice.

I followed a suggestion below and stir fried the chicken in a tbsp of coconut oil. That was perfect. I garnished it with cilantro. I could not find my fennel, but it was still great without it. I'm looking forward to using it next time.

PLEASE, please...curry leaves discussion aside, who has made this, following the recipe reasonably closely, and How. Much. Did. You. Like. It?  I really haven’t been able to get a clear idea as to its success -as written.

Very good. I don't have a mortar and pestle, so I just minced the ginger and garlic as fine as I could, and added a splash of water to form a paste with the spices. Only had 15 minutes to marinate the chicken, and it was still incredibly flavorful.
Used about 1/2 cup oil for the chicken.
Curry leaves really added a lot, as did the fresh ground fennel in the marinade. Served with yogurt with a little lemon.

Forget deep frying: use a few tbsp oil (I used canola), and then remove chicken, put shallots, etc, into pan after chicken is cooked, then mix chicken back in.  (Deep frying would waste all that great marinade!) Also: no curry leaves at the shop, used basil leaves plus about 1/4 lemon zest. Also used smoked hot paprika instead of chiles.  Didn't use chili powder, though probably will next time.  I'd suggest a little less salt.  Delicious!  I plan to try it with prawns, should be great.

Well, this continues to be one of our most favorite and requested NYTimes recipes of all time!  And a perfect go to meal during this time of self isolation for us. I make no adjustments to the recipe. This is our big splurge meal and quite off the beaten path as we usually cook Italian/Mediterranean meals. Grateful for the tips to freeze extra curry leaves and we now keep a full stock of all the spices required. For side dishes: Saag Aloo, Vegan Lentil Dahl and Naan

I put coconut oil and dendê oil in the same group. Use as little as possible and as infrequently as possible. As Julia always said, "everything in moderation." Once in about 10 years, for the sake of authenticity, I'll survive a little unhealthy stuff. Anyway, I don't plan on living forever. The one change I'd make is to substitute thighs for breast meat. I hate dry, stringy chicken.
Knee-jerk reaction or not, I do think that 3 cups of oil is excessive. I have a feeling it's meant to be 3 Tablespoons (generous) for the marinade and more as needed during frying.

this was outstanding! i subbed peanut oil (for the higher smoke point) for frying- i liked the larger amount of oil for frying due to less need for "deliberate" flipping of such small pieces. i used dried curry leaves in the garnish- i agree that 1/4 cup of coconut oil did seem excessive in that i poured off a couple of tsp after scooping out the garnish.
overall 5 stars- because short of a tad too much garnish oil- this recipe was Spot On and crazy simple.
cheers  thanks for sharing!

This was so good!  ok i have to admit that I couldn't find curry leaves and I thought we had coconut oil and no, we didn't.  So i used peanut oil and it turned out nice.  Also, I dropped some broccoli in with the shallots and did some rice...a whole meal.  That spice blend was so good.  I'll try it again when I get all the stuff and can find those dang leaves!
Curry leaves!?

I cannot get fresh Curry leaves Is it OK to use dried Curry leaves instead?If so,what quantity?
Judylynn
I've often substituted dry curry leaves and they work quite well.
When you do run across fresh curry leaves you should buy more than you need. They freeze well. Just drop them in a baggie and press out the air. They'll keep for months.

I've experimented with dried curry leaves some. I think they are passable in certain masalas (like sambar powder), but I'm afraid to say I really don't think they'll work in this recipe. Fresh curry leaves are lemony, piny and piquant — and most of that essence is lost in the dried form.

Super tasty dish. Had everything in stock except curry leaves, so I used Thai basil (sue me). I used only thighs since chicken breasts taste like cardboard. Also decided to pan fry in about 1/4" of coconut oil to avoid mess/hassle of deep frying. Worked out nicely. NB to those hoping to re-use oil: it gets spicy from the marinade.
Coconut oil is essential for the authentic flavor of this dish so I would not omit it....
it needs much less oil than called for.  But it is delicious.

After reading these comments, I used about a third of the amount of coconut oil — you really only need as much will cover the chicken completely while it's deep frying (for my 10 inch skillet, that's a lot less than three cups!). Delicious and will make again.

This is a much requested meal in our house!  We mostly cook Italian and the combination of spices, coconut oil and heat are wonderful! I double and triple the recipe for 5 people and have leftovers.  I have switched to only using boneless chicken thighs which have more flavor than breasts.  To accompany-Sag Aloo and the Davis Tanis recipe for Winter Vegetable Curry with Apple Raina. AMAZING!

I am a 2nd gen South Indian married to a North Indian spouse, and I like most Indian food. I did not like the flavors in this chicken at all. The only thing I did differently was to stirfry it in a wok in a small ant of coconut oil rather than deepfry, but I don't think that would be enough to change the flavor. Maybe the fennel threw me off?

Sujatha, I am from North India. I too would not use fennel in this dish, but each to his own. I'd definitely also not do ghee as suggested by an earlier reader, but coconut oil. The meat does not have to  be deep-fried, For me, the Indian curry leaf is essential! And, I would use lamb/goat as an option, leaving the bone in (removing the skin) I am glad to have this recipe. It is interesting how minor variations in cuisine across our sub-continent are so awesome!

This is a truly delicious dish!  I had whole fennel seeds and no grinder, so I put the seeds in a zip bag and used my heavy rolling pin.  Worked great.  So glad we have Asha here in Atlanta.  Her cooking is a treasure!!

Kashmiri chilli is wonderful, vibrant, flavorful -has just a little heat very flavorful & gorgeous color. Wks in butter chicken too & in many Amer recipes. Fried curry leaf w/shallots in coconut oil is traditional Kerala garnish that is used to top different things (like Kerala
stew made w/coconut milk) - delicious. I a bag of Kashmiri & freeze - refill a jar from frozen stash! Curry leaves freeze too - they have an earthy deliciousness & wk in place of bay leaf in stews.
Are the Kashmiri peppers the same as the "flower" peppercorns used in Sezchuan cooking? -- They're my latest fave, and especially useful since I am a "heat" wimp.
Try slightly stale croissants, flattened and heated on a griddle as a substitute for the flaky bread--may sound odd but works great as a pinch hit sub for parathas to sop up gravies of this dish!
similar dish in sumatra uses handfuls of curry leaves mixed with strips of pandan leaves and sliced lemon grass in addition to the shallots, and whole green birds-eye chili peppers.

Delicious- I cooked per the recipe minus a bit of the oil as it was a smaller batch.  I used hot paprika as I had no Kashmiri chili powder and cilantro rather than curry leaves. The garnish adds a nice additional element.  We served with rice, various Indian pickles and chutney.
Ghee (clarified butter) is a good substitute for coconut oil. Both have very high smoke points.

I served this with Greek lemon yogurt and my kids who never eat anything interesting ate it well. Also used mix of smoked and sweet paprika since I couldn’t find Kashmiri powder (am in Chicago, what?) and crazy spicy wouldn’t fly here.

This was absolutely delicious!  I found the Kashmiri chili powder and fresh curry leaves in a local Indian grocery -- it's worth the effort to find them.  I stir-fried the chicken in coconut oil instead of deep-frying, and it was fine.  I agree with some of the other readers about using chicken thighs instead of breasts: I plan to try this next time, as I think that will make the dish even more flavorful.  As a final note, I served this over brown basmati rice.
Super yummy!!!  Will definitely cook this again, but just a quick warning— it made my apartment smell strongly of Indian spices for a few days!

Even if you don't have all the ingredients, suggest you try this recipe.  Out of our wheelhouse. Kitchen will smell heavenly.  Marinated chicken wing sections in the paste mixture with a few spoons of coconut oil. Grilled them.  Dredged the sliced shallots in curry and cayenne and fried in coconut oil until crispy...like candy, but too spicy for hubby.  I know curry leaves important, but hey...if you don't have them, you don't have to go back to tried and true, try something new.
Use a really good virgin coconut oil for a fresh and round taste. I could not find Kashmiri chili so I used korean Gochugaru.
Delicious recipe! I didn't have time to marinate the chicken for the full one hour and it still turned out great. Well worth it to find fresh curry leaves for this one!

Oh, my, so tasty. Who would have imagined that such a humble sounding garnish could be so delicious? I think I may have to put it on everything from here on out. 

Like many other people, I did not deep fry the chicken. I also used aleppo pepper flakes to substitute for dried chili peppers that I did not have.

My husband really liked this dish and will definitely make again. I had fresh curry leaves on hand which prompted me to try this dish. We only had chicken thighs and I couldn't bring myself to use 3 cups of coconut oil so used maybe a quarter cup. The moisture from the chicken and the remaining oil allowed me to create a gravy that added an extra bite of taste. Served with sliced baby turnips sauted in olive oil and Penzey's Mural of Flavor seasoning.  Subtle aftertones of heat. Great meal.
I used chicken thighs, added a bit of olive oil to the marinade and baked it in the oven. Excellent with some roasted broccoli on the side.

Delicious! I followed the recipe except the deep frying. I cooked this as I do most South Indian chicken -- saute the shallots/onions in a bit of oil (not 3 cups), saute the chicken, then deglaze as needed to keep the spices from burning.
Best new chicken recipe I’ve tried in the past year. I truly thank you for this.
One of the greatest things I have ever cooked. The confluence of flavors such as fennel, curry leaves, coconut oil is mindblowing. I used sourdough to sop up all the fat. Will make again and again!
You can use a heaped tablespoon of coconut oil and turn the chicken just fine. Could with a little salt and lime juice.
If you skip on the coconut oil you're missing out on the flavor and authenticity it imparts. Kerala basically translates to "Land of Coconuts" — making this dish without it would be so sad.

I used chicken thighs. I only used a tiny bit of oil (a teaspoon or so if that) to fry the chicken in my enamel griddle pan and that was plenty - I used coconut oil. It was absolutely delicious!! I will make this again and try it with tofu next time. So authentic tasting and just the right amount of spice. I didn't add the chili powder, but it was still divine. thank you for this fabulous recipe!

Delicious.  Made it exactly as written except for a few minor tweaks.  Doubled the garlic, Kashmiri chile powder and dried red chiles.  Let the chicken marinate in the refrigerator closer to four hours.  Did the garnish in the same pan used to fry the chicken since the oil was already in there and heated.  Truly incredible, can't wait to make it again.
Double fried, needed flaky salt at the end.  Very good, would make again as a double batch.
Love this dish!!! Add extra ginger if possible
This recipe seems to be missing something: how much coconut oil do you use to deep fry the chicken?
Three cups. The 1/4 cup is used for cooking the garnish, separately, in a small skillet.
It says 3 cups coconut oil as the last ingredient in the list under "For The Chicken".
question:  can this be done in a hot air fryer?

I would suggest at least doubling the shallots for the garnish. Once it was all cooked, I drained and wiped the pan and tossed the fried chicken pieces with the garnish right before serving. It was big hit, even with substitutions (frozen lime leaves for curry, peanut oil for coconut, cumin/caraway for fennel seed).

My husband does not like spicy food.  I followed the recipe using smoked paprika in the marinade, and then fresh sweet peppers and mint leaves in the garnish.  I served it over brown rice.  It was not very satisfying.  If I were to do it again I think I would want more vegetables in the mix and some kind of sauce/liquid.
Would this work as a vegan recipe if I just left out the chicken?

I'm plant based so I don't eat oils, fats, dairy, animals, or add sodium to my food.  I've made this dish twice already, once with tofu and once with garbanzo beans with a small handful of walnuts tossed in!  Omit the coconut oil to make it heart healthy and don't add any salt, it's really flavorful  without added sodium.
Any ideas about where to get curry leaves?
Amazon.  I just leave them out, though.
Many cities have at least one Indian grocery, and those places always carry curry leaves.
This is outrageously delicious.
Add more salt. Sauté in few Ts coconut oil, not cups. Cook shallots on med, hot burns too fast. Make cucumber raita to accompany, good for balance.
I added no salt and it was delicious!  Really these flavors pop without added sodium.

I followed the recipe pretty faithfully. I found  it a bit too garlicky and not enough ginger. The dish tasted great and I hope to repeat it soon. What would s Kerala cook make to go with this. I did potato and broccoli but not very Indian. Don't try to debone drumsticks!
Very good, and easy to make. We did not make the garnish.

2 questions:

1) Curious if Kaffir lime leaves could used in the dish instead of using lime juice?  Other people have suggested adding the lime but would the leaves be a better option?  The leaves could be sliced an added to the marinade (and not cooked) or the whole leaves could be fried with the curry leaves and then removed.

2) Trying to locate a good garam masala that has little if any cinnamon. I just don’t like the taste of it in a savory dish. I know masala’s can vary greatly. 

Thanks
You can grind the spices without cinnamon to make the garam masala to your own taste.  Look online for spice proportions or a good Indian cookbook should have a recipe.

This is simply delicious! OK, it might not be the most healthy meal, but then you don't eat it every day so it shouldn't matter much...

I marinated the chicken overnight and took Bhut Jolokia powder instead of Kashmiri chile powder to get it really hot... It was great, will surely cook it again!

P.S.  I did not use the recommended amount of coconut oil.  I used about 1/2 cup, maybe less.  I had adjusted the recipe to prepare about 3/4 pound of chicken.  Worked fine.  I kept turning the chicken pieces to get them brown all around.

Great recipe. I grew up in North India; there was a small Kerala kitchen near my college. They made this except they also did it with lamb/goat. There was also a hint of tamarind in it. I do not recall having it with paratha, but with coconut rice. Of course the "correct" way to eat was the middle three fingers of your right hand, rolled up into a ball and popped into your mouth.
Thanks for sharing. I will call my local Indian market and order some baby goat and make this.

I marinated the Trader Joe’s chicken In buttermilk and the spices/garlic. Sautéed it in coconut oil and served with Trader Joe’s Naan and a salad mix which I added a chopped up apple, avocado and a little coconut oil and vinegar for the dressing. I’m sure following the recipe would be great but I have a problem following cooking directions exactly. This was a great, healthy and quick meal.

2 questions for anyone out there.....I am so NOT a cook but I love Indian food and would need to travel 6+ hours to get any so I made this. My questions are, which thing would I increase if I liked it hotter, the peppers or the chili powder? Also is it supposed to be so dry? I thought it’d be something I could serve over rice but it was too dry (or I didn’t cook it right, probably that’s it). Thanks in advance.
Yes, this is meant to be dry. It's a street side snack and not a gravy.

Made it exactly as directed, but wasn't able to mash the garlic & ginger into a paste, despite working on that step for 15 minutes or so with mortar and pestle. Still super delicious, with rave reviews from the family. Recipe was requested by my son-in-law!
If you have a local Indian Grocer, then they sell garlic paste and ginger paste... Done for you :)

Second time making this, I was able to make a good paste of garlic & ginger. Served at a dinner party with turmeric-coconut/yellow rice and naan. This is definitely a keeper that we will enjoy again and again!

Second time making this.  Grilled chicken as kebabs. Used 1/2 tsp high heat cayenne plus 1/2 tsp smoked sweet paprika instead of chili powder.  No deep frying, just canola oil for the relish (and basil leaves plus about 1/2 lime zest instead of curry leaves, which weren't available).  Really superb.
I really loved this! Couldn’t find curry leaves, so used 1 tsp Sambar curry powder. Delicious! Perfectly hot and spicy with the peppers. This tastes really authentic.
We didn't love this dish. We served it with vege biryani. It was ok, but we won't be cooking it again. The leftovers stunk the fridge out, even when covered with wrap!
Good flavor. I added the spices to Greek yogurt to marinate the chicken, then skewered the pieces and cooked them under the broiler till done. PERFECT, and I avoided 3 1/2 cups of coconut oil.

Made this yesterday with thighs instead of breasts and marinated only 3 hours. As other notes suggest, I used significantly less oil, probably about a tbsp / tbsp and a half to cook the chicken and it worked well. After cooking, I placed the chicken on paper towel to drain excess oil (there was still a lot). Topped with the garnish as well as cilantro and lime. 

The recipe was fantastic with these minor alterations. I would make this 100x over.

So flavorful. Chicken breast is perfect for this dish. I had to make some substitutions. Did not have coconut oil so used neutral canola plus some coconut milk to marinade. Also added lime zest and juice. Did not have curry leaves but did have fresh basil which was nice and bright mixed in at the end. This recipe is adaptable and was a huge hit.

I made this as written with the exception of frying the chicken pieces for only 2-3 minutes per batch, as 5-6 minutes seemed far too long for breast meat. My chicken still came out dry. I wished I had used thighs instead as I would normally do for dishes of this sort. Very disappointed and will not repeat.
I would make double the topping next time. Delicious!
This recipe turned out perfect and is a favorite in my home.
Adapted as rub plus some coconut oil on chicken breasts, baked at 425 (convection) for ~20 minutes
