Whoa this is amazing!!! I had the recipe saved for a while, but held off trying it because i could never find fresh curry leaves. Last night i made the recipe without curry leaves and i was blown away. I love this dish! Since i didn't have curry leaves, i added some fresh chopped cilantro
I made this with a cup less coconut oil, simply because I ran out, and just flipped the chicken.  It’s amazing.  Served with rice and some apricot jam.  The apricot is a nice compliment.

Coarsely grind — or better yet, crush with a mortar and pestle — some black peppercorns with the fennel seeds, and sauté these with the shallots, and add some lightly roasted grated coconut. Use chicken thigh pieces, and don’t use so much oil, and you’ll get some really nice, relatively healthy Kerala pepper chicken!
I am heat intolerant. Is there a way to cut the heat without terribly changing the flavor of this dish?

Two things:

Garam masala is a North Indian spice mixture. I’m surprised it’s in any South Indian recipe. Is this a short cut? Common for this dish? Anyone know?

Step 4 is called a tadka. It’s a common finish to a Southern dish. The recipe doesn’t mention is that curry leaves splutter when they hit the hot oil. Have a lid ready when you add the to the oil. The the leaves should turn almost translucent. When the noise and splatter dies down, then add the chilies. Don’t burn them. Done.

I was short on time and skipped the garnish step, but it was still quite tasty. The chicken breast wasn't flavorful enough; I wonder how it would be with lamb or pork. Oh, I shallow fried the meat (life is too short to clean up the mess from deep frying!)
I used a half cup of coconut oil. Plenty.

Like others, I would consider a mix of sautéing and oven-baking to limit the amount of coconut oil consumed in this recipe.  Coconut oil has been mistakenly considered healthy because of good marketing.  It is predominantly made up of Lauric acid, which raises total and "bad" cholesterol levels, albeit less so than butter.  Everyone deserves some indulgence once in a while, but the myths surrounding coconut oil have been debunked.

I made a version of this dish inspired by the spice mixes. Not an authentic version of this dish (no disrespect to what this dish is supposed to be) but it was delicious.  After letting the chicken soak up the spices, I sauteed it in a pan with 3 tablespoons of oil.  When lightly browned, I added coconut milk.  Once everything was well blended and the spices permeated the coconut milk, I added slivered onions and quartered mushrooms and simmered.  Served over rice. Garnished with fresh cilantro.

This was delicious. As some suggested, I used a lot less coconut oil. It was easy to fry the chicken in the oil at medium heat, then flip it over. Curry leaves were easy to find (like for $2/bag) in an Indian grocery.

This is one of my favorite chicken recipes. The only change I made was using thighs instead of breasts. The large amount of oil is necessary to "deep fry" the chicken. But since there's no flour or starch, the oil doesn't stick to the meat the way it might with a traditional breadcrumb/flour crust. It did not come out oily at all. The curry leaves are fantastic in it.

I really enjoyed the flavors and found the Kashmiri chile powde not to spicy which was great. I bought a pre grilled flatbread which I warmed and made a cucumber salad with a sour cream and lemon dressing to offset the heat which paired nicely. The only addition I made was a couple of lemon wedges to squirt onto the chicken. I’ll surely make this again. A pleasant and filling meal that was totally worth it and unexpected.

Instantly a go-to dish. One of those ones you think about all the next day, and imagine which friends will enjoy it. Perfect as a coffee-table feast for a TV binge, with a few crudities and dips on the side. The paratha absolutely makes it - I’d never used frozen ones before, but they are a revelation.
On advice of others here, I dis this with just a few tablespoons of coconut oil instead of a few cups. DELICIOUS.

Don’t skip the coconut oil if you can tolerate it and use  unprocessed coconut oil to get the coconut flavor that comes with it.

Only use a few tablespoons and fry it in a wok at high temp in batches. Gets just as crispy and tender as deep fry.

One of our all time favorite dishes to make with chicken, so satisfying and the perfect amount of flavor and spice.

I subbed anise for fennel. Also, instead of frying, I baked the chicken at 400 F for 20 minutes then about 6 minutes while I sautéed some pre steamed broccoli in the shallots and peppers then served that on the side. A drizzle of lemon on the chicken really made it pop.
Came out good and delicious. Adjust chilli to your taste.
This was excellent. I added whole coriander seed to the marinade, which was a nice extra touch.
Delicious. Virgin coconut oil (cold pressed is aromatic and flavorful) added to the flavor. I will make this again.

title: Roman-Style Spring Lamb With Fresh Sugar Snap Pea Salad Recipe
author: Kim Severson

comments.commentBody:
This looks excellent. Trader Joe's (am I allowed to say?) sells young boned New Zealand lamb leg that would work nicely for this.

Much too early of course for local peas, snap or otherwise. Thoughts on early local greens that might work instead?
Try dandelion greens.
Re Trrader Joe's - the only boned lamb I've seen there has been pre-seasoned.  Will have to look again.  Also, they sell the sugar snap peas year-round.
'lay-up' is lightly grilled asparagus, peeled and cut on a bias, harder to find are fiddlehead ferns; either combined with a mix of wild mushrooms

This was excellent. For the salad I added thinly sliced yellow and red bell pepper. I also used snow peas rather than the snap (the snaps looked pretty sad in the grocery store). I did thinly slice the fennel as well. I feel like you could use a mix of any crunchy, sweet veggies for this salad.
Lard is a magic ingredient in this recipe.
What exactly are anchovy filets? Are they fresh or canned?  How large should they be?

You can easily buy them canned in almost any grocery store. They're packed in flat can like sardines but they're even
smaller. I would start out adding just a few and tasting since they're strongly flavored and very salty.
Thanks!

This was delicious. Cut the recipe by two thirds and was worried there wouldn't be enough liquid for the lamb to braise, but I added a few more tablespoons of water towards the end and the meat was succulent. Used English peas and added baby heirloom tomatoes to the salad. Will definitely repeat this!

The amount of meat specified is fairly meager, especially when you consider you'll have to do a little trimming of fat and tendon. I bought about a pound more, and increased the other ingredients proportionally. If you have leftovers, so much the better.
Polenta or Farro makes a nice side.
The anchovies are *essential*. If you taste this while it's cooking, you'll think, "hmmmm, OK but a little thin and acidic". The anchovies completely transform the sauce.
I substituted balsamic for suggested vinegar and lamb stock, then added sautéed vidalia onions.  Lastly, I finished I  a low heat oven.  It was a loved by all

Had 3-4 lbs of med. rare roast leg of lamb leftover from Easter Supper. Diced this and sauted in the olive oil. Didn't have white wine or Champagne vinegar but did have a very good white balsamic that has become a staple for me. Like anchovies used a full small can (2 oz) of Crown Prince anchovies in olive oil drained. Cut simmering time to 45 min added the anchovies as indicated with 15 min left. Cut back salt in both recipes couple of pinches coarse kosher salt for each. Loved results.

Aside from dropping Hazan's option for butter as a cooking fat and decreasing the amount of flour from 1 tbsp to 2 tsp, it's hard to see how Severson has "freshened", rather than merely tinkered with, Hazan's recipe for pan-roasted lamb.
I don't believe I have encountered Calabrian peppers in any domestic market, but the recipe contains NO info on sourcing or substitutions.  Ideas?
They are available on Amazon, but after reading about them, I am going with hot cherry papers since I already have them on hand.

It's just 1 teaspoon, I'm using
sambal!

Look for the grass fed lamb (Randalls, Central Market, Costco) for a 'sweeter' and less 'gamey' or strong-tasting lamb. Makes all the difference. Roast or saute with rosemary, mint, herbs, garlic,salt etc. Lamb is very forgiving; you can overcook it and still have a delicious meal!

I just made this four our Easter dinner. Some of my guests are iffy about lamb so I used pork shoulder. It is an amazing and tasty dish. I couldn't find the right peppers so I used others and added a bit of Cayenne to give them zip. And, I forgot the anchovy as I purchased anchovy paste in a tube. I think a little would go a long way and make the dish a bit richer.
Loved this braised lamb recipe.  I used Balsamic Vinegar (good quality) rather than the Champagne or white wine vinegar.  It just seemed more logical to me and turned out well.
Made it for Easter dinner... excellent... very similar to Hunter's Chicken preparation which translates to Pollo di Cacciatore... its DELISH!

Coat lamb chunks with olive oil, garlic powder, and black pepper.
Broil until browned.
Mix flour, rosemary, vinegar, and mashed anchovies with pan juices; pour over meat.
Bake for 1 hour; add water if juice gets too thick
Make the salad and dressing as indicated; serve on top of cooked meat.
Best lamb you will ever eat comes from Alberta, Canada. We love to buy locally and this is just one more reason to. Thanks for the new recipe and will try it for supper soon.
Has anyone made the lamb the day before and rewarmed?

This is a wonderful recipe that I have made several times, following the recipe just about 100%,  Did it again yesterday.  I use a little more water and flour to make a little extra gravy.  I myself LOVE the vinegar taste but not everyone else does -- the one I make I will cut the vinegar in half.  Aside from that, it's fantastic!
What is a Calabrian pepper?  Is there a substitute?  Is it hot, like a chipotle?  I can't find it in Whole Foods!

I made the sauce as gravy for a roast boneless leg of lamb, with the salad on the side, and it was pretty great. I added arugula to the salad, and subbed radicchio for the fennel (family is not into fennel) - next time will add some sweet peppers for balance. As for the gravy, the anchovies become a paste but didn’t completely incorporate. Next time I will put it in a blender. (Or the Magic Bullet, a miniature Bass-o-matic 😀

Most Romans cook abbacchio in the oven, in a wide terracotta casserole, turning once halfway through and adding a glass of white wine after the turn. The meat is coated in flour from the start, the anchovy oil added near the end. 

If you’re looking for authenticity, serve that pea salad on the side; Italians don’t put vegetable courses on top of meat courses.

title: Sugar Snap Pea Salad With Calabrian Pepper and Fennel Recipe
author: Kim Severson

comments.commentBody: Calabrian pepper substitute?
Did you find the Calabrian Pepper?  I am looking to buy some but not sure where
if you can't find it in your local store, you can get it on Amazon
I'm going to use samba, its just 1 teaspoon

I agree this could use more lemon juice or other acid. I doubled the amount. The Calabrian peppers could be boosted a little as well. They are barely detectable in the amount given. Of course, peppers vary wildly in hotness, even within the same variety. So taste yours, and if you add, add in small amounts. Salad dressing is ruined completely when it just becomes hot sauce.
I never could find the tough string in the peas I bought. I just trimmed the ends, and cut them lengthwise. Worked fine.
I have another sugar snap pea salad that calls for blanching the peas before julienning, so plan to try that.
I would have preferred them slightly blanched as well.

Made this and it was indeed delicious.

However while the julienned snap peas look lovely, it makes the salad very difficult to spear with your fork and get into your mouth. Those long, skinny, stiff pieces are not very cooperative.

Remade it the next day and sliced the snap peas crosswise into more bite sized pieced and it was much more user friendly.

Oh and the second day I added fresh corn ant that was amazing!
There are such amount of peppers, I used a jar of roasted peppers which were fine.  When I got to the end of making this, I just tossed all of the ingredients together and it was great.
What does it mean to 'julienne" the peas?

A julienne cut involves cutting vegetables into uniform strips like matchsticks. Many videos online demonstrate the technique.

To be honest, though, peapods seem like an odd thing to julienne, and the picture here is not close up enough to give one a sense of what to aim for.

Cut into thin length-wise slices.  Julienne, in general, means to cut into matchstick pieces - long with a small cross-section. (but I think if you try to do this with sugar snaps, you will end up with pea mush).
Slice thinly into strips. Shred.
What does it mean to shave the fennel?
Cit into very thin strips.  You can do this easily with a standard kitchen peeler.
What would be a good alternative to fennel in this recipe? It looks appealing, but I've never liked the flavor of fennel.
Celery  or baby bok choy.  You could even do radishes sliced. thinly.

Salad dressing is too oily, not enough lemon. I doubled the lemon and even added a bit of champagne vinegar. I think the ingredients would make a fantastic dressing if just mixed in a blender (all dressing ingredients except the peppers which I would add in the end).
Snap peas too hard to halve also. I subbed the Calabrian with long hot peppers. use fresno red peppers also if you can find them.

I made this for Easter and it was outstanding.  I substituted Pimento peppers for the Calabrian and just added a little kick to the dressing.  No one complained.  One of the best salads I have made in a long time.  Declared a "keeper"
Use more fennel and mint
Blanch peas lightly after stringing but before cutting.  More chili. Use Heritage Farms Hannah cheese.  Don't forget salt.
I found the mint overpowered the thyme. I think I’ll use one or the other next time.
I used a lot more fennel and mint, added more shallot to the dressing and then blended it. Used fresh spicy Korean peppers in place of Calabrian.
My family was obsessed with these peas!  They loved the julienne-- which in my case was simply halving them... and not along the seam, just roughly in half-lengthwise.

Tip: TJs makes an incredible "Bomba" Calabrian chili sauce perfect for this recipe

We didn't use it for this, though, as we wanted to get through a jar of actual full-on Calabrian chilis and didn't even realize they were the same thing!

Also subbed basil for mint and champagne vin for lemon (out of both). Instead of neutral oil did half Calabrian chili oil and half EVOO. It was spicy but a winner! Demolished same day over some polenta. Will make again.
The flavors did really well together but I'd recommend blanching the peas.

title: Cracked Green Olive, Walnut and Pomegranate Relish Recipe
author: Kim Severson

comments.commentBody: Rather than making a separate relish for the salmon, I served the salmon with lemon wedges and added chopped olives, chopped toasted walnuts, pomegranate seeds and avocado chunks to a mixed green salad.
What's a cracked olive, please?
An olive that has been cracked open..as in pull it apart, smash it up a bit with your fingers, like how it looks in the photo.  No knife use.

Cracked olives are olives which have been slit or gently bruised before curing so that they absorb the curing materials faster. As a result, they are ready to eat much more quickly, but their flesh is still fully flavored. Labeling for cracked olives may call them split or slit olives, depending on the region they are produced in.

I am not a fan of pomegranate, so I omitted the seeds and concentrate and ended up with a wonderfully flavorful, savory tapenade. I used Castelvetrano olives from the Whole Foods olive bar, which were ideal. This tasted great over salmon, and I imagine would make a great topping for a vegetable-forward sandwich.

I made this tonight to dress up a lovely piece of king salmon.  Since it is no longer pomegranate season and my local store does not carry frozen pomegranate seeds, I substituted diced fresh strawberries and it came out amazing! The hubby gave it a thumbs up and he is a rather picky eater.

Maybe my favorite thing I've made all year! I used castelvatrano olives that I crushed with a knife, pulled out the pits and tore the olives into pieces with my fingers and it looked exactly like the photo! Pomegranate isn't in season right now and I couldn't find frozen so I subbed some boysenberries. I also was certain I had walnuts only to find that I didn't and had to sub pistachios.  It was divine...and it somehow improves with time.  This will be a staple in our house.

For once I followed a recipe to the letter, and it was a hit. I let it sit for 24 hours before serving. Since “cracked olive” was a broad term and not one slapped on the olive bar, I went with a milder green olive with a lovely color. I also toasted the walnuts before adding.
Where does one buy cracked olives?Or do I just do it myself.  Thank you.
Great recipe, turned this into a main dish salad by adding cooked quinoa and feta. Didn’t have parsley so I subbed cilantro. Fresh, bright flavors.

This is the perfect thing to do with pomegranate. I served it with lamb shanks and warm baba ganoush. I didn’t have pomegranate concentrate so I skipped that and doubled the lemon juice and it turned out great.

My Whole Foods didn't have any pomegranate seeds so I used some fresh blueberries and some dried cranberries. It was very good and possibly will be even better when I have pomegranate seeds. Served this with the oven steamed salmon (arctic char in my case since it tastes a little milder for my DH who doesn't like salmon). Would definitely make this again.
Gorgeous, gorgeous, gorgeous. In. Every. Way.
Excellent recipe, didn't have molasses or pomegranate concentrate so substituted maple syrup and it still turned out gorgeous! Very easy dish to impress dinner guests with!
Make this with wheat berries for a great wheatberry salad

We really liked this.  I recommend really rinsing the green olives well - our relish was a little too salty (and no additional salt needed).  Ratio of pomegranate seeds was too low - think it needs closer to 3/4 cup pips, which would look more like the picture. But blend of flavors was great.
Any suggestions on a sub for fresh pomegrante seeds?  Red currants, I suppose, but they are even less available than pomegranates.

Hmm, there is nothing quite like a pomegranate seed, is there? I think dried cranberries could be good, or a crisp apple or pear, peeled and diced. I quite like beet with walnut, so I might roast whole beets, peel and dice, and let them soak in some extra lemon juice for a bit (to brighten up their flavor) before adding to the relish. It would be a departure, but hopefully a good one.
I have another problem, when I firrst saw the photo: I thought that the yellow pieces were peach, and now I simply cannot figure out which items on the ingredient list they correspond to?
Those are the green olives.
I don't love walnuts and I am picky about olives but I have always loved this salad/relish.  It's perfect for a holiday table.

title: Oven-Steamed Salmon Recipe
author: Kim Severson

comments.commentBody:
I cook the most delicious salmon every week - living on the beautiful West coast of British Columbia. My recipe is similar - except instead of the water I wrap in parchment. 10 minutes at 400. Superb.
As my Mom always says "10 minutes per inch of thickness".
How on earth is that similar? Your temperature varies from the recipe's by 150 degrees, and the cooking method is completely different.
Janis, do you mean you leave out the pan of water on lower shelf and cook salmon wrapped in parchment on that shelf or higher one.  Sorry for my confusion!
Mom's wisdom is what i will take careful note of....she would know
Heat the oven to between 225 and 275 degrees.  What does this mean?  Do you mean 250?
I say give it a go?
I had the same question! Need specific temp!

The wide range given is still low. The oven's temperature setting doesn't really matter other than to help you guesstimate when the salmon will reach the internal temperature of your choice.  Enjoy your salmon and the experience!!

TIPS: Season the FULL fillet, then slice into individual serving "strips" BEFORE COOKING (true for all salmon fillets) giving even "cuts" for serving. Keep together when 'steaming' for presentation, or cook an inch/two apart for shorter cooking time. Season with juice & zest of 1 lemon. A smear of garlic paste/powder adds a dynamic. For lower fat/thiner Salmons (Coho) a bit of butter helps, gIves sheen. GR8 4 Arctic Char, Salmon Trout & steak cut.

Why go through all of this when it's easier to put the salmon on a plate or in small thin baking dish, season any way you like, but including a small amount of liquid in the pan such as mirin/soy mixture, wine/butter, or other, and bake at 225F until done? The salmon comes out medium rare and moist, and there's one less pan to wash.

I grew up on salmon. The main thing I've found is that there is a sweet spot where the flesh remains translucent, but tender and flakes easily under the fork. Salmon cooked for too long or at too high of a temperature turns opaque. The oils congeal. The color changes from a brilliant, saturated orange-y pink to a dull, greyish-pink tint. The taste is bitter and carries a whiff of ammonia, and the texture is dry. Keep the oven low and take a little longer to cook the fish.

Right you are Stella! There's a brief but crucial point of perfection and once you've managed to achieve it once you'll never be satisfied with anything less.  The key is translucent but tender and unbelievably succulent. My go-to cooking method is to first marinate in a soy-lime-juice-ginger-garlic-dill sauce (but only a short time) and then grill skin-side down until the skin starts to blacken and the flip for 30 seconds or so, pull the blackened skin off & let fish rest.

This recipe was simple and deliciously moist.For safety, I boiled a cup of water in the microwave and added it to a pan already in the oven. No spills! I served the salmon on a bed of butter-braised leeks and brussels sprouts along with a little teriaki sauce. Yum!

I cook salmon at low oven temp once a week.  Simply set oven temp to 200-220F and watch carefully &/or use a good instant read thermometer.  There is plenty of moisture in the fish.  Additional steam added to the oven (and the fuss this recipe calls) for should not be needed.  It's hard to dry out a piece of fish/meat at a temp below or near water's boiling point. To speed up the process,  use a higher temp and wrap the fish in parchment; which will keep the moisture contained.
Using an instant read thermometer, any suggestions on a temp to cook the fish to. Diabetes has caused me to lose the touch/eyesight form of testing for being done. Tyia

Ummm, this salmon is over cooked. When you can see the albumin (the white stuff) the protein tightened up and sent that stuff to the surface. Absolutely nothing wrong with it, but it's dry and well done. All that juiciness is on the tray and not in the fish...get a good thermometer and a cedar plank and babysit it (grill or oven)...the lower temp in this recipe is genius

Delicious recipe. I wouldn't use the picture that accompanys the recipe as a guide. The fish in the picture is over cooked...the white stuff on the surface of the fish is albumen pushed/ squeezed up as the flesh dries out.

Brining the salmon for about ten minutes before patting dry, brushing with olive oil, and putting in the oven, relaxes the muscle fibers of the fish so they don't tighten and squeeze out the albumin...  also enhances the flavor.

If the salmon isn't perfectly fresh, you'll really know it in this preparation.

Disposable foil pans are perfect for this.

The thin sheet pan is ESSENTIAL for the time & temp to succeed.

You can make an effective substitute out of several (at least 4) layers of aluminum foil

The fish in the picture is over cooked...the white stuff on the surface of the fish is albumen pushed/ squeezed up as the flesh dries out.

If you don't have a really thin pan, it also works to lay the fish on a piece of foil, put the thicker pan in the oven for 10-15 minutes to get it really hot.  Then place foil onto pre-heated pan and cook as directed

My mother-in-law in Idaho used to cook a big chunk of salmon, the kind that are cut through the whole fish, by putting thin slices of Walla Walla sweet onions all over it, covering the whole thing with mayonnaise like icing a cake, and roasting it. The first time I saw her do it I was appalled though silent; but it was the best, most flavorful, moist, tender salmon I've ever eaten. The mayonnaise disappeared completely and I think the onions did too.

I have cooked salmon in many ways but this was exceptional for texture and flavour - a really great way to avoid any drying out and toughness - all too easy no matter how careful one is - but the slow oven and probably the humidity made the difference and it was easy.  I used a thick layer of tin foil rather than a sheet pan - whatever that is!  Tin foil worked well and was thin and light.
Why heat up an oven when you can use a cast iron pan with a tight fitting top? Just add chicken stock after searing filet and cover for 10min. Velvety juicy unlike the broiled steaks.

Gratifyingly simple technique. Delicious result. I ate it out of the pan, with my fingers (no one saw).
Cooked a 1.4-lb, head-end piece of Coho, in a 250 for degree oven, for 20 or, perhaps, 25 minutes.
I put the water for steaming, in a cake pan.
(I only ate a little bit of it, with my fingers)

I wrap fillet tightly in aluminum foil and stick it under the broiler for ca. 20 min. (or more for a very thick fillet). Perfect every time. No need to add water, oil, or fiddle with oven temp. Have done this in different ovens and it always works. Perfect for no fuss dinner. Addictive!
This turned out delicious.  Used the four sheets of aluminum foil method to create a thin sheet pan.
The dishwasher is great

This was unbelievably good. I used fresh wild coho; convection oven temp 250; 12 minutes for ¾ pound or so. I sprinkled on a little fresh thyme, because I had it.

Instead of making the olive salad, I added a couple of chopped olives, pomegranate seeds, walnuts and half an avocado to mixed salad greens.

Thank you, Paula, and Emily.
Per Paula Wolfert; Use shears to cut salmon into portions before cooking. Leave pieces together for presentation or apart to speed cooking time.
I use a 200 degree oven, pan of water on lower rack, fish directly on upper rack, without pan, for one hour - superb!
Would an Asian steam basket work as well on the stove top?

We used 11.36 oz. Copper River salmon, since that's what was in stock.  Oven cooking temp was 250 degrees. If there is a next time, temp will be 275 and might add 5-10 min, to cooking time as salmon seemed undercooked, even when measured with instant-read thermometer. However, texture was juicy, and skin peeled off salmon with ease. Fresh chive and a bit of fresh lemon were inconsequential. Somehow, the end product seemed closer to sushi rather than cooked.

I made this with a large steelhead trout fillet. It turned out perfect and moist. Very easy method.

I am looking forward to trying this with a salmon fillet next.

i made this recipe for a lg group and used two three lb slabs of salmon.  when done i slathered it with highly seasoned mayo  and covered it all finely sliced ,blanched cake slices.  overlapped them from tail to head, to look like fish scales.  delicious and beautiful!
I forgot to defrost the salmon so I baked it frozen. It took a little longer but at the end the texture is very tender.

"Set oven temp to 200-220F and use a good instant read thermometer.
"To use a higher temp, wrap the fish in parchment;
"Easier to put the salmon on a plate or in small thin baking dish, season any way you like, but including a small amount of liquid in the pan such as mirin/soy mixture, wine/butter, or other, and bake at 225F until done.
"Sub several (at least 4) layers of aluminum foil. Oil or spray the interior, place salmon on it & season.
I cooked this using frozen salmon from Costco.  It was good.  The next time I'll shorten cooking time by a couple minutes to get a more medium rare finish.
How is this recipe different from Mark Bittman's version which was published in the NY Times many years ago?  Some of us have a memory, you know!

More work than need be:

Place salmon skin-side down in glass baking dish. Pour enough orange juice over the fish to cover and leave about 1/8" in the bottom of the dish. Sprinkle with some Herbs de Provence. Cover dish with tin foil. Place in middle rack in oven. Turn oven ON to 350°. Set timer for 30 minutes. Fish will be perfect and oh-so-tasty.

The texture of the salmon is almost as wonderful as sous-vide but without much of the fuss.It took a little longer to cook than advertised but was absolutely worth the effort. Next time will try the technique with a different fish.

I prepped as directed, set oven to 250 degrees Fahrenheit.  It took 20 minutes for internal temp to reach 115.  Mine were 5 oz filets, no more than 1 inch thick.   The results were great, but we ate our salad as an appetizer vs. with our protein.
After the salmon was cooked I raised the water in the frying pan back to boiling and cooked green beans, which added a pleasing economy to making this recipe.

I have achieved this in the microwave.   Cook in a covered dish (Pyrex or Corningware works) at a  setting of 1 or 2.   The fish comes out very tender and tasty and can be sauced or garnished as you wish.  Start with 1 minute and keep testing and adding time in seconds.

What is a setting of 1 or 2 mean?
I microwave fish also. 50% power for 5 minutes with lemon juice.Usually works. May need another 30 seconds or so.

I cook a Scottish organic filet of salmon on asoaked cedar plank in my Green Egg at defused heat at 275º until the internal temp. of the fish is 120º. It is as you describe: moist and succulent. Same principal. Takes about 40 minutes on the barbecue.

Made this tonight using the aluminum foil "sheet-pan" idea. I cooked about a pound of salmon at 230 degrees until it was medium rare. I used a digital thermometer without paying much attention to time passing, but I'd say it probably took a total of 20 minutes for me. I've never had such delicate, mild salmon. I still prefer a nice, crispy skin but this cooking method was simple with very little clean-up and no fishy odor in the house at all.

I also oven "steam" my salmon fillets in individual oiled parchment packages, spread (per fillet) with a mixture of 2 tbsp good cream cheese, 1.5 teaspoons dijon mustard, 1 tbsp chopped fresh dill and a squeeze of lemon. No need to worry that the folds will hold - make folds and staple at the corners and a few times around.  Roast at 400 F for 10 minutes unless fillets are thin - then no more than 8 minutes.  All those lovely juices are in the package when you open and no pan to wash.
Like maybe tuna?
My opinion is that tuna is far better seared on the outside, raw in its center.
I don't have a think sheet pan, just a sort of thick one. Am I in trouble?

The thin sheet pan is ESSENTIAL for the time & temp to succeed.

You can make an effective substitute out of several (at least 4) layers of aluminum foil. Make a flat base about 2 inches larger than the size of your salmon piece(s). Fold up the four sides and crimp the corners to make a strong 1/4 inch rim. Oil or spray the interior, place salmon on it & season. Transfer in and out of the oven using the largest spatula you have.
Are these temperatures in fahrenheit or celcius?  Doesn't seem that fish would cook in 10 min even with the boiling water at these low temps.

I checked this out with another NY Times recipe using an identical technique for oven-roasting salmon. Temp for 300 F and replies indicated it was perfect. Hope this helps. I will use 300 F as my guide for this recipe.
275 C is over 500 F.

Oven temps are Fahrenheit. The oven temp range is due to the various weights of the salmon. Lower weight~~lower oven temp; and lower weight~~shorter cooking time. I cook 2 skinless filets 1-inch at thickest point, (6-7 ounce portion), oven temp 250, steamed for 10-12 minutes. Test doneness with a fingertip @ thickest point; press gently and when you feel slight resistance it is about 115 degrees. If you do a whole side filet increase time & temperature. Thin sheet pan is critical.

I steam fish all the time on the stove top, Cantonese style, with a large two tiered steamer.
FYI: Whole recently alive fish, fresh ginger, jalapeno pepper, white pepper, Peanut oil, sugar, soy sauce, and cilantro.
My question is : How much steam is produced and does it have an effect on any of the electrical components inside of the oven if this technique is used regularly.
Tasted great. Living in Denver at 5280 ft 1.5 lb fillet took 20 minutes

We only have a microwave-convection oven with one shelf for use with the convection oven setting. I suppose I could put a small Pyrex cup of water on the bottom of the oven I put it would have to be place in one of the corners. Any suggestions?

After many tries, the over pans are a theatrical excuse to overuse your oven. A better result is obtained by steaming the salmon fillets in a covered cast-iron skillet with the natural juices of your favorite wine-reduction sauce.

This is a brilliant way to cook salmon. It took a little longer to cook than expected - probably because my sheet pan wasn't thin enough - but it still worked and was worth the wait. Perfect with a side of fresh, steamed asparagus, baby broccoli and chive oil.
sorry-i meant to say cuke slices!
Thanks for being specific about type of salmon - they are not all created equal.

Though I'm sure this is good, I cook my salmon in the microwave. Put whatever you want on it, cover with wax paper & cook at one minute (or less) intervals & let it sit a bit & rest. If the piece of fish is thick or large, I'll cut it in half & rotate uncooked pieces towards edge of dish.
yep. I microwave steak that way and it comes out incredible. I dont understand all the fuss.
12 minutes was too long. It was already medium by that point. I will start at 8 minutes next time.

I did this with a whole, 4 pound sockeye and it came out moist and very tasty (everybody had seconds). I started with the oven at 350, then dropped to 250 after 10 minutes or so, then kept watch on the internal temp until it got to 125. I sprinkled dill inside and out along with a little lemon juice. Easy way to cook fish.

I use tinfoil too but only one layer: a piece large enough to fold up over the sides and crimp at the top. More salmon --- more pieces of tinfoil.
Some thin slices of pickled ginger atop the salmon before sealing it into the tinfoil enhance the flavor of the salmon.
can u make this with another fish

I fond this recipe sometime ago, tried it and have never looked back. It's my go-to for an easy and extremely tasty salmon preparation. I have lightly dressed it with everything from miso sauce to brown sugar with ground chilis and never been disappointed. I use mostly Coho and time the cooking accordingly. Convection roast @ 225 deg. for 11 to 15 minutes.

This was fine.  3 sockeye filets, each ~4oz.  Oven at 270°F.  Took about 15-16 minutes.  The texture of the fish is different when cooking it this way - more silky, less flaky.  Next time I'll use much less salt as it didn't melt as it would when roasting or sauteeing.  Served with brown rice pilaf and braised bok choy.

I did not have a thin sheet pan, but this worked with my heavy pan--my 2.5-lb fillet cooked in about 20 mins. at 250F, then another 10 at 275F. It was moist and tender, and fantastic with a homemade aioli, and very easy. A lovely way to cook salmon and the result was amazingly succulent.
I heated water in a stainless steel frying/saute pan to boiling, and then put it on the lower rack. Easy to grab the handle to slide in and slide out after cooking.

This recipe worked beautifully for a 1/2 side of wild salmon. My experience with wild salmon is that it's often a little dry.  Not so using this steaming method.  Cooked at 250 for 12 minutes to internal temp of 115, per instructions. Perfect!
Great idea to add the skillet of boiling water. I baked it at 250 for 15 min. Perfect salmon. The essence of minimalism. No oil, no butter, no herbs, no garlic. Just fish.
Make sure it's cooked through, don't just go by thermometer.

I followed the advice to make a tin foil pan for the fish. It was a 1.5. salmon fillet. I cooked it at 250F for 20 minutes. We live in the NW, so salmon is regular part of our summer and now winter meal schedule. My husband who is the fish expert, declared this was the best cooked salmon ever and suggested it become our default method for anytime we have salmon.

This is now how I make salmon always. So good, so easy! 
As I had a fennel orange salad on the side, I made a simple orange-butter and added a dollop with fennel fronds before serving.
easy and excellent

This is the best way to cook salmon I’ve ever f
Ound. I used four layers of aluminum foil. The salmon came out impeccably moist, steamed just enough to retain a hint of raw. So good.
I had thawed two 5 oz salmon fillets so I had to cook them even though it was late after a long day and i was NOT in the mood.  This recipe worked perfectly! And my mood lifted. :-)

I'm struggling with this recipe. I've tried it twice now, once with previously frozen but (sold thawed) wild sockeye, and once with fresh farmed king salmon. The first time I cooked to 115 degrees, the 2nd to 120. 

In both cases, the flesh was VERY mushy, and I'm left wondering if I've exposed myself to parasites from undercooked salmon. The mushiness was quite unappealing. I do not have much experience under my belt cooking fish. Am I supposed to test the temperature in a lot of places?

I looked through the comments, trying to find someone who would explain why a very thin sheet pan is crucial - not a pre-heated sheet pan, not a pizza stone, only a thin sheet pan (or 4 layers of aluminum foil?!).  Some of you seem to know this rule. Can you explain?

The salmon was cooked absolutely perfectly. That said, the final product wasn't a winner with my fam, who both said there just wasn't enough flavor. Any recs about how to add a punch would be quite welcome. Our salmon itself might be the root of the problem I suppose. The supermarket baited us with wild sockeye salmon on sale but "had to" change it to Norwegian farmed due to an increase in the price of the Sockeye. I'm guessing this was a legitimate concern on their part.

title: Sweet Potato Confit With Chorizo and Crème Fraîche Recipe
author: Kim Severson

comments.commentBody: Why do people share notes on recipes they've never cooked. Just made this, as directed. Incredibly delicious. Will make it again.
I think it needs the 1/2 cup of bacon fat to add to the 3 cups of olive oil , then it could be called "Olive Oil and Bacon Fat Stew with Chorizo and Sweet Potatoes."

I took this dish and made it in a few different ways. The first time, I made it pretty much as written, for a dinner party at my parent’s house. The second time I roasted the sweet potatoes instead of making a confit. Last night, 8 made sweet potato pasta dough which I turned into ravioli. I sautéed some andouille sausage with pecans, and that became the ravioli filling. The creme fraiche/lemon was drizzled on as a sauce, with mint leaves for garnish. Paired well with a 2007 Nerello Mescalese.

Made this for my guests at Easter Brunch. When we were done eating, I asked which of the seven courses they would make for their own guests. This recipe was the unanimous choice. Everyone was surprised at how the disparate flavors worked so well together. But it sure is a lot of work. I did the sweet potatoes in oil the night before and stored overnight. Worked just fine.

For the greater good, I'll point out that the sweet potato in the picture appears to be what most supermarkets label a yam.  In fact these so called yams in supermarkets are in fact sweet potatoes.  (True yams are somewhat hard to find because, compared to sweet potatoes, yams don't taste very good.)  A sweet potato has tapered ends and thin, smooth skin and flesh that can range from light beige to orange-ish or even purple-ish.

Well this was outstanding. Definitely a "make this when you want to impress somebody" meal. I think this definitely makes an appetizer-sized serving, so in hindsight, I would have not cut the recipe in thirds for the two of us. I didn't have enough EVOO to cover the potatoes fully, so I bet that would make it even better. Made homemade creme fraiche using Chef John's recipe.
These potatoes are to die for. I couldn't wait for the next day, so I fried some and ate them by themselves.
Outrageously delicious, no bacon fat, reduced the oil so that potatoes were covered but not swimming, spur cream might even better.
I used left over fat/drippings from the Christmas ham -- worked really well.
So great! Used Trader Joe’s vegetarian chorizo. Baker it at 350 for 15 minutes then left it in the oven for 2 hours to see a movie and the consistency of the sweet potato was perfect.
Wow, way to take healthy ingredients and totally make them unhealthy.

I live in California. A suggestion to name the kind of chorizo. Fresh Mexican chorizo is available but I have to order Spanish chorizo online (none available locally). Having it in the ingredients list would be wonderful.

Two things: 1) Yippee, I'm making this with Mexican chorizo. 2) Commenters, please don't be snarky with me for asking. Between the picture and step 4 I figured it out but it would be nice to have it listed in the ingredients.
it says fresh chorizo in the ingredient list. spanish chorizo isn't fresh (i.e. raw). (not being snarky, simply pointing out that they did specify the variety.)
What does the peanut gallery think of using goose fat? (I have a lot left over from Christmas.)
I definitely will try. Anything by Andy Ticer and Michael Hudman will be delish. They take simple and local and make it taste extraordinary.

i would actually like to make the long version of this recipe, making my own chorizo (which i have done before and is pretty easy so i'd like to try their recipe!) and creme fraiche. would love if NYT would revisit this and share the details if Andy Ticer and Michael Hudman would be willing to volunteer them :)

title: Meatloaf With Moroccan Spices Recipe
author: Kim Severson

comments.commentBody: If I don't eat lamb  would this recipe still carry a similar punch with turkey, beef and pork mixed?
This will work with ground turkey...Which always taste best with a lot of spice.  But use an 80% lean mix.  You'll need that extra fat to keep the meatloaf moist.
My wife doesn't eat lamb either. I mix half lamb and half beef, and she can handle it.
I see no reason why not, though I'd seriously consider trying it with lamb.  I know it is not a popular meat in the US, but as a Kiwi I'd suggest trying it.
Wonder if anyone has tried this recipe with ground turkey or chicken?

I only use ground turkey to make meatloaf.  With lots of Italian spices and fresh parsley.  I use Whole Food's Italian Spice (Herb?) Mix, chopped onions, ketchup, garlic powder, an egg and organic brown rice flower as a binder.
I made this with half ground beef and half ground chicken. I used teaspoons of spices instead of tablespoons. It was delicious.

I made this recipe with ground beef and was very pleased with the taste. Next time I make this recipe I would use about three quarters of the quantity of each of the spices as the meatloaf was quite spicy (for a few in the family!). The soft texture meant the loaf came out of the cooking pan slightly broken at one end; an expanding loaf pan would avoid this. The sauce was delicious. I used walnuts + half a teaspoon of sumac. Chopped dried apricots or pomegranate would work well in the sauce.
Can this be turned into meatballs for a cocktail hour?  If so, how would you finish the cooking part?

I made leftover into meatballs and they were delicious. I seared them in a large cast iron pan and then finished them in the ovn...they were not the prettiest meatballs but they were moist and delicious. My guests said they liked the meatball version better than the meatloaf.
Did u ever try? I was wondering about that as well. Loved the meatloaf though a lot of herb chopping!

Roll into meatballs and cook at 400 degrees on greased parchment paper for 10 minutes, turn over cook another 10 minutes.  Allow to cook through in sauce (a spicy thin tomato sauce with capers is what I use, just look up a greek tomato based sauce).

The seven tablespoons of tomato paste in the recipe is about the same as a 6 oz can of tomato paste.
Delicious recipe. Worth the time and trouble.

I was very disappointed with this recipe. I made it exactly as outlined. Usually I love big flavors, and esp. the ones in this recipe. However, the spices were overpowering and the consistency was mushy (it crumbled when cutting it).  I wonder if there is a typo with spices, and it should be TSP instead of TB? There are 6 TB not even including salt, ginger and fresh spices. I used Penzeys spices so maybe they were too fresh?  Could not even tell I was eating lamb. Sauce was nice tho!
Consider toasted, unsalted sunflower seeds instead of the pine nuts. Also, toast cumin and coriander seeds in a dry skillet and grind them yourself... the flavor is enhanced.
We follow a low carb diet. Can I make this without the breadcrumbs?

I was wondering about a grain or at least gluten-free substitute. I briefly considered coconut flour (in a smaller volume than is called for of breadcrumbs) might work because it certainly soaks up liquid, but I am afraid too much coconut flavor might come through. Almond flour, perhaps?
i make meatloaf often and i usually just leave out the bread crumbs and egg and i have no problem at all
I've always used some kind of starch or grain. I'd suggest cooked rice or uncooked oatmeal if you are looking for something other than breadcrumbs. Rice would be a good gluten-free alternative.

This is a fabulous recipe, a redemption  for meatloaf malaise , and a gateway re-lapse drug for us semi vegetarians who do get a craving for meat every once in awhile, but have lost all interest in beef, no matter what the cut. Lamb is expensive; I bought 2 lbs of gound lamb from Australia, a total of about $20--but so worth it, for taste and its "satiety" factor.  Followed the spice suggestions  almost  exactly, cooked a little tester first, added another teaspoon of fine sea salt.

Served this to poker group. They all loved it.  Agree it may be too heavy on cumin & smoked paprika. I felt it a bit too moist & hard to slice because of moistness. Next time, will cook for 45 min. in water bath, then remove for last half. Taking foil off after 1 hr. is best or top never gets crusty. Also, the rec re: 140 degrees is extremely helpful; otherwise, you may think the meat is not cooked because loaf is so moist. Next time I'm going to add some cooked chard or spinach.

This is a delicious meatloaf, if a bit expensive if you go for really good lamb.  I found that after draining the fat, I wished I'd kept a little because the loaf is a tad dry.  But I did make the sauce and that helps a lot.  Very flavorful!  Thanks a lot.  I've already shared it with my neighbors.
I found this to be so heavily spiced that it ended up tasting like soap. I'll try it again, but will cut the spices in half.

Excellent. For 2 of us I used 1 lb of ground lamb and roughly 1/2 the spices  and herbs. Omitted mint. Baked in a 8" loaf pan a bit short of the 90 minutes (used a thermometer). I was able to turn it onto a serving platter and I quite loved it. The sauce was good but not necessary, as the meatloaf was very juicy. I would make it again. Served it with a roasted, spiced eggplant; recipe from La Boite site.
Are the bread crumbs fresh or dried?

continuing--I used sweet smoked paprika, not hot, which would change the flavor profile enormously.  Also, left out the cilantro and upped the fresh parsley  and mint 100 percent. Sending thanks to Anne Kornblut for a marvelous recipe that every cook can make his or her own (see comments!)

What a disappointment! Dry, crumbly -- a major failure. I painstakingly followed directions, step by step. Recipe does not work. Did the authors test it themselves? Hard to believe. I made it for a small dinner party. All guests had read the NYT article, were delighted to try something new. Nobody ate more than a polite forkful or two. Mr. Bruni, Ms. Steinhauer: test your recipes before printing!

I made this but was too afraid to put in the amount of spice indicated.  I reduced the cumin, paprika, coriander, and cinnamon by half.  The meat loaf was absolutely delicious with half the amount of spice.  I also made the side sauce using sour cream since I had no yogurt.  It was excellent with the meat loaf.  I will definitely make this again.

While I loved the spicing, the meatloaf didn't hold its shape. It was too moist. Meatloaf that doesn't hold its shape just doesn't work at all, does it? I may try it again, but take it out of the water bath for the last 1/2 hour.

The meatloaf was a big hit. I added ten minutes to the cooking time for safety, since the lamb was ground, to get the temperature up to 168.  I halved the spices, as the amount seemed excessive, and it was interesting and delicious.

I just made this most delicious meatloaf with dark turkey meat instead of lamb.   I'm sure the lamb is very good, but the turkey is excellent.   I made it just as the directions said; cooked a little tiny bit in a pan to check the seasoning, and added a little salt.  I think this will be good hot or cold....great to take to a picnic.  A definite 5 star meatloaf.

Just want to add a note re the ground turkey I used....I put tinfoil on per instructions with the dull side facing out, and took it off for the last 30 minutes.   Good consistency and not not overly soft.

I do not really care for regular meatloaf, but love Moroccan food, and  this one is wonderful!  I used 1 lbs pork, 1 lbs beef 20% fat, 1 lbs chicken (because those are the ground meats Trader Joe's sells:), freshly made bread crumbs from artisan country bread, upped spices and other ingredients accordingly and the family said it was the best meatloaf they ever had.  It's very flavorful without being spicy, a definite keeper.

I used ground turkey, adding some more olive oil to add more fat. It came out well, though I would either decrease the amount of smoky paprika or eliminating it all together next time.  Plain Greek yogurt was good, even without the lemon and pine nuts.
They don't sell pine nuts here.  Would toasted pecans work for the sauce?
I would use slivered almonds instead slightly toasted
I think almonds would be great, or walnuts crushed up into smaller pieces.

Walnuts are sometimes used as a substitute for pinenuts in Genovese pesto so I would try that (yes, I know not authentic but still tastes fine).  If you live in the States there are great sources on-line.  The best ones import from Turkey.  Avoid pinenuts from China -- some packers use a preservative that causes "pinenut mouth."

Mine is a general question for the basic veal,pork and beef meatloaf. I find it difficult to properly mix the three and butchers usually have the three separately ground. My mother always softened stale bread with milk. One egg seems to make the loaf tighter and finely chopped raw onion is fine by me.

Nancy, I often mix up meatloaf in my Kitchen Aid stand mixture. I suppose it depends on the texture you're going for, but I think it turns out fine if meats and all other ingredients are mixed in a stand mixer. Saves at lot of time and effort!
Can a regular meatloaf mixture such as ground beef and ground pork be used instead of ground lamb?
Yes you don't have to use ground lamb.  I made the recipe with ground beef (not very Moroccan) but it gave a beautiful taste.  I imagine that a half beef/half pork combination would be delicious.

Absolutely - I used beef/pork and chicken in equal quantities and it was a big hit - not crumbly or too moist as others have complained, holds its shape perfectly, really really tasty.  I used 80/20 beef because I thought the chicken would make it dry, poured out the fat once cooked and it worked out great.  Do not let the lack of lamb prevent you from trying this recipe, it's really inspired!

I made this dish last night, substituting cayenne, black pepper and nutmeg for the coriander, used 1 lb. lamb and 1 lb. beef (the lamb was quite expensive) and followed the rest of the recipe.  It came out perfectly as far as I'm concerned, not too dry, definitely spicy but isn't that the point?  Oh, and after baking for 1-1/2 hours, I took off the foil and baked for 10 more minutes which made the top a bit more crusty.
This is great meatloaf recipe,love the spices.

Just made it with ground turkey. It was quite good.
Did not include celery as I am not a fan of cooked celery.
You need to cook it to at least 150 degrees because you are using turkey. Also, since there is little fat in the meat you need to keep it covered with foil the entire time. A little extra olive oil mixed in would probably help.

I used 1 lb lamb + 1 lb ground beef.  There was a ton of grease at the end, so much so that I found the dish somewhat unappetizing.  Even after I poured it out and transferred the loaf to a plate, I couldn't get over the amount of grease that the dish had produced.  It was good, but I can't say I'll make it again.  I will, however, make the yogurt and pine nut sauce again.  Makes a great dip!

I'll bet you purchased the lamb and beef "ground".

I bought lamb stew meat and had the butcher grind it for me because there was no "pre-ground" lamb available. Voila...no grease, apparently because the the stew meat is way leaner than what the market uses to make ground lamb. Lesson learned.

BTW, the meatloaf was delicious. And yes, the sauce was wonderful...and so simple.

I was without mint and cilantro and perhaps that impacted upon the flavor, but there are so many layers of taste that this recipe could be made missing one thing or the other and that would only result in many, many excellent and slightly different dishes. BTW, the sauce is a definite requirement!

I made this recipe with ground turkey. I would not suggest swapping for turkey. It turned out overly soft and the consistency was off. The consistency felt like the meatloaf was under cooked although it certainly was not. I cannot speak for the recipe with lamb, but I am attributing the poor result from swapping the turkey for lamb. Not sure if I will repeat the effort with lamb or chalk this one up to a loss.

Made this recipe exactly as printed. It was horrible. We love Moroccan food and love spice but this did not work. The taste was comparatively bland given the amount of spices, but had a bad taste. Our ground lamb was very lean, and the texture of the meatloaf was mushy.

I made this exactly as written and it was quite good but a bit disappointing in that I found it a bit greasy.  This might be improved by serving it really hot, instead of moderately re-warmed. It’s a bit of work so I would double it and have 2 loaves for not much more trouble.

this is a very useful base recipe. I have made it several times using ground pork from my CSA half-pig. I have gone light on the bread crumbs for dietary reasons with no problems. Spices depend on your flavor philosophy.

Great recipe and a real crowd pleaser.  Next time I would double the recipe for the sauce since the recommended amount is barely adequate and is almost as delicious as the meat loaf itself.  One question:  why isn't this recipe included in Cooking's "Meat Loaf" recipes--I had to hunt for it under Frank Bruni's name.

Cook for closer to one hour
Add 1 tsp red pepper flakes

We didn't care for this.  Despite all the ingredients, the resulting meatloaf tasted bland and completely un-Moroccan.  It needed harissa, or something to give it some pzazz.  Truth to tell, we did not make the sauce, but I don't think it would have improved the dish for us.

Ground lamb was in the freezer, so made with 1 lb. each beef + pork. Was tempted to skip sautéing veggies and wish I had--they're barely discernible when mixed with ground meats, and their crunch would have been welcome. Not enough flavor from seasonings. We all love spicy food, but agree with those who found this surprisingly bland. Honestly, I prefer the classic Ann Landers recipe... as does my family.
I love this dish.  Meat loaf is so good but doesn't always come out well.  Wonderful spices, I love it with lamb and think the foil on top and water bath really helped.

I made this with ground dark meat turkey. I followed the directions, with these modifications: I used a few spoonfuls of harissa in place of some of the tomato paste, I left out the mint and cinnamon, and I sprinkled some pine nuts on the top before baking. It was delicious and moist and sliced neatly.  I added a spoonful to tahini to the sauce since I only had 2% yogurt.
I made this with a meat loaf mix, pork, beef, veal. Reduced seasonings to 1 tsp and baked without water bath. It came out moist and delicious after baking for 1 Hour.

This recipe is very tasty. Give yourself plenty of time as it takes a while to put together the meat loaf and then 90 minutes to cook it. I did not have any celery, so I skipped that and the loaf was fine without it. I grated the carrot and ginger so the pieces would not be so large in the loaf. I combined one pound of lamb with a pound of beef/pork/veal. My grocery store sells the meat combined like this for meat loaf. The side sauce is good without the pine nuts. A keeper!
This is tasty.  I used the typical meatloaf mix pork veal beef and spices were in teaspoons.  It's a unique recipe, I'll cook it again.
Absolutely delicious. We paired with a moroccan cauliflower rice dish (https://sugarfreelondoner.com/moroccan-cauliflower-rice/) that made this a perfect meal.
the best meatloaf ever! i made with beef
The amount of spices indicated are way too much.  It's a shame because the concept is great but this is not a recipe to follow as indicated.  Cut the spices down at least by 1/2.

These comments are generally so old! Anyone can make a "meat"loaf, using any kind of food incorporating most of these spices & herbs: squash with faro is nutty, flavorful, & sweet; chicken, turkey, lamb, pork, or beef or a combo of the latter two meats always work well, as does a variety of veggies; e.g., making a "meat"loaf out of ingredients you'd use for ratatouille? Sublime. As a long-time chef, don't restrict yourself to a recipe. Undo your reins & give it another name besides "meat"loaf.

I just read about "pine nut mouth" checked my refrig, and found 2 bags of pine nuts from China!  What to do about them?  Can they be rinsed of preservative?  Where can I buy a preservative free brand?

Can anyone suggest a good substitution for the tomato paste?  I used to love meatloaf but I no longer am able to eat concentrated tomato anything.  
Thanks
We get our pine nuts at Costco...big bag...freezes well.  No problems.

I read all the notes and cut back the spices a little, but after eating it with the yogurt sauce I would suggest cutting the spices in half. We like flavourful food and we eat a wide variety of foods, but this was definitely overboard. Also, I added some lemon zest, salt, cilantro, and crushed up the pine nuts in a mortar & pestle. We will definitely make the yogurt sauce again.

I made this meatloaf with ground turkey, and I used the Moroccan spice blend Ras El-hanout ( Ras Al-hanout)  which I had on hand. It includes most of the spices called for in the recipe, including cumin, coriander, ginger, and more. I added smoked paprika to the meat mix, and I sprinkled smoked paprika and black pepper on the top.  I think I will increase to 3 eggs the next time, to help it hold together a little better, but the taste was excellent.
Reduce the spices by half, add two eggs and a few tablespoons of olive oil to add moisture.  The recipe as written is dry and chalky, and spices overwhelm the lamb flavor.

I subbed 3 lb ground turkey.
Kept spice amounts the same, except left out cinnamon & coriander and sprinkled in some za'atar.
Bumped up bread crumbs slightly and added one egg.

Water bath just for the first hour, then cooked w/out water bath remaining time (took about 1 hr 45 min to get up to 165 F).

Especially good with shwarma-style garlicky yogurt sauce: 6 oz grk yogurt, splash of lemon juice, 1 garlic clove, salt to taste.

I made this exactly as proposed, except used beef, and made 3 smaller loaves - to cook more quickly. They took 50 minutes to reach 145 degrees under the aluminum foil, in the pan inside another pan with water in it. Then I browned the top under the broiler. Also added 1 minced clove garlic to the the sauce. It was fantastic.

Made this with half lamb and half ground beef (85/15) and didn't cook it in the water bath.  Took about 15 min less time to get to 140, but it was still plenty moist and delicious.  Love all of the spices.

Had 1.67 lb lamb for filling zucchini boats so scaled down a bit. Grated rather than diced the veggies to reduce bulk, replaced Tbsps of spices by tsps. No ginger or tomato paste. About 1 C herbs combined.  Added more bread than called for to soak up veg. juices and 4 eggs instead of 2 to bind everything because the cooked meat was pretty dry after draining off fat. Topped filled boats with slices of white cheddar. Very good!

I totally love this recipe, as the "meatloaf" I was looking for and for the spices bowing to Moroccan culture. Although I made attempts over a few days to get it, finally after taking all my free time rinsing and drying our herbs, mincing the copious garlic and chopping everything, I had it lined up to be ready at dinner. The result, following your specifications for ingredients and time, was stupendous. Thank you Frank and Anne for publishing this delightful digestive "meatloaf" recipe.

In our rotation is a slightly modified version of this recipe using turkey. One pound makes 5 or 6 burgers. They rest in the fridge while I make the salad, and take about 12 minutes on the stovetop. We like them with gochujang mixed with ketchup on the side. Diners decide themselves the proportion of spicy to sweet. One admonition: don’t stint on the spices.

Wow! Absolutely delucious! I made it exactly as written, except substituting 1lb of lean ground beef for 1lb of lamb (what I had to hand). The spices are right on. Yes, it is tablespoons, not teaspoons. I could see riffing on this next time by adding sliced olives and dried fruit (apricots, maybe?). This is a keeper!

title: Eastern North Carolina Fish Stew Recipe
author: Kim Severson

comments.commentBody: I grew up in Greenville, NC, which is 30 miles from Kinston. We had a very similar fish stew all the time because rockfish, the real name for "striped bass," was plentiful in local tidal waters. We made it the same way but without the tomato paste. I imagine that the addition of tomato in some form might improve it. It would certainly make it look more appetizing.
Very satisfying stew. During shad season a portion of shad roe was substituted for or added to egg. Needed to cook 10 more minutes.

Betty from VT.....I also grew up in Kinston and ate a fabulous fish stew at neighborhood cook outs, where it was cooked over a fire in a cast iron pot on a tripod ......remember it being so good. Will be trying this over a fire pit in Guilford VT this summer.  Hope it is as good as what I recall.

Many years ago now after a terrible day of fishing on the west coast of Florida someone just gave us a red drum they had caught in a nearby boat.  I found a similar recipe and made this dish and it was one of the most memorable dishes I have ever cooked.  Well worth the effort.  And a testament to a delightful region where we had docked our sailboat in the past.

I've made this two times and will make a third time later today for dinner, and each time I cut the recipe by two thirds - a pound of potatoes and a pound of fish, as well as other ingredients cut by two thirds - because I was making it for just my spouse and me.  Other than that I followed the recipe exactly.  The result could feed four.  It turned out perfect both times, and I expect to be again tonight.  Hard to believe it comes to such a savory, hearty finish with such a simple preparation.

I grew up in Kinston and remember a fish stew for a couple of years now.  I tried to make it from memory, but, it just wasn't the same.  Thanks for the recipe, off to get the ingredients; off to get them and make the stew today.
Any suggestions on making this without fishbones for those of us who prefer to not be vigilant while we eat? I was thinking thick fillets of whatever fish I can find at the market.
Use filets or if using whole fish with larger bones, like a rock fish... alot/some will just settle at the bottom of the pot.

This looks both delicious and intimidating. The amount is daunting, but I can figure that out. What I don’t like (and neither does my husband), is the fish bones. I imagine they add flavor, but wouldn’t a fish stock or broth be as effective? Or even clam juice, or seafood broth. Who needs fish ones in their soup?
I just watched Cooks Country which made this dish.  They used cod without skin or bones.

I'm going to try this, because the flavors look intriguing. But, unfortunately, I live in central Illinois, and my access to fish steaks is limited. I'll use the thickest fillets I can find, but am expecting the fish to flake rather than remain intact as in the photo. I'm come up with a new name for the result.
Any suggestions for making this without bacon?
Smoked salmon sauteed in a little olive oil is a good swap for bacon.  Use thick pacific smoked salmon, skin removed and crumbled, not lox.
Choose another recipe, Amanda.
in oil, Try frying two shallots until crisp. then add diced sun dried tomatoes and fry more. Generously add smoked paprika. Two or more table spoons of table Parmesan cheese.  You got veggie bacon

title: Bits and Pieces Party Cheese Ball Recipe
author: Kim Severson

comments.commentBody: Added some dried cranberries to the pecan and parsley mixture. Tasty and festive. Also, made mini balls with a pretzel stick in them for easy grabbing. Yum.

This was really, really good, and very well received!  I used equal parts double cream bleu cheese and extra sharp cheddar. I didn't want to buy fish sauce just to use 1/4 tsp, so I used Worcestershire and a bit of anchovy paste, which I always have on hand.  I also added a little liquid smoke and I really think the smokiness added more depth of flavor.  I couldn't find roasted pecans, so I seasoned my own with butter, salt, a little cayenne pepper, and roasted them in the oven.
I want your recipe...
Make a day in advance and refrigerate.  The flavors intensify by waiting and the result is worth the wait
To make this vegetarian, use soy sauce instead of the fish sauce.  You will add the umami and salt, maybe not the “funk” but a great sub.
I used a healthy pinch of porcini mushroom powder to the same end.
Shape into a log instead for easier serving

I've been making a version for decades after co-worker's holiday dish was a hit. Simply cream cheese, bit of Louisiana hot sauce, crumbled extra sharp cheddar (shred if u can't crumble) all warm to room temp. Mix by hand w/big 2 tine meat fork-to maintain chunks! Mix in fine diced blk olives,scallions, sweet red&green bell pepper, shape&roll in nuts/parsley. Go for 60/40 cheese/veg ratio. Veggies add HUGE flavor & texture. Vary by adding blue cheese, bacon, ham bits.
I have made this recipe several times with different cheeses but my latest combination of gruyere and gouda has been the family favorite.

I’ve made many delicious cheese balls without fish or anchovy sauce.  When serving a normally ‘vegetarian ‘ dish that has hidden fish sauce in it, please be kind enough to inform your guests of the ingredients.  It would be greatly appreciated.  Thank you.
Made this for Xmas (of course) and used chopped pistachio for the nuts. This will become a must-make for holiday...everyone liked it, even those who snarked about how they didn't like cheese balls!
I'm veggie and skipped the fish sauce... Was excellent and barely any left after our party. Will make again.
This was amazing. AMAZING. Cheeseball skeptics at my party stood corrected!
I made this recipe and didn't follow any of the instructions or use any of the recommended ingredients. (Wry humor) I actually made this exactly as written. It is delicious.
We need to stop ridiculing the cheese ball. FWIW, it has French bonafides... "Fromage Fort" is the thrifty use of bits and pieces of assorted leftover cheese.

This is the best cheese ball recipe I’ve ever made. The fish sauce is brilliant, it’s undetectable in the final form, it just makes this exceptionally delicious. And the butter makes the texture super smooth and creamy.

I made this a week in advance and it froze perfectly. Thawed in the fridge the night before than sat at room temp for a couple hours before eating. It was too lemony for a cheese ball. I think I’ll cut the lemon juice by half next time.
Absolutely delicious. Just make sure to chill it enough to make a log or a ball. If too soft, it gets really messy.

“Let the grass die. I let almost all of my indoor plants die from neglect while I was writing the book. There are all kinds of ways to live. You can take your choice. You can keep a tidy house, and when St. Peter asks you what you did with your life, you can say, 'I kept a tidy house, I made my own cheese balls.” 
— Annie Dillard
@Spider thank you for this
"... Added some dried cranberries to the pecan and parsley mixture. Tasty and festive. Also, made mini balls with a pretzel stick in them for easy grabbing. ..."

I added some Korean chili flakes to the cheese mixture and then rolled the ball in black and white sesame seeds, caraway seeds, cumin seeds, and the parsley. Amazing, but I should have toasted the seeds first. Next time, for sure.

Delightful melange of flavors. With ingredients at hand, mixture of Colby and bluecheese, feta p/o goat, worcestershire p/o fish sauce, walnuts p/o pecans, rosemary p/o parsley, unsalted butter with a touch of sea salt added to unsalted toasted walnuts. The whole cheese ball was suffused with fresh rosemary which added an unexpectedly delightful flavor. The dip was gone in a matter of minutes with everyone asking for the recipe!

This was the best cheese ball, like ever! I made it three times over the holidays and it was adored. I think the star ingredient was smoked gouda and white cheddar i used for the cup of shredded cheeses. The smoked gouda really added depth and richness. A cheeseball is the perfect excuse for richness! Also i salted and roasted the chopped pecans. Best appetizer in 2020!

QUESTION: How many days will this last in the fridge, wrapped tightly in plastic wrap? I doubled the recipe, not realizing how big the ball would be and don't know if I can freeze it or let it sit in fridge for a week before next get-together.

Good to have tried.  David Lebovitz (Chez Panisse alum of L'Appart and other cookbook fame) starts with citing the same Vivian Howard recipe as referenced for this one.  Roughly the same but without fish sauce (using 1/4 tsp salt and hot sauce instead) and adding 1/4 C chopped dates (and also suggests other dried fruits like apricots or cranberries would work) and a couple TBSP chopped scallions or chives.
David Lebovitz also makes his own roasted salted pecans for the cheese ball. Simple and delicious!
We always added a splash of brandy or wine to the cheese ball when I was growing up.
5 oz sharp cheddar, 4 oz cream cheese, 3 oz Danish blue, 4 Tbsp butter

Excellent! A huge hit with my church community. Love how flexible it is, depending on what I have on hand. Shallots for scallions, soy sauce or Worcestershire for fish sauce, walnuts or pistachios or pecans, dried cherries for cranberries, different cheese combinations have all been winners.

Easy and delicious, for the 1 cup of cheese I used up a hunk of cambazola and shredded some syrah soaked asiago. I'm looking forward to trying with some different combos. Not as dense as some cheese balls are. Good with and without the spicy plum chutney I had on the side.

Christmas Day 2024 NYTC real-time holiday reporting continues. I forgot that I had made this because I've just been making all the things. But the ball is DELICIOUS! I used Trader Joe's Unexpected Cheddar, Gruyere and Cotswold and of course the fish sauce, I'm Asian and it's listed as an ingredient 🤯
So delicious! I've made it now a few times and just adore it.
Any vegetarian substitutions for the Worcestershire or fish sauce?

There is no reason to add fish sauce to a cheese ball.  To add a bit of umami presence and a tad of savory, add finely chopped mushrooms and a bit of salt or tamari.  That said, a cheese ball with a blend of high quality, flavorful cheeses served at room temperature can stand on its own.  Serve with fresh, high quality crackers and a nice sweet pepper jelly.  Always place an ingredient list nearby if you put fish sauce in a typical food item that a vegetarian might eat.  Thank you.

Cheeseballs were quite the rage in certain circles in Columbus, Georgia during the 70's and many were recycled and went to more parties than a debutante, dressed as a pineapple or a football or a skull. You could always tell the well traveled ones by the presence of lots of cayenne to mask the funk.
Germans (Bavarians) make something similar called Obazda it is delicious, healthy and you can't recycle it.
Has anyone prepared this cheese ball and successfully frozen it? If so, any tips? I’d like to tuck a couple away for a party next week.
I make a bleu cheese ball (includes walnuts and parsley, among other things -- just to note similarities) and have frozen them successfully many times.

Yes I usually make many, but don't roll them in the parsley mix. Then wrap individually in plastic wrap and put in a zip loc or some sort of freezer container. They do great in the freezer, keep for ages.
used some feta spread (spicy) whole bag cheddar (sharp) salted butter and goat cheese. also a bit of creme fraiche, greek plain yogurt  and mayo (that is what I had around) . Quite yummy!

Made for a holiday party. Very retro and looked festive on table with crackers. 

Made pretty much as is except it subbed fish sauce for worstershire bc i ran out. It was not salty enough so either add more or keep fish sauce. 

Took others’ advice and made in advance letting flavors come together then left it out at room temp for an hour before serving. 

Would make again.

Man, this was good. Used Gruyere and sharp cheddar for my combo, Worcestershire instead of fish sauce, and chives in lieu of scallions, since that's all I had. It was devoured! Next time I want to add blue cheese.

This cheese ball looks impressive–definitely finish with pecans and parsley. I don't think the cheeses one chooses to use should differ too much from the recipe. I used a small bit of very good blue cheese and it overwhelmed I thought. Also, the spice didn't particularly match. Perhaps if there are less "stinky" cheeses in the ball the spice would match, as in the recipe. I used several soft, aromatic cheeses I had in the fridge and this gave it a pungent quality I didn't love.

In the 60’s my mother made a cheese ball with butter which I always thought curious but delicious! She used sharp cheddar, cream and blue cheeses and rolled in pecans and parsley.  Instead of fish sauce (which was not a staple in our home) she used Worcestershire sauce. She also added garlic and onion salt.  This recipe is even better with the added heat of cayenne, Gruyère and goat cheese. This brought back lots of memories of my folks elegant 60’s cocktail parties!!
Family loved this -- added chopped dried cranberries and chopped pickled jalapenos to the pecan & parsley mix.

This was definitely a winner. I didn’t have roasted pecans, so I roasted them myself with butter & cayenne. I used white cheddar with the goat & cream cheese. Peeps were fighting to take the leftovers home. Would def recommend making the day before - flavors come together beautifully. And yes, use the fish sauce.

Have made this twice for parties and it's a HUGE hit. Super easy and flexible. The first time I used hazelnuts instead of pecans, shallot instead of green onion, and added dill. I choose the spicy chili cheddar from Trader Joes and smoked aged gouda for my cheeses for a kick and depth of flavor, and I leave out the butter and opt for low-fat cream cheese. It's rich and decadent already with these short-cuts, and insanely flavorful and creamy.

4 ounces GOAT CHEESE, 3 ounces CREAM CHEESE, simply because goat cheese comes in 4 ounce packages. I’ve also used 3 wedges of Laughing Cow Light Swiss cheese in place of cream cheese, very good.  
A seeded, finely chopped jalapeño in place of the scallions, delicious if you like a little spice, probably too spicy for kids. 
Medjool pitted dates & thick savory bacon (Whole Foods) chopped fine, in place of pecans and & parsley.
Would it be weird to add a splash of brandy to the cheese ball mix? Asking for a friend…

Great recipe! Now that I've made it a few times I've started to adapt. I had smoked almonds in the cabinet which I added to the pecan crust--a nice smoky and salty addition. Also, I've realized that I don't love the filmy mouthfeel of cold butter, so I am going to reduce by half (2 Tbsp) next time. You really can use whatever you have on hand as the recipe suggests! I always make the day beforehand and I think it improves the flavor.

Love this! It's a huge hit at parties. I usually skip the butter or only add 1 tablespoon. It's very versatile - I've used different nuts and herbs for the coating based on what I've had on hand, and definitely recommend a high quality, interesting, flavorful cheese to make it pop. To be veg I add worcestershire sauce or miso, and to be spicier add some smoked chili powder and/or hot sauce.
I used Murray’s English cheddar, Vermont creamery goat cheese, and Philadelphia cream cheese. Everyone loves it!
Delish! I did sort of doubled it. 10 oz cheddar, about 8 blue cheese, 8oz cream cheese, and a stick of butter. Amazing stuff. Really like the parsley

Very good. Had my doubts when I tasted it immediately after making it, but 24 hours later it was terrific. Made as written with the exception of worcestershire in place of fish sauce. Needed a lot more pecans and parsley than called for. Next time I will divide this into two balls and freeze one, which would still be ample for eight of us. I found it a bit salty and will use unsalted butter instead and a lot more scallion. Looking forward to trying this with blue cheese.
