We usually do this once a year. We made it with Roquefort, Caprino, and Morlacco del Grappa for Boxing Day. We love that the recipe is infinitely variable according to which cheeses you use.  The only thing is that serving this as an antipasto, you seriously risk guests arriving at the table already sated.

This is delicious. I’ve come back to it so countless times and it’s a hit every time! Someone recommended dried cranberries added to the exterior here, I’ve done that as well as sour dried cherries, both wonderful additions. I’ve also started subbing pistachios (yet another commenter’s suggestion) when my daughter was diagnosed with pecan allergy. Again, wonderful! For me, I can’t imagine not having the fish sauce. Try it as written and make riffs from there!
Great recipe and was a hit. If you have none of these items in the pantry, initial cost of ingredients will run you $25.
This was really good. It tasted just like a classic Midwestern cheeseball from the state fair (iykyk) but better.

Made it to host the neighbors, as directed. None left! Success! 
Did pair it with Triscuits.
For someone with a fish allergy- I am really confused why anyone would put fish sauce in a cheese ball. Please - just don’t do it.
@Wanda for the people that don’t have a fish allergy?
@Wanda for umami flavor

I made a half recipe which was still a fine size. Used yellow cheddar cheese since that’s what we had. The scallions and goat cheese gave it a nice flavor. I would cut the cayenne in half as I’d prefer not as much spice, but that’s my preference. It did make a somewhat soft ball, but that’s made it easy to spread. And it still held its shape well. Will be making again.

This glorious gluttonous ball of cheese has gotten through countless potlucks, house parties, even picnics, as a single lady who'd rather bring a nicely paired bottle of bubbles to your party than a  mediocre plus one. Thank you, Kim Severson.
I used sharp cheddar. Everyone loved it.
Made it for a party and it was a hit! Everybody wanted the recipe. Perfect!
I live in an area without block cream cheese. Would the spreadable Philadelphia work or would I be better off using fresh white cheese (Frischkäse)?
Philly would work just fine.
Why the butter? It seems a bit unnecessary.
butter equals mouth feel
Sarah F etc Christmas party - people liked it - pretty traditional cheese ball. Would do again.
Used Kerry gold salted butter and good local cheddar. Made as directed, but doubled and formed a log, due to the way the ingredients come packaged. Very good, and only a tiny portion was leftover.
Also replaced the pecans with sesame and poppy seeds, as a friend has a nut allergy.

This was amazing. And it was gone in a flash. I feel like it’s a little more sophisticated than the cheese balls I grew up eating every Thanksgiving. I used toasted almonds instead of pecans cuz I’m not a big pecan person. Can’t wait to make it again.
What could I substitute for goat cheese?
I subbed feta for the goat cheese.
Brought this little jewel to my evening bible circle Christmas party.  I have to say I owe it all  to NYT Cooking. That little bit of cayenne and blue cheese made all the difference!
For those with tree nut allergies, replace the pecans with pepitas or sunflower seeds.

One of the biggest hits of my Christmas entertaining. So easy to make and tasty as heck. Great with cocktails! I made twice the toasted pecans to make sure the whole ball was covered. My cheese combination was a sharp 3-year-old cheddar and Gruyère with the goat cheese and cream cheese. I can see where blue cheese would be a great add to this, but because it was my first time making it, I avoided it for the non-blue-cheese lovers attending. I definitely will make this again and highly recommend.

Made for a group of French, Italian and Spanish people and it became the center of attention for the party. It looked curious and stunning for everyone to comment on, then all loved its taste. A recipe from the U.S. said I, and people were stunned, because, well, "America is so well-known for its cheese" said a French man.
I made this for my book group, and everyone loved it. I used a mixture of gruyere and sharp cheddar, and I substituted chives for scallions. It was delicious. Thank you!

I’ve made this for the holidays the past couple years. First time I followed the recipe. Amazing! 
This year I added a few of my own twists, soy sauce instead of fish sauce. HALF a de-seeded jalapeño with a minced scallion, everyone loved the little- but not overwhelming- bit of heat.  For the toasted pecans I added a Tbsp of brown sugar.  
For the cheeses I used 4 ounces goat, 3 wedges Laughing Cow light cheese, no cream cheese.  Blue cheese, Gouda, Pecorino & Sharp cheddar. Yum!
I’m curious about the above question regarding how far in advance can it be made.

A friend gave me a tip for a great vegetarian substitution for fish sauce — a product called Bragg’s Liquid Aminoes. (I admit, it has about the least-appealing name imaginable.) It’s labelled as a soy sauce substitute, but it’s more umami rich and dynamic than soy sauce.

I make this as an appetizer for Christmas dinner.  Everyone really liked it.  Did not use fish sauce. However when I followed the recipe and tasted it, I thought it was really bland.  In increased the amount of cheddar cheese by and additional 1/2 to 3/4 cup but did not increase the cream cheese or goat cheese.  Served with crackers and sliced pears.
A WINNER!  Guests were RAVING.  A retro delight.

Made for the holidays and it was the first thing gone on the buffet table!   I used smoked Gouda and Dubliner cheeses.   No fish sauce at my store, so I used some Veggie Umami sauce instead.  It was great, in fact I might use more than a quarter teaspoon next time.   Making this again for New Year’s Eve party.

Added some horseradish sauce. 
Used ~3T goat cheese frommage blanc instead of goat cheese.

Pretty addictive. 
Added some horseradish sauce for flavor. 
Substituted goat cheese frommage blanc for goat cheese.
Used 5 oz sharp cheddar, 4 oz cream cheese, 3 oz Danish blue cheese, 4 Tbsp unsalted butter, 1/4 t sea salt
Can I use a food processor rather than a stand mixer?
I used a bowl and a fork- still turned out great!

Last time I made this, I used a food processor - forgot that the instructions called for using the mixer.  No problem with the final product - really the only thing is that scraping the soft cheese off of the food processor blades was a bit more hazardous than the mixer paddle.

We had Red pepper jelly on hand which made for a crab Rangoon like experience when combined on a cracker. 

10/10
Dazzling. This was a hit at my holiday party. Fish sauce was awesome and the chef would let their vegan diners know you tools.
Made with gruyere and gouda, no fish sauce but added dried cranberries and jalapeno, then let it sit overnight.  This is so easy and delicious!
An easy but impressive looking appetizer. I used sharp cheddar, goat cheese, and blue cheese because I wanted a cheese ball with some kick.
This was delicious. My old standby is easier—roasted walnuts or pecans, garlic, cream cheese, and blue cheese.
Has anyone used some miso instead of fish sauce?

This was last minute, so I served a deconstructed cheese ball. Spread it on a salad-sized plate, topped liberally with hand-broken walnuts that were toasted with olive oil and coarse salt. Dusted with some Penzey’s Outrage seasoning. So appropriate for our times.
This was a big hit! I used blue cheese, goat cheese, sharp cheddar, and some odds and ends from a cheese plate. The fish sauce adds the perfect saltiness. Great flavor and no leftovers!

I did not use the fish sauce, used coconut aminos instead. After putting it in the refrigerator we tasted it and the shallots were overpowering so we added a little more lemon juice. It was a hit at the party we took it to!
I’m planning on making this tomorrow if not today! If I get busy tomorrow, I’ll shoot for Thursday.
I made this as written for a potluck - the fish sauce is the perfect umami note. So good, everyone loved it!

Second time I made this and probably the last time.  It is crumbly. I had forgotten how crumbly it was the first time. It’s tasty, but almost embarrassing to watch people try to spread it at a potluck.
